# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete TEM Optical Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EG[Electron Source - FEG or LaB6] --> AC1[Accelerating Anode 60 to 1000 kV]
    AC1 --> SC[Spot Size Condenser Aperture 5 to 200 um]
    SC --> C1[First Condenser Lens C1 - Demagnifies Source]
    C1 --> CA[Condenser Aperture 50 to 200 um]
    CA --> C2[Second Condenser Lens C2 - Sets Beam Convergence]
    C2 --> SCAN[Pre-specimen Scan Coils in STEM mode only]
    SCAN --> SPEC[Ultrathin Specimen less than 100 nm]
    SPEC --> OL[Objective Lens OL - Most Critical Lens]
    OL --> OA[Objective Aperture - Selects BF or DF beam]
    OA --> DIFF[Selected Area Diffraction Aperture SAD]
    DIFF --> IL1[Intermediate Lens 1 - Switches between Image and Diffraction Mode]
    IL1 --> IL2[Intermediate Lens 2]
    IL2 --> IL3[Intermediate Lens 3]
    IL3 --> PL[Projector Lens PL]
    PL --> SCREEN[Phosphor Screen or CCD CMOS Camera]
    SCREEN --> EELS[Post-column EELS Spectrometer - Optional]
    SCREEN --> EDS[EDX Detector - Optional]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reader's Note:** This block diagram traces the path of electrons from the gun to the detector. The **Objective Lens** is the most critical element — its aberrations dominate the final image quality. The **Intermediate Lens** is the "mode switch" — selecting the focal plane of the objective lens (image plane = real-space image; back-focal plane = diffraction pattern).

## 4.2 Block Architecture — SEM Signal Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EGS[Electron Gun - Tungsten or FEG] --> ACC[Accelerating Column 0.5 to 30 kV]
    ACC --> CL[Condenser Lenses]
    CL --> FA[Final Aperture 10 to 100 um]
    FA --> SCANCOIL[Scan Coils - Raster Generator]
    SCANCOIL --> SPEC[Specimen Stage - Bulk or Thin]
    SPEC --> SEDET[Secondary Electron Detector - Everhart-Thornley]
    SPEC --> BSEDET[Backscattered Electron Detector - Solid State]
    SPEC --> EDSX[EDX Detector - Lithium-drifted Silicon]
    SPEC --> STEM[TLD or STEM Detector - Optional]
    SEDET --> SEAMP[SE Amplifier]
    BSEDET --> BSEAMP[BSE Amplifier]
    EDSX --> EDSMCA[EDX Multichannel Analyzer]
    STEM --> STEMAMP[STEM Amplifier]
    SEAMP --> MON[Display Monitor - Image Reconstruction]
    BSEAMP --> MON
    EDSMCA --> MON
    STEMAMP --> MON
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Context:** Unlike TEM, SEM uses a **raster-scanning** strategy. The scan coils deflect the beam in a TV-like pattern; the detected signal intensity at each (x, y) pixel is mapped to screen brightness. The **dwell time per pixel** controls the signal-to-noise ratio and is typically 0.1 – 100 µs.

## 4.3 TEM Imaging Mode Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to image specimen] --> Q1{Aperture selection question}
    Q1 --> BF[Direct beam only - Bright Field BF]
    Q1 --> DF[One diffracted beam g only - Dark Field DF]
    Q1 --> HRTEM[Multiple beams through large aperture - HRTEM]
    Q1 --> SAED[No image aperture - SAED diffraction pattern]
    Q1 --> STEM[STEM mode - Scanning HAADF BF ABF]
    BF --> USES[Uses: morphology, mass-thickness, defects]
    DF --> USES2[Uses: specific grains, strain, precipitates]
    HRTEM --> USES3[Uses: atomic structure, interfaces, dislocations]
    SAED --> USES4[Uses: crystal structure, orientation, phase ID]
    STEM --> USES5[Uses: Z-contrast atomic columns, EELS mapping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Selection Guide:** Choose **BF** for general morphology; **DF** to highlight a single diffracting variant; **HRTEM** to "see atoms" (requires thin, low-defect, zone-axis aligned specimen); **SAED** to identify phase; **STEM-HAADF** for atomic-number contrast (especially with heavy dopants in light matrix).

## 4.4 EELS Spectrum Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ZLP[Zero-Loss Peak ZLP at 0 eV] --> LL[Low-Loss Region 0 to 50 eV]
    LL --> PL[Plasmon Peak 15 to 35 eV typical]
    LL --> IB[Interband Transitions and Bandgap]
    PL --> CORE[Core-Loss Region above 50 eV]
    IB --> CORE
    CORE --> KEDGE[K-edges for light elements C N O]
    CORE --> LEDGE[L-edges for 3d transition metals Ti Fe Ni]
    CORE --> MEDGE[M-edges for lanthanides and actinides]
    CORE --> EXELFS[Extended Energy-Loss Fine Structure EXELFS 50 to 300 eV past edge]
    KEDGE --> ELNES[Energy-Loss Near-Edge Structure ELNES within 30 eV of edge]
    LEDGE --> ELNES
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The ZLP carries the elastic and zero-loss information (used for thickness measurement). The plasmon peak carries **dielectric function** and **thickness** information. Core-loss edges carry **element-specific** information. ELNES and EXELFS together give local atomic environment, bonding, and coordination.

## 4.5 RBS Spectral Block Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SPEC[Specimen with layers] --> SURF[Surface Peak at Energy K times E0]
    SURF --> L1[Layer 1 Signal at K1 times E0 minus energy loss in L1]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 Signal at K2 times E0 minus energy loss in L1 and L2]
    L2 --> SUB[Substrate Signal at K_sub times E0 minus energy loss in all layers]
    SURF --> MASSID[K value yields element mass]
    L1 --> DEPTH[Energy shift from surface yields depth]
    L1 --> COMP[Height and width yield concentration and thickness]
    SUB --> INTER[Interface mixing yields profile broadening]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading Rule:** In an RBS spectrum (Energy on x-axis, Yield on y-axis):
> - **Position (Energy axis)** → mass of backscattering element + depth.
> - **Height (Yield axis)** → concentration of that element at that depth.
> - **Width** → thickness of the layer containing that element.
> - **Slope of trailing edge** → concentration gradient at interfaces.

## 4.6 Electron-Specimen Interaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PE[Primary Electron Beam 0.5 to 30 keV] --> IV[Interaction Volume - Teardrop shaped, 0.1 to 5 um deep]
    IV --> SE[Secondary Electrons - escape from top 1 to 10 nm]
    IV --> BSE[Backscattered Electrons - escape from 10 to 100 nm]
    IV --> XRAY[Characteristic X-rays - emitted from full IV]
    IV --> AUG[Auger Electrons - escape from top 5 nm]
    IV --> CL[Cathodoluminescence - light from bandgap materials]
    IV --> ABS[Absorbed Current - remainder of beam]
    SE --> SEM_TOPO[Topographic Image - SE detector]
    BSE --> SEM_Z[Z-contrast Image - BSE detector]
    XRAY --> EDS[Elemental Map - EDX detector]
    AUG --> AES[Surface Chemistry - Auger Electron Spectroscopy]
    CL --> CLMAP[Optical Property Map - CL detector]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The **interaction volume** (not the beam spot size) limits the spatial resolution of bulk SEM imaging. At 30 kV on silicon, the interaction volume is ~1 µm in size — so even with a 1 nm probe, the resolution of BSE or EDX maps is only ~1 µm because the signals originate from this entire volume.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]
**Define elastic and inelastic scattering of electrons in the context of TEM. How do these mechanisms contribute to image contrast in BF and DF imaging modes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Board expected length: ~10–12 lines):**

**Elastic scattering** is the deflection of incident electrons by the Coulomb field of atomic nuclei **without significant energy transfer** (energy loss ≪ 1 eV). The electron's kinetic energy is conserved; only its direction changes. This is the **Bragg-type scattering** that produces electron diffraction patterns and is the basis for **diffraction contrast**.

**Inelastic scattering** involves energy transfer (typically 1 – 1000 eV) from the incident electron to the specimen's electronic, vibrational, or plasmon excitations. Energy is lost by the electron; in return, secondary electrons, plasmons, phonons, or inner-shell ionizations are generated.

**Contrast origin in imaging modes:**
- In **Bright Field (BF)**, the objective aperture selects the **direct (transmitted) beam** only. Image contrast arises because regions that diffract strongly (e.g., a bent crystal) deflect electrons *out* of the direct beam, appearing **dark** in BF.
- In **Dark Field (DF)**, the objective aperture selects **one specific diffracted beam $g$**. Only the regions satisfying the Bragg condition for that $g$-vector appear **bright** in DF, while the matrix appears dark.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:** Many students write that BF contrast comes from "inelastic scattering" — this is **incorrect**. BF and DF contrasts are fundamentally **diffraction contrast** based on **elastic** Bragg scattering. Inelastic scattering contributes to EELS, EDS, and SE/BSE signals in SEM, NOT to BF/DF TEM contrast. Lose 1 mark if confused.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of the kinematic factor $K$ in Rutherford Backscattering Spectrometry. Why is RBS insensitive to hydrogen when using He⁺ projectiles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **kinematic factor $K = E_1 / E_0$** is the ratio of the energy of an ion after elastic backscattering to its incident energy. It is determined purely by the conservation of energy and momentum in a two-body elastic collision:

$$
K = \left[\frac{\sqrt{M_2^2 - M_1^2 \sin^2\theta} + M_1 \cos\theta}{M_1 + M_2}\right]^2
$$

where $M_1$ is the projectile mass, $M_2$ is the target atom mass, and $\theta$ is the backscattering angle. The kinematic factor allows **identification of the target mass** $M_2$ from the measured backscattered energy $E_1$.

**RBS insensitivity to hydrogen with He⁺:** From the kinematic factor expression, when $M_1 > M_2$, the term under the square root ($M_2^2 - M_1^2 \sin^2\theta$) becomes **negative** for many scattering angles, making $K$ undefined (no physical solution). Physically, a He⁺ ion **cannot backscatter** from a lighter target — it transfers all its energy to the lighter atom and continues forward. Therefore He⁺ RBS is **blind to hydrogen**. Specialized RBS variants use heavier projectiles (e.g., $\text{C}^+$, $\text{N}^+$, or recoil detection) to detect hydrogen.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:** Some students write "RBS cannot detect H because the cross-section is too small." The **true** reason is **kinematic** (momentum conservation), not cross-section. Lose 1 mark if cross-section is the only reason given.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, ESE Module 2]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the construction and working of a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) with a labeled block diagram. Discuss the function of the objective lens and the role of the intermediate lens in mode switching.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the principle and instrumentation of Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS). With a neat sketch of a typical EELS spectrum, label and describe the zero-loss peak, low-loss region (plasmons), and core-loss edges. State two engineering applications of EELS.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) [7 marks]:

**1. Construction of TEM (3 marks):**

A TEM consists of the following subsystems (described top-to-bottom along the column, maintained at ~$10^{-7}$ Pa vacuum):

(i) **Electron Source (Gun):** Tungsten hairpin, LaB₆ thermionic emitter, or Schottky Field Emission Gun (FEG). The FEG provides the highest brightness and coherence, essential for HRTEM and EELS.

(ii) **Acceleration stage:** 60 – 1000 kV accelerating voltage applied between cathode and anode.

(iii) **Illumination system:** Two condenser lenses (C1, C2) with associated apertures demagnify the source and control beam convergence onto the specimen.

(iv) **Specimen stage:** Double-tilt/lift holder for thin foils (< 100 nm); airlock for sample insertion.

(v) **Imaging system:** Objective lens (the most critical lens), objective aperture, selected-area aperture, 3–4 intermediate lenses, and 1–2 projector lenses.

(vi) **Detection system:** Phosphor screen + CCD/CMOS camera, sometimes with an integrated EELS spectrometer or EDX detector.

**2. Function of Objective Lens (2 marks):**

The objective lens is the **first image-forming lens** after the specimen. It accepts the electrons transmitted/diffracted by the specimen and forms:
- A **real-space image** in its image plane (used for imaging mode).
- A **reciprocal-space diffraction pattern** in its back-focal plane (used for SAED mode).

Its aberrations — primarily **spherical aberration ($C_s$)** and **chromatic aberration ($C_c$)** — dominate the final resolution. The Scherzer focus condition:

$$
\Delta f_{\text{Scherzer}} = -1.2 \left(C_s \lambda\right)^{1/2}
$$

gives the defocus that maximizes interpretable resolution by balancing defocus and spherical aberration.

**3. Role of Intermediate Lens in Mode Switching (2 marks):**

The intermediate lens system is the "mode selector." By adjusting its excitation current, it can project either:
- The **image plane of the objective lens** (producing a real-space, magnified image — used in BF, DF, HRTEM), OR
- The **back-focal plane of the objective lens** (producing a diffraction pattern — used in SAED).

This dual capability allows a single instrument to switch between imaging and diffraction without moving any physical component — a defining feature of TEM.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) [7 marks]:

**1. Principle of EELS (2 marks):**

When a high-energy electron traverses a thin specimen, it may undergo **inelastic scattering**, losing a discrete amount of energy $\Delta E$ to inner-shell ionization, plasmon excitation, interband transitions, or phonons. A magnetic-prism spectrometer placed below the imaging column disperses these electrons by energy, producing a spectrum where counts are plotted against energy loss. The ZLP sits at $\Delta E = 0$; peaks and edges appear at higher energy losses corresponding to specific excitations in the specimen.

**2. Instrumentation (2 marks):**

A post-column EELS spectrometer (Gatan GIF / Enfinium type) consists of:
- A magnetic sector prism (90° or more) that deflects electrons by an angle dependent on their energy.
- Focusing multipoles that project the dispersed electrons onto a scintillator-coupled CCD.
- A drift tube for high-energy resolution (≤ 0.7 eV in modern systems).

**3. Spectral Anatomy (2 marks):**

A typical EELS spectrum is sketched below (in words; the student should draw a graph with Energy Loss on x-axis from 0 to 1000 eV and intensity on y-axis):

- **Zero-Loss Peak (ZLP):** Sharp peak at $\Delta E = 0$ from elastically and quasi-elastically scattered electrons. Used for energy calibration, energy resolution determination, and thickness measurement ($t/\lambda$).
- **Low-Loss Region (0 – 50 eV):** Contains the dominant **plasmon peak** (typically at 15 – 35 eV for most solids), arising from collective oscillation of valence electrons. Also contains interband transitions and the bandgap onset. Provides the dielectric function $\varepsilon(E)$ and bandgap.
- **Core-Loss Region (> 50 eV):** Contains **ionization edges** for each element present. The edge threshold identifies the element; the fine structure (ELNES, within ~30 eV of edge; EXELFS, beyond) reveals bonding, oxidation state, and local coordination.

**4. Engineering Applications of EELS (1 mark):**

(i) **Quantitative elemental mapping** at sub-nm resolution in semiconductor devices (e.g., dopant profiling in transistor gates).
(ii) **Bonding and oxidation state analysis** in catalysts, ceramics, and battery electrode materials.
(iii) **Thickness mapping** of TEM foils in real time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning #1:** Students often confuse **EELS** with **EDX/EDS**. EELS measures energy loss of *transmitted electrons*; EDS measures energy of *emitted X-rays*. EELS is superior for **light elements (Li, Be, B, C, N, O)** because the ionization cross-section is high and the edges are sharp; EDS is preferred for **heavier elements** (Z > 11) with high-energy X-ray lines. Mixing the two in a single answer costs 1 mark.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to state the units of energy loss (eV) or the typical energy range of each region. Always specify these numerically.

---

### Question B: [Alternative 14-Mark Choice for ESE]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the principle, construction, and working of a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). Compare the information obtained from Secondary Electron (SE) and Backscattered Electron (BSE) imaging modes with respect to contrast origin, escape depth, and resolution.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the principle of Rutherford Backscattering Spectrometry (RBS). Derive the kinematic factor for elastic backscattering, and explain how RBS spectra are interpreted in terms of (i) mass identification, (ii) depth profiling, and (iii) film thickness measurement.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) [7 marks]:

**1. Principle of SEM (2 marks):**

A SEM produces images by raster-scanning a finely focused electron probe (1 – 100 nm diameter) across a specimen surface in a TV-like pattern. At each pixel, the beam-specimen interaction generates a cocktail of signals — SE, BSE, X-rays, Auger electrons, and cathodoluminescence. Detectors collect one or more of these signals, and the signal intensity modulates the brightness of the corresponding pixel on a synchronized display. A complete image is built up scan-by-scan.

**2. Construction (3 marks):**

- **Electron Column (top to bottom):** Electron gun (W hairpin, LaB₆, or FEG) → accelerating column (0.5 – 30 kV) → 2–3 condenser lenses → objective lens with internal scan coils and final aperture.
- **Specimen Chamber:** Multi-axis stage (X, Y, Z, tilt, rotation) at the base of the column; typically accommodates bulk samples (up to several cm in size) unlike TEM's thin foils.
- **Detectors:** Everhart-Thornley SE detector (positively biased collector + scintillator + photomultiplier), solid-state BSE detector (annular silicon diodes), EDX detector (Si(Li) or SDD), and optional CL mirror.
- **Vacuum System:** ~$10^{-3}$ – $10^{-5}$ Pa maintained by turbomolecular + ion pumps.
- **Display and Control Electronics:** Scan generator, amplifiers, image processor.

**3. Working (1 mark):**

The scan generator drives the scan coils in a raster pattern synchronized with the display. At each pixel, dwell time (0.1 – 100 µs) determines signal-to-noise. Magnification is controlled by the ratio of display raster size to scanned area on the specimen — increasing scan coil current decreases scanned area, increasing magnification (e.g., scanning a 10 µm × 10 µm area displayed on 100 mm × 100 mm = 10,000× magnification).

**4. Comparison: SE vs BSE (1 mark — tabulated for clarity):**

| Property | SE Imaging | BSE Imaging |
|---|---|---|
| Energy of signal electrons | < 50 eV | > 50 eV (up to $E_0$) |
| Escape depth | 1 – 10 nm | 10 – 100 nm |
| Contrast origin | Topography (edge effect) | Atomic number Z (mass contrast) |
| Resolution | ~1 nm (limited by escape depth) | ~10 – 100 nm (limited by interaction volume) |
| Application | Fracture surfaces, biological tissue, grain morphology | Phase identification, heavy-atom locations |

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) [7 marks]:

**1. Principle of RBS (2 marks):**

A beam of monoenergetic, positively charged ions (typically $\text{He}^{++}$ at 1 – 3 MeV) from a Van de Graaff or tandem accelerator is directed at a target in a high-vacuum chamber. The ions undergo **Rutherford (Coulomb) elastic backscattering** from the nuclei of atoms in the target. A surface barrier detector placed at a defined backscattering angle (commonly $\theta = 170°$) measures the energy of each backscattered ion. The energy spectrum is recorded and analyzed to extract information about the target's composition, depth profile, and film thickness.

**2. Derivation of Kinematic Factor (2 marks):**

Apply conservation of kinetic energy and momentum to an elastic collision between projectile $M_1$ (incident energy $E_0$, velocity $v_0$) and target $M_2$ (initially at rest). The projectile backscatters with energy $E_1$ at angle $\theta$. After algebraic manipulation:

- Conservation of kinetic energy: $E_0 = E_1 + E_2$
- Conservation of momentum (parallel): $M_1 v_0 = M_1 v_1 \cos\theta + M_2 v_2 \cos\phi$
- Conservation of momentum (perpendicular): $0 = M_1 v_1 \sin\theta - M_2 v_2 \sin\phi$

Solving these three equations for the ratio $K = E_1 / E_0$:

$$
K = \left[\frac{\sqrt{M_2^2 - M_1^2 \sin^2\theta} + M_1 \cos\theta}{M_1 + M_2}\right]^2
$$

**3. Spectral Interpretation (3 marks):**

(i) **Mass identification:** The kinematic factor $K$ depends on $M_2$. Since $K = E_1 / E_0$ and $E_0$ is known, measuring $E_1$ directly yields $K$, from which $M_2$ (the target atom mass) is calculated. Different elements in the sample produce backscatter peaks at different $E_1$ values, allowing mass-resolved identification.

(ii) **Depth profiling:** An ion that backscatters from a depth $t$ below the surface loses energy in two legs: (a) on the way in, due to inelastic stopping (electronic excitation), and (b) on the way out, after backscattering. The total energy loss $\Delta E$ is proportional to depth (via the stopping power $[S]$). Therefore the energy shift from the surface peak position is converted to a depth scale:

$$
t = \frac{\Delta E}{[S]_{\text{mean}}}
$$

This enables non-destructive depth profiling to depths of ~0.1 – 2 µm depending on material and ion energy.

(iii) **Film thickness measurement:** The width of a thin-film peak in the RBS spectrum (in energy units) is directly proportional to the film thickness. After converting energy width to depth width via the stopping power, the thickness is obtained. For example, a 100 nm SiO₂ film on Si gives a peak of width $\Delta E \approx 30$ keV for 2 MeV He⁺.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning #1:** Students often confuse the **energy loss mechanism in RBS** with that in TEM. In RBS, energy loss is dominated by **electronic stopping** (inelastic collisions with bound electrons) along the ion path. This is fundamentally different from TEM's elastic Bragg diffraction, although both involve "energy loss" terminology. Lose 0.5 mark if confused.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting that RBS is **non-destructive and quantitative** (no standards needed) — this is a major advantage over EDX. State this explicitly for full marks.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** Students often say "RBS measures thickness" without specifying the **energy-to-depth conversion** via stopping power. Always state the conversion explicitly.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- ☐ **Electron wavelength at 200 kV** = **2.51 pm**; at 300 kV = **1.97 pm**; at 100 kV = **3.70 pm**.
- ☐ **Resolution** is limited by both **wavelength $\lambda$** and **lens aberrations** ($C_s$, $C_c$); aberration-corrected microscopes push resolution below 0.5 Å.
- ☐ **Rayleigh criterion:** $d = 0.61 \lambda / \sin\alpha$.
- ☐ **BF TEM** uses the **direct (transmitted) beam**; **DF TEM** uses **one diffracted beam** $g$; **HRTEM** uses **multiple beams** for phase contrast.
- ☐ **STEM-HAADF** gives **Z-contrast** $\propto Z^{1.7-2}$ — best for atomic-number imaging of heavy elements in light matrices.
- ☐ **EELS ZLP** → energy resolution + thickness; **plasmon peak** → dielectric function; **core-loss edges** → elemental ID + bonding.
- ☐ **EELS thickness formula:** $t = \lambda_{\text{mp}} \ln(I_t / I_0)$.
- ☐ **SEM SE imaging** → topography, ~1 nm resolution, escape depth 1–10 nm.
- ☐ **SEM BSE imaging** → Z-contrast, escape depth 10–100 nm, resolution limited by interaction volume.
- ☐ **RBS kinematic factor:** $K = E_1 / E_0$ depends on $M_1, M_2, \theta$ — used to identify element mass.
- ☐ **RBS depth profiling** via stopping power $[S]$ — non-destructive, absolute (no standards), blind to H with He⁺.
- ☐ **Stopping power** in RBS = electronic energy loss along the ion path; converted to depth scale.
- ☐ **Relativistic correction** for $\lambda$ matters above ~100 kV; full formula in derivation 3.1.

### Common KTU Pitfalls
- ☐ Don't say "BF contrast comes from inelastic scattering" — it's **diffraction contrast** (elastic).
- ☐ Don't say "RBS can't detect H because of small cross-section" — it's **kinematic** ($M_1 > M_2$).
- ☐ Don't confuse EELS (measures electron energy loss) with EDS (measures X-ray energy).
- ☐ Don't omit **units** in formulas — KTU examiners deduct for "naked numbers."
- ☐ Always state **accelerating voltage** when discussing resolution in TEM.
- ☐ Remember: RBS gives **depth** info; SEM gives **surface** info; TEM gives **internal** info; EELS gives **chemical state** info.

### Mnemonics to Remember
- ☐ **"BFin = BEAM in, BLACK in BF"** — in BF, regions that diffract out appear dark.
- ☐ **"SE for Surface, BSE for Bulk"** — SE sees only top few nm; BSE sees deeper.
- ☐ **"ZLP first, Plasmons second, Core-loss last"** — EELS spectrum order from left to right.
- ☐ **"K is the KEY"** in RBS — kinematic factor unlocks mass identification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Day Strategy (KTU):** In a 14-mark question, allocate approximately 7–8 minutes per sub-part. Always draw a labeled diagram (≥ 1 mark reserved for it). Always state **units** and **numerical values** where possible — they transform a generic answer into a KTU-grade answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)** is a thermoanalytical technique in which the **temperature difference ($\Delta T$)** between a test sample and an **inert reference material** is continuously recorded as a function of furnace temperature (or time), while both are subjected to an identical controlled heating (or cooling) program in a uniform thermal environment.

Mathematically, the measured signal is:

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{sample}} - T_{\text{reference}}
$$

where $T_{\text{sample}}$ and $T_{\text{reference}}$ are the instantaneous temperatures of the sample and the reference, respectively, under isothermal or non-isothermal conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping)**
> DTA belongs to the broader class of **thermal analysis** methods and is mapped to **CO3** of PEPET412 — *"Apply thermal, microscopic, and spectroscopic characterization techniques to interpret material behaviour."* Students must be able to draw the instrumentation block diagram, label a typical DTA thermogram, distinguish DTA from DSC, and list the factors affecting a DTA curve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Twin Marathon Runners" Intuition

Imagine **two identical twins** starting a marathon together under the same weather conditions (identical thermal program). One runner carries a **water bottle that periodically refills with ice or hot soup** (the sample undergoing physical/chemical changes like melting, oxidation, or decomposition). The other twin is a **"reference" runner with a sealed insulated bottle** (inert reference material, typically calcined $\alpha$-$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ or empty pan, with no phase change).

A camera continuously films the **distance gap between the two twins** as they run (this gap is the analog of $\Delta T$). When the first twin stops to drink cold water (endothermic melting), he falls behind — the gap widens **negatively**. When hot soup gives him a burst of energy (exothermic crystallization/oxidation), he surges ahead — the gap widens **positively**. The DTA thermogram is precisely this "gap-versus-time/track-position" plot, but translated to *temperature difference* vs *furnace temperature*.

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Take-away**
> - $\Delta T \approx 0$ → both are behaving identically (no transformation).
> - $\Delta T < 0$ → sample lags (endothermic process — absorbing heat).
> - $\Delta T > 0$ → sample leads (exothermic process — releasing heat).
> - A **peak** in the $\Delta T$ curve = a thermal event (transition, reaction, decomposition).

## 1.3 Standard Reference Materials and Constants

- **Reference material:** must be **thermally inert** over the experimental temperature range, with no phase transitions. Standard choices: **$\alpha$-$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (calcined alumina)**, **MgO**, or simply an **empty crucible** in modern heat-flux designs.
- **Standard heating rates ($\beta$):** typically $\mathbf{1 \; \text{K/min}}$ to $\mathbf{20 \; \text{K/min}}$ (most common: $10 \; \text{K/min}$).
- **Atmospheres used:** static air, dynamic $\text{N}_2$, $\text{Ar}$, $\text{He}$, or vacuum.
- **Thermocouples:** **Type K** (Chromel–Alumel, up to ~$1370 \; \text{K}$), **Type R/S** (Pt/Pt-Rh, up to ~$1900 \; \text{K}$), or **Type N** (Nicrosil–Nisil, up to ~$1570 \; \text{K}$).

> [!TIP] **Examiner Tip:** Always write the *full* thermocouple composition and temperature limit when you name a thermocouple in the exam. A bare "Type K" answer loses marks; "Chromel–Alumel, range 0–1370 K" is the KTU board's gold standard.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

When a sample undergoes a **physical transformation** (melting, boiling, polymorphic transition, sublimation) or a **chemical reaction** (dehydration, oxidation, reduction, decomposition), it either **absorbs** (endothermic, $\Delta H > 0$) or **releases** (exothermic, $\Delta H < 0$) thermal energy. This enthalpy change momentarily unbalances the otherwise identical heat flow into the sample and reference holders, creating a measurable **temperature differential** $\Delta T$ between them. DTA exploits this differential to **detect**, **locate** (temperature), and — with calibration — **quantify** (enthalpy) thermal events.

## 2.2 Instrumentation — Functional Subsystems

A DTA apparatus is composed of six functionally distinct blocks:

1. **Furnace** (programmable resistance or induction heater) — provides a linear or modulated temperature ramp.
2. **Sample holder assembly** — symmetric twin-crucible block, often made of platinum, alumina, or nickel, holding sample and reference in thermal symmetry.
3. **Temperature sensors** — two matched thermocouples, one embedded in (or in contact with) the sample, one with the reference.
4. **$\Delta T$ amplifier** — high-gain DC differential amplifier to boost the small millivolt-level thermocouple difference.
5. **Programmer / controller** — closed-loop PID controller driving the furnace at a preset $\beta$ (e.g., $10 \; \text{K/min}$).
6. **Recorder / Data Acquisition System (DAS)** — plotter or computer-based ADC logging $\Delta T$ and $T_{\text{reference}}$ simultaneously.

## 2.3 The DTA Thermogram — How to Read It

A **typical DTA curve** plots **$\Delta T$ on the Y-axis** against **furnace temperature $T$ (or time $t$) on the X-axis**. Convention varies (IUPAC recommends *endothermic = down* and *exothermic = up* when $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$, but many texts use the opposite). Always check the convention on the y-axis label.

| Feature on Curve | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Flat baseline** ($\Delta T \approx 0$) | No thermal event; sample and reference track each other. |
| **Sharp downward peak** | Endothermic event (e.g., melting, dehydration, decomposition to volatile products). |
| **Sharp upward peak** | Exothermic event (e.g., crystallization, oxidation, combustion). |
| **Onset temperature $T_{\text{onset}}$** | Extrapolated start of the peak from the baseline — defines the *true* transformation temperature. |
| **Peak temperature $T_{\text{peak}}$** | Maximum/minimum $\Delta T$ point; depends on heating rate. |
| **Peak area $A$** | Proportional to the **enthalpy change** $\Delta H$ of the event (basis of quantitative DTA). |

## 2.4 Theoretical Heat-Flow Model (Qualitative)

For the sample and reference held in identical holders with thermal resistance $R$ to the furnace at temperature $T_f$:

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} + \dfrac{T_s - T_f}{R} = \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

$$
C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} + \dfrac{T_r - T_f}{R} = 0
$$

where $C_s$ and $C_r$ are heat capacities, and $df/dt$ is the rate of reaction progress. Subtracting yields the governing DTA equation:

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} - C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} + \dfrac{\Delta T}{R} = \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

If the sample and reference have similar $C$ values, the heat-capacity term is small, and we recover the central result that **$\Delta T$ is proportional to the instantaneous heat generation rate** in the sample.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning of Each Symbol | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$ | Differential temperature measured | Primary DTA signal |
| 2 | $\Delta H = K \cdot \dfrac{A}{m}$ | **Speil's equation** — peak area $A$, sample mass $m$, calibration constant $K$ | Quantifies enthalpy from a single peak |
| 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Heating rate (typically $1$–$20 \; \text{K/min}$) | Controls peak sharpness and resolution |
| 4 | $T_{\text{onset}} \approx T_{\text{equilibrium}}$ for $\beta \to 0$ | Extrapolated onset temperature | Best estimate of true transition temperature |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{\max} \propto \beta$ | Peak height rises linearly with $\beta$ | Diagnostic for distinguishing DTA from DSC |
| 6 | $A_{\text{peak}} \propto \Delta H \cdot m$ | Area scales with mass and enthalpy | Basis of quantitative analysis |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{peak}}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{onset}}} + \dfrac{2.5 \, R \, T_{\text{peak}} \cdot \ln(\beta)}{\Delta H}$ | Kissinger-type relation for reaction kinetics | Activation-energy determination |
| 8 | $\text{Sensitivity} \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$ (holder resistance) | Lower holder resistance → higher peak height | Guides crucible material choice |
| 9 | $T \text{ (in K)} = T \text{ (in } ^\circ \text{C)} + 273.15$ | Absolute-to-Celsius conversion | Required whenever the IUPAC thermodynamic scale is invoked |
| 10 | $\text{Noise floor} \approx 0.01 \; \text{K}$ for well-designed DTA | Practical detection limit of $\Delta T$ | Sets the minimum detectable $\Delta H$ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **CRITICAL Notation Rule for KTU Scripts**
> In your answer book, use $\Delta T$, $T_s$, $T_r$, and $\beta$ **consistently**. KTU examiners penalise inconsistent variable substitution (e.g., writing $T_s$ on one line and $T_1$ on the next without defining the equivalence).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Ceramics and refractories:** identification of clay minerals, quartz inversion at $573 \; ^\circ \text{C}$, mullite formation.
- **Polymers:** determination of glass-transition ($T_g$), melting ($T_m$), and crystallisation ($T_c$) temperatures.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** polymorph screening and purity assessment of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs).
- **Minerals and ores:** quantitative mineralogical analysis of limestone, cement raw mix, and bauxite.
- **Metals and alloys:** detection of solid-state phase transitions, precipitation, and oxidation onset.
- **Catalysis:** studying catalyst precursor decomposition and phase evolution.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The DTA Heat-Balance Equation (Sample and Reference)

**Statement to be derived:**
The temperature difference between sample and reference holders, $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$, is governed by the heat capacities of sample and reference, the thermal resistance $R$ between holder and furnace, the heating rate $\beta = dT_f/dt$, and the rate of heat generation/consumption $\Delta H \cdot df/dt$ within the sample.

**Step-by-step logical development:**

### Step A — Set up the energy balance for the reference holder
The reference is inert; heat enters from the furnace of temperature $T_f$ through a thermal resistance $R$:

$$
C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} = \dfrac{T_f - T_r}{R}
$$

### Step B — Set up the energy balance for the sample holder
The sample additionally generates or absorbs heat at the rate $\Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}$:

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} = \dfrac{T_f - T_s}{R} + \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

### Step C — Introduce the heating rate $\beta$ of the furnace
By definition:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{dT_f}{dt}
$$

### Step D — Subtract the reference equation from the sample equation
Subtracting Step A from Step B eliminates the common $T_f$ term:

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} - C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} = \dfrac{T_s - T_r}{R} \cdot (-1) + \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

Re-arranging, with $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$:

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} - C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} - \dfrac{(-\Delta T)}{R} = \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} - C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} + \dfrac{\Delta T}{R} = \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

### Step E — Recognise the governing DTA equation
This is the canonical governing equation of DTA. The left-hand side contains three terms: a sample heat-capacity term, a reference heat-capacity term, and the differential-temperature term. The right-hand side is the heat-generation rate from the sample's transformation.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> When *no* transformation occurs ($df/dt = 0$), the equation reduces to a coupled heat-conduction problem. The baseline shift you see on a real DTA curve (a gentle drift, not a peak) is precisely the manifestation of $C_s \neq C_r$ — a key reason a thermally matched reference is desirable.

## 3.2 Derivation II — The Speil Equation for Peak-Area Enthalpy

**Statement:** The area $A$ enclosed by a DTA peak and the extrapolated baseline is proportional to the enthalpy change $\Delta H$ of the underlying transformation.

**Derivation walk-through:**

### Step 1 — Assume the baseline (no transformation) sets $\Delta T_0 = 0$
For an ideal baseline:

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s^{(0)}}{dt} - C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} + \dfrac{0}{R} = 0
$$

So:

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s^{(0)}}{dt} = C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt}
$$

### Step 2 — During the transformation, $\Delta T \neq 0$
The full equation is:

$$
C_s \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} - C_r \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} + \dfrac{\Delta T}{R} = \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

### Step 3 — Take the difference between the "with reaction" and "baseline" cases
Subtracting the baseline result from the full result:

$$
C_s \dfrac{d(T_s - T_s^{(0)})}{dt} + \dfrac{\Delta T}{R} = \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

### Step 4 — Assume a small heat-capacity correction
If the sample's heat capacity is approximately constant over the narrow temperature range of the peak, the first term integrates to a small constant baseline shift, which we absorb into a calibration constant $K$. We are left with:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta T}{R} = \Delta H \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}
$$

### Step 5 — Integrate over the entire peak (from onset to completion)
At the start and end of the peak, $f = 0$ and $f = 1$ (no conversion to full conversion):

$$
\int_{\text{peak}} \dfrac{\Delta T}{R} \, dt = \Delta H \int_0^1 df = \Delta H
$$

### Step 6 — Express the integral in terms of peak area
The integral $\int \Delta T \, dt = A_{\text{peak}}$ (peak area on the $\Delta T$ vs $t$ plot). If we plot $\Delta T$ vs $T$ instead, and recall $\beta = dT/dt$, then $dt = dT/\beta$, giving:

$$
A_T = \int \Delta T \, dT = \beta \int \Delta T \, dt
$$

Combining:

$$
\Delta H = \dfrac{K \cdot A_T}{m \cdot \beta}
$$

where $K$ is a calibration constant determined experimentally by running a standard of known $\Delta H$ (e.g., melting of high-purity indium, $\Delta H_f = 28.45 \; \text{J/g}$ at $T_f = 156.6 \; ^\circ \text{C}$).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Calibration is Mandatory**
> The Speil equation **in isolation** is *not* a quantitative tool. The proportionality constant $K$ depends on the sample holder geometry, thermocouple placement, gas atmosphere, and heating rate. KTU board questions that ask you to "quantitatively determine $\Delta H$" **always** require you to state that a standard calibration has been performed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of DTA vs DSC vs TGA

This matrix is a **KTU board favourite** for 7-mark sub-questions.

| Parameter | DTA | DSC (Heat-flux) | TGA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Quantity measured** | $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$ | Heat-flow rate $dq/dt$ (in mW) | Mass change $dm$ |
| **Primary output** | $\Delta T$ vs $T$ (or $t$) | $dq/dt$ vs $T$ | % mass loss vs $T$ |
| **Endothermic sign** | Convention-dependent (often down) | Always UP per IUPAC | (Not applicable) |
| **Quantitativity of $\Delta H$** | Semi-quantitative (needs $K$) | **Directly quantitative** | N/A |
| **Sample size** | mg to g | mg | mg |
| **Resolution of $T_{\text{onset}}$** | Lower (peak-broadened) | Higher | N/A |
| **Simultaneous technique?** | Often paired with TGA (STA) | Often paired with TGA (STA) | Often paired with DTA/DSC |
| **Sensitivity to $\Delta H$** | Moderate | High | N/A |
| **Information given** | Phase transitions, reaction temps, qualitative $\Delta H$ | Same as DTA + **quantitative $\Delta H$**, $C_p$ | Decomposition, oxidation, thermal stability |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

**Question (Typical KTU Style):** A 12.5 mg sample exhibits a DTA melting peak with onset at $156.4 \; ^\circ \text{C}$, peak at $158.7 \; ^\circ \text{C}$, and peak area $A_T = 7.2 \; \text{K}\cdot\text{K}$ on the $\Delta T$ vs $T$ plot. The instrument was calibrated with indium ($\Delta H_f = 28.45 \; \text{J/g}$), giving a calibration constant $K = 49.4 \; \text{mJ/(mg}\cdot\text{K}\cdot\text{K)}$. The heating rate was $\beta = 10 \; \text{K/min}$. Calculate the enthalpy of fusion.

**Solution (every step shown):**

$$
\Delta H = \dfrac{K \cdot A_T}{m \cdot \beta}
$$

Substituting values (convert $K$ to consistent units: $K = 49.4 \; \text{mJ/(mg}\cdot\text{K}\cdot\text{K)}$):

$$
\Delta H = \dfrac{49.4 \;\text{mJ/(mg}\cdot\text{K}\cdot\text{K)} \times 7.2 \;\text{K}\cdot\text{K}}{12.5 \;\text{mg} \times 10 \;\text{K/min}}
$$

$$
\Delta H = \dfrac{355.68 \;\text{mJ/mg}}{125 \;\text{K}\cdot\text{mg/min}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{unit-check:} \;\;\dfrac{\text{mJ/mg}}{\text{K/min}} = \dfrac{\text{mJ}\cdot\text{min}}{\text{mg}\cdot\text{K}}
$$

Wait — we must re-examine the unit. A cleaner form is to express $K$ with the time integral already absorbed. The standard reporting form is:

$$
\Delta H \;[\text{J/g}] = K \cdot \dfrac{A_T \;[\text{K}\cdot\text{K}]}{m \;[\text{mg}]}
$$

Plugging numerical values:

$$
\Delta H = 49.4 \times \dfrac{7.2}{12.5} = 49.4 \times 0.576
$$

$$
\Delta H = 28.45 \;\text{J/g}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Pedagogical Note**
> The numbers were chosen so that the answer matches indium's tabulated $\Delta H_f$ — illustrating the calibration philosophy: $K$ is found *from* a known standard, then applied to unknowns. Always explicitly state the units being assumed for $K$ in the exam.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DTA Instrument Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["PROGRAMMABLE TEMPERATURE CONTROLLER"]
    node2["PID CONTROL LOOP FOR FURNACE"]
    node3["RESISTANCE OR INDUCTION FURNACE"]
    node4["SYMMETRIC TWIN CRUCIBLE HOLDER BLOCK"]
    node5["SAMPLE CRUCIBLE WITH S THERMOCOUPLE"]
    node6["REFERENCE CRUCIBLE WITH R THERMOCOUPLE"]
    node7["DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER STAGE"]
    node8["REFERENCE TEMPERATURE AMPLIFIER"]
    node9["DATA ACQUISITION AND ADC CARD"]
    node10["COMPUTER WITH DTA SOFTWARE"]
    node11["PRINTER OR PLOTTER OUTPUT"]
    node12["GAS FLOW SYSTEM NITROGEN OR AIR"]
    node13["COOLING WATER JACKET"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node12 --> node4
    node13 --> node3
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **furnace** is the heat source, controlled by a **PID loop** that maintains the linear heating rate.
> - The **symmetric holder block** ensures the sample and reference sit in *thermally equivalent* positions.
> - The **two thermocouples** are the heart of DTA: their difference ($\Delta T$) and absolute value ($T_r$) are separately amplified, digitised, and recorded.
> - The **gas inlet** controls the atmosphere (oxidis­ing, inert, or reducing) — a critical experimental parameter.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — DTA Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["STEP 1: SAMPLE PREPARATION"]
    stepB["STEP 2: WEIGH SAMPLE PRECISELY ANALYTICAL BALANCE"]
    stepC["STEP 3: LOAD SAMPLE AND REFERENCE INTO CRUCIBLES"]
    stepD["STEP 4: PURGE WITH INERT GAS NITROGEN"]
    stepE["STEP 5: SET HEATING PROGRAM BETA AND T MAX"]
    stepF["STEP 6: RUN BASELINE WITH EMPTY PANS"]
    stepG["STEP 7: RUN SAMPLE RECORDING DELTA T AND T"]
    stepH["STEP 8: DETERMINE T ONSET AND PEAK AREA"]
    stepI["STEP 9: CALIBRATE USING STANDARD E G INDIUM"]
    stepJ["STEP 10: CALCULATE ENTHALPY OF TRANSFORMATION"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of a Typical DTA Thermogram

A labelled DTA curve typically exhibits the following features (described textually since Mermaid cannot render Cartesian plots cleanly):

- **Y-axis:** $\Delta T$ in microvolts ($\mu\text{V}$) or millikelvin ($\text{mK}$), positive upward = exothermic per IUPAC.
- **X-axis:** Furnace temperature $T$ in $^o\text{C}$ or K (or time $t$ in min).
- **Baseline** near $\Delta T = 0$ before the first event.
- **First peak** (downward = endothermic): e.g., dehydration of a hydrate at ~$100 \; ^\circ \text{C}$.
- **Second peak** (downward, sharper): e.g., polymorphic inversion of quartz at $573 \; ^\circ \text{C}$.
- **Third peak** (upward = exothermic): e.g., crystallisation of an amorphous phase.
- **Fourth peak** (downward, large): e.g., decomposition of the carbonate at $800$–$900 \; ^\circ \text{C}$.
- Marks for $T_{\text{onset}}$ (extrapolated from baseline to leading edge) and $T_{\text{peak}}$ (extremum).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]

**Define Differential Thermal Analysis. Mention any two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA) is a thermoanalytical technique in which the temperature difference ($\Delta T$) between a test sample and an inert reference material is recorded as a function of furnace temperature (or time) while both are subjected to an identical controlled thermal programme. The signal of interest is:

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_r
$$

**Two advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Rapid qualitative identification** of phase transitions, dehydration, decomposition, and oxidation events in solids.
2. **Small sample requirement** (few milligrams) and **wide temperature range** capability (from cryogenic to $>1500 \; ^\circ \text{C}$ with appropriate furnace).

**One limitation (0.5 mark):**
The enthalpy change $\Delta H$ is only **semi-quantitative** in classical DTA — accurate quantification requires calibration with a standard of known $\Delta H$ and depends strongly on sample geometry, packing, and heating rate.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**List the factors affecting the DTA curve and explain the effect of heating rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The factors affecting a DTA curve are:
1. Heating rate $\beta$ (1 mark for listing + 1 mark for the explanation below)
2. Sample mass
3. Particle size and packing density
4. Atmosphere (air, $\text{N}_2$, vacuum, etc.)
5. Thermal conductivity of the sample and reference
6. Crucible material and geometry

**Effect of heating rate (1 mark):**
- **Peak height $\Delta T_{\max}$** increases with $\beta$ (approximately linearly at low rates).
- **Peak temperature $T_{\text{peak}}$** shifts to higher values with increasing $\beta$, creating a thermal lag.
- **Peak area $A$** may be slightly affected because at very high $\beta$, the system cannot reach equilibrium.
- A high $\beta$ gives better sensitivity but **poorer resolution** of closely spaced peaks.

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner Note)**
> Many students write only "heating rate affects the curve" without specifying *which* parameter (height, position, area, resolution). Always name the affected feature explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified from Past Paper]

#### (a) Describe with a neat labelled block diagram the instrumentation of a DTA apparatus. Discuss the role of each block. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

A DTA instrument consists of the following interconnected blocks (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Furnace (1 mark):** A programmable resistance or induction furnace heats both sample and reference holders symmetrically. It accepts the linear temperature ramp from the controller and achieves heating rates of $1$–$20 \; \text{K/min}$ routinely, up to $50 \; \text{K/min}$ in some instruments.

2. **Sample and reference holder assembly (1 mark):** A symmetric twin-crucible block (often $\text{Pt}$, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, or Inconel) holds the sample and reference in identical thermal contact with the furnace wall. **Symmetry is critical** to ensure that the *only* temperature difference observed is due to the sample's transformation.

3. **Temperature sensors (thermocouples) (1 mark):** Two matched thermocouples (typically Type K: Chromel–Alumel for $<1370 \; \text{K}$ or Type R/S: Pt/Pt-Rh for higher temperatures) are embedded in the holders. One measures $T_s$, the other $T_r$. The difference gives the DTA signal; the absolute value gives the programme temperature.

4. **$\Delta T$ amplifier (1 mark):** The millivolt-level differential thermocouple voltage is amplified (gain $\times 100$ to $\times 1000$) by a low-noise DC differential amplifier, since $\Delta T$ is often only a fraction of a degree.

5. **Programmer / Temperature controller (1 mark):** A closed-loop PID controller compares the actual furnace temperature with the desired programme and adjusts the heating power. This ensures a *linear* $T$–$t$ profile regardless of endo/exothermic disturbances.

6. **Recorder / Data Acquisition System (1 mark):** A two-pen chart recorder, or a modern ADC–computer combination, simultaneously records $\Delta T$ and $T$ (or $t$). Modern systems digitise at $10$–$100 \; \text{Hz}$ for noise reduction and post-processing.

7. **Gas-flow system (1 mark):** A controlled supply of purge gas ($\text{N}_2$, $\text{Ar}$, or air) maintains the desired atmosphere, removes volatile decomposition products, and prevents condensation on cold surfaces.

> [!TIP] **Presentation Tip:** In your answer book, **always draw the block diagram first**, label every block, and then write the description in the *same order* as the blocks. KTU examiners reward logical flow and penalise jumbled explanations.

#### (b) Differentiate between DTA and DSC. List the applications of DTA in materials characterisation. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Distinguishing features (4 marks):**

| Basis | DTA | DSC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Measured quantity | $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$ | Heat-flow rate $dq/dt$ (in mW) |
| Sensor | Two thermocouples | Differential temperature sensors in a heat-flux plate |
| Quantitative $\Delta H$? | Indirect — needs Speil calibration ($K$ required) | **Direct** — the heat flow axis is pre-calibrated with standards |
| Baseline drift | Significant (depends on $C_s - C_r$) | Minimal (heat-flux design compensates) |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | Higher |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

> Additional verbal distinctions (to add in script):
> - DSC measures the *energy* required to keep $T_s = T_r$ (zero $\Delta T$ design), while DTA measures the *temperature difference* allowed to develop.
> - DSC is better for $C_p$ measurements; DTA is preferred for *very high temperatures* (above $1500 \; ^\circ \text{C}$) where heat-flux plates degrade.
> - DTA thermograms are more prone to baseline drift from changes in sample thermal conductivity.

**Applications of DTA (3 marks — list at least 5 with one-line explanations):**

1. **Phase-transition studies:** detection of polymorphic inversions, melting, and boiling points of metals, ceramics, and minerals.
2. **Thermal stability of materials:** determination of decomposition onset temperatures of polymers, pharmaceuticals, and energetic materials.
3. **Crystallisation studies in glasses and amorphous polymers:** exothermic crystallisation peak temperature and crystallinity index.
4. **Solid-state reaction kinetics:** Kissinger analysis of peak shift with heating rate to compute activation energy $E_a$.
5. **Identification of minerals and clay:** characteristic endothermic peaks for kaolinite ($550 \; ^\circ \text{C}$), montmorillonite, calcite ($750$–$900 \; ^\circ \text{C}$), and gypsum.
6. **Catalyst characterisation:** study of catalyst precursor decomposition, reduction, and phase formation in supported catalysts.
7. **Quality control in cement and ceramics:** estimation of free lime, $\text{CaCO}_3$ content, and burnability of cement raw mix.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

#### (a) Explain the principle of DTA with a neat thermogram. Identify the salient features: baseline, onset temperature, peak temperature, peak area, endothermic and exothermic peaks. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (3 marks):**
DTA is based on the principle that when a substance undergoes a physical change (such as a phase transition, melting, or boiling) or a chemical change (such as decomposition, oxidation, or reduction), heat is either absorbed (endothermic) or released (exothermic). In DTA, the temperature of the sample is compared to that of an inert reference material under identical heating conditions. Any deviation in temperature between the two indicates that a thermal event has occurred in the sample.

**Salient features of the DTA thermogram (4 marks — 0.5 mark each, with thermogram description):**

1. **Baseline (0.5 mark):** The flat portion of the curve where $\Delta T \approx 0$ — no thermal event; sample and reference track the furnace temperature identically.
2. **Onset temperature $T_{\text{onset}}$ (1 mark):** The temperature at which the peak *first deviates* from the baseline (extrapolated intersection of the leading edge with the baseline). It is the best estimate of the *true* equilibrium transformation temperature.
3. **Peak temperature $T_{\text{peak}}$ (1 mark):** The extremum (max or min) of the $\Delta T$ curve; represents the maximum rate of transformation.
4. **Peak area $A$ (0.5 mark):** The area enclosed between the peak and the extrapolated baseline; proportional to the enthalpy change $\Delta H$ of the event (Speil's equation).
5. **Endothermic peak (0.5 mark):** A downward deflection indicating heat *absorption* by the sample — typical of melting, boiling, dehydration, and decomposition to gaseous products.
6. **Exothermic peak (0.5 mark):** An upward deflection indicating heat *release* by the sample — typical of crystallisation, oxidation, combustion, and certain solid-state reactions.

#### (b) Discuss the factors affecting the DTA curve. Why is the choice of reference material important? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Factors affecting the DTA curve (5 marks):**

1. **Heating rate $\beta$ (1 mark):** Higher $\beta$ increases peak height and shifts $T_{\text{peak}}$ upward (thermal lag). Very high $\beta$ reduces resolution of closely spaced events.
2. **Sample mass (1 mark):** Larger mass produces a larger peak (more heat absorbed/released) and shifts $T_{\text{peak}}$ upward. A small, uniform mass is preferred for resolution.
3. **Particle size and packing (0.5 mark):** Fine, uniformly packed samples give reproducible peaks. Coarse or non-uniform packing causes peak broadening and asymmetric shapes.
4. **Atmosphere (0.5 mark):** Oxidising atmospheres (air, $\text{O}_2$) promote exothermic oxidation events; inert atmospheres ($\text{N}_2$, $\text{Ar}$) suppress them. Reactive atmospheres can introduce new peaks.
5. **Crucible material and geometry (0.5 mark):** Reactive crucibles can interact with the sample (e.g., $\text{Pt}$ crucibles in reducing atmospheres). The thermal resistance $R$ of the crucible affects sensitivity.
6. **Thermocouple placement and type (0.5 mark):** A poorly placed thermocouple introduces thermal lag; using the wrong type can saturate at high temperatures.
7. **Sample thermal conductivity and heat capacity (0.5 mark):** Insulating samples develop larger $\Delta T$ than conducting samples; mismatched $C_s$ and $C_r$ cause baseline drift.

**Importance of reference material choice (2 marks):**

The reference material must be:
- **Thermally inert** over the entire experimental temperature range (no phase transitions, no decomposition). Standard: calcined $\alpha$-$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$.
- **Thermally matched** to the sample in terms of heat capacity, thermal conductivity, and density. This minimises baseline drift caused by $C_s \neq C_r$ and ensures symmetric heat flow.
- **Chemically inert** — must not react with the sample, crucible, or atmosphere.
- **Physically stable** — should not sinter, decompose, or change mass over the experiment.

> If the reference reacts, decomposes, or has its own phase transition, **it generates its own DTA peak** that contaminates the sample's thermogram — a catastrophic source of artefacts. Therefore, the choice of reference is as critical as the choice of sample.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (KTU Board Pattern)**
> 1. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark questions on instrumentation — *minimum 1–2 marks lost* if the diagram is missing.
> 2. **Confusing DTA and DSC** in the comparison question — DTA measures $\Delta T$, DSC measures $dq/dt$ in mW. Examiners flag this and deduct 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the convention** for endothermic/exothermic peaks (IUPAC vs older convention) — always specify the sign convention.
> 4. **Not mentioning the calibration step** when quantitative enthalpy is asked — write "the constant $K$ is determined using a standard of known $\Delta H$ (e.g., indium)".
> 5. **Stating $T_{\text{peak}} = T_{\text{transition}}$** — this is wrong. $T_{\text{peak}}$ depends on heating rate; the true transition temperature is $T_{\text{onset}}$ (or the extrapolated onset).
> 6. **Failing to list the type and range of thermocouples** in the instrumentation answer — write "Type K, Chromel–Alumel, range up to $1370 \; \text{K}$".
> 7. **Missing the role of the purge gas** — examiners expect at least one sentence on atmosphere control.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DTA = differential temperature analyser** — measures $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$ vs $T$ (or $t$).
- **Reference material:** thermally inert, thermally matched, chemically inert — typically calcined $\alpha$-$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$.
- **Thermocouples:** Type K (Chromel–Alumel) for routine work up to $1370 \; \text{K}$; Type R/S (Pt/Pt-Rh) for $>1370 \; \text{K}$.
- **Endothermic peak** (IUPAC: $\Delta T$ negative if $T_s - T_r$ convention is used): melting, boiling, dehydration, decomposition to volatiles.
- **Exothermic peak** (IUPAC: $\Delta T$ positive): crystallisation, oxidation, combustion.
- **$T_{\text{onset}}$** = best estimate of true transition temperature; **$T_{\text{peak}}$** = maximum rate (heating-rate dependent).
- **Speil's equation:** $\Delta H = K \cdot A / m$ (semi-quantitative; $K$ from calibration with known standard).
- **Heating rate $\beta$:** higher $\beta$ → higher peak, shifted to higher $T$, poorer resolution.
- **Atmosphere control** via purge gas is essential — affects oxidation peaks.
- **DTA vs DSC:** DTA measures $\Delta T$ (semi-quantitative $\Delta H$); DSC measures $dq/dt$ in mW (quantitative $\Delta H$ + $C_p$).
- **STA (Simultaneous Thermal Analyser):** combines DTA (or DSC) with TGA in one instrument.
- **Kissinger relation:** peak shift with $\beta$ gives activation energy $E_a$ of solid-state reactions.
- **Applications:** phase transitions, thermal stability, glass crystallisation, mineral identification, cement and ceramic QC, catalyst characterisation.
- **For 7-mark instrumentation answers:** always draw the block diagram, label every block, and discuss each in *one* sentence.
- **For 7-mark DTA vs DSC answers:** tabulate the differences *and* add 2–3 lines of verbal explanation — tabulation alone is not enough.

> [!TIP] **One-Line Memory Aid for KTU Viva / Oral Exam**
> *"DTA listens to the **temperature gap** between two twins — one reacting, one inert — and tells you **when** a phase change happened and roughly **how much heat** it took."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** is a thermoanalytical technique in which the **heat flow** (rate of heat energy transfer) into a sample and a reference material is measured as a function of **temperature** or **time**, while both are subjected to a controlled temperature program under a specified atmosphere. The technique quantitatively measures the **enthalpy changes** ($\Delta H$) associated with various thermal transitions and reactions occurring in the material.

The fundamental measured quantity in DSC is the **differential heat flow** $\frac{dQ}{dt}$ (in $\text{mW}$ or $\text{mJ/s}$), which is plotted against temperature (or time) to produce a **DSC thermogram**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Burner Stove" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine you have a kitchen with two identical saucepans (sample pan and reference pan) sitting on a smart induction stove. The stove has a built-in sensor that constantly adjusts the power to each pan so that both pans reach exactly the same temperature at every moment.
>
> - If both pans contain **inert empty crucibles**, the stove supplies equal power to both. The differential power ($\Delta P = 0$) — this is your *baseline*.
> - Now place your **polymer sample** in one pan. As the temperature rises, the polymer may undergo a *melting* event (it needs extra energy to break its crystalline lattice). The stove automatically **pumps in extra power** to that pan to keep it at the same temperature as the empty reference pan. This **spike in differential power** is what the DSC records.
> - Conversely, when the polymer *crystallizes* (releases latent heat), the stove must **reduce power** to keep the temperature equal — producing a dip in the curve.
>
> This "smart stove" is exactly what a DSC instrument does — it continuously records the **power difference** required to keep sample and reference at the **same temperature**, revealing the hidden thermal events within the material.

## 1.3 The Two Principal DSC Modes

KTU 2024 syllabus classifies DSC instruments into two design architectures:

| Mode | What it measures | Symbol | Mechanical Setup |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat-Flux DSC** | Temperature difference between sample and reference | $\Delta T$ | Single furnace, sample & reference on same sensor disk |
| **Power-Compensation DSC** | Electrical power difference supplied to two heaters | $\Delta P$ | Two independent micro-furnaces, one per crucible |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Which is more sensitive?", the answer is **Power-Compensation DSC** (shorter response time, lower thermal mass). However, **Heat-Flux DSC** is the most common laboratory type (e.g., Mettler-Toledo, TA Instruments Q-series).

## 1.4 Information Obtained from a Single DSC Run

A well-designed DSC experiment can yield:

1. **Glass transition temperature** ($T_g$) — second-order transition
2. **Crystallization temperature** ($T_c$) and **enthalpy of crystallization** ($\Delta H_c$)
3. **Melting temperature** ($T_m$) and **enthalpy of fusion** ($\Delta H_f$)
4. **Degree of crystallinity** ($\%X_c$) — critical for semi-crystalline polymers
5. **Specific heat capacity** ($C_p$) — using the three-run method
6. **Oxidation Onset Temperature (OOT)** and **Oxidation Induction Time (OIT)**
7. **Kinetics of curing** (for thermosets, epoxies, resins)
8. **Purity analysis** of pharmaceutical / organic compounds

## 1.5 Universal Physical Constants Referenced in DSC

> **Key DSC Reference Standards (used in calibration):**
> - **Indium (In):** $T_m = 156.6\,^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta H_f = 28.45\,\text{J/g}$
> - **Tin (Sn):** $T_m = 231.93\,^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta H_f = 60.46\,\text{J/g}$
> - **Zinc (Zn):** $T_m = 419.5\,^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta H_f = 108.4\,\text{J/g}$
> - **Sapphire (Al₂O₃):** Standard reference for $C_p$ measurement
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Heart of DSC

The DSC instrument is governed by a closed-loop thermal control system. The basic operating logic is as follows:

1. **Sample and reference** are placed in identical crucibles (usually aluminum, hermetically sealed or open).
2. Both are subjected to an identical **temperature program** — typically linear heating at a constant rate (e.g., $5$, $10$, or $20\,^\circ\text{C/min}$).
3. The instrument continuously monitors the **heat flow** supplied to each.
4. The **differential signal** $\left(\frac{dQ}{dt}\right)_{\text{sample}} - \left(\frac{dQ}{dt}\right)_{\text{reference}}$ is plotted versus temperature.
5. When the sample undergoes a transition (absorption or release of energy), the differential signal deviates from the baseline.
6. The area under the peak = enthalpy of the transition.

## 2.2 Heat-Flux DSC — Detailed Working Logic

In a heat-flux DSC, a single furnace heats both sample and reference through a common metallic disk (the *thermal resistance platform*). Let $R_s$ and $R_r$ be the thermal resistances from furnace to sample and furnace to reference respectively.

- Heat flow to sample: $\frac{dQ_s}{dt} = \frac{T_f - T_s}{R_s}$
- Heat flow to reference: $\frac{dQ_r}{dt} = \frac{T_f - T_r}{R_r}$
- Differential heat flow: $\Delta \frac{dQ}{dt} = \frac{dQ_s}{dt} - \frac{dQ_r}{dt} = \frac{T_r - T_s}{R_s} = K \cdot \Delta T$

> Here, $K = \dfrac{1}{R_s}$ is the **cell calibration constant** (units: $\text{W/K}$ or $\text{mW/°C}$), determined experimentally using indium calibration.

The instrument's software multiplies the measured $\Delta T$ by the pre-determined $K$ value to convert it directly into heat-flow units ($\text{mW}$).

## 2.3 Power-Compensation DSC — Detailed Working Logic

In power-compensation DSC, sample and reference are placed in **two separate, independently controlled furnaces**. Each furnace has its own heater and temperature sensor.

- A control loop adjusts the **power to each heater** such that $T_s = T_r$ at every instant.
- The measured quantity is the **difference in electrical power** supplied: $\Delta P = P_s - P_r$.
- For small sample masses and low heating rates, $\Delta P$ directly equals the differential heat flow: $\Delta P \approx \dfrac{dQ_s}{dt} - \dfrac{dQ_r}{dt}$.

> This design has a **lower thermal mass** and **faster response time** — it can resolve sharper transitions and is preferred for high-sensitivity work (e.g., biological samples, pharmaceuticals).

## 2.4 Conventions for Plotting DSC Curves

| Event Type | Physical Meaning | Sign on thermogram (Standard IUPAC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Endotherm** | Sample absorbs heat (melting, vaporization, desorption) | **Peak points UP** (positive $y$-axis) |
| **Exotherm** | Sample releases heat (crystallization, oxidation, curing) | **Peak points DOWN** (negative $y$-axis) |
| **Glass transition** | Second-order change in $C_p$ | **Step change** in baseline (no peak) |

> [!WARNING]
> Always check the convention used by your instrument! Some manufacturers (older Mettler data) invert the $y$-axis, plotting endotherms as negative. KTU 2024 expected answer: **Endotherm = UP, Exotherm = DOWN** (IUPAC recommendation).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = K \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat-flux DSC fundamental relation | $\text{mW}$ |
| 2 | $T = T_0 + \beta t$ | Programmed temperature ($\beta$ = heating rate) | $^\circ\text{C}$ or $\text{K}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \beta$ | Heat flow to sample at constant $C_p$ (no transition) | $\text{mW}$ |
| 4 | $\Delta H = \displaystyle\int \dfrac{dQ}{dt} \, dt$ | Total enthalpy of transition = area under the peak | $\text{J}$ or $\text{J/g}$ |
| 5 | $\Delta H_f = \dfrac{A_{\text{peak}}}{\beta \cdot m}$ | Enthalpy from peak area, $A_{\text{peak}}$ in $\text{mJ}$, $m$ in $\text{mg}$ | $\text{J/g}$ |
| 6 | $\%X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100$ | Percent crystallinity (semi-crystalline polymer) | dimensionless % |
| 7 | $C_p = \dfrac{1}{m} \cdot \dfrac{dQ}{dT} \bigg\vert_p$ | Specific heat capacity at constant pressure | $\text{J/(g·K)}$ |
| 8 | $\Delta C_p (T_g) = C_{p,\text{glass}} - C_{p,\text{rubber}}$ | Heat capacity jump at glass transition | $\text{J/(g·K)}$ |
| 9 | $\ln \beta = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_p} + \text{const}$ | Kissinger equation (kinetics of curing/crystallization) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $x_p = 1 - F(\alpha_p) = 0.37$ | Constant relating peak position to activation energy | dimensionless |

> **Note on Table 2.2:** In equation (1) above, the cell constant $K$ has units of $\text{W/K}$; in equations (4) and (5) the integral is taken over the transition peak, excluding the baseline.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

DSC is the **workhorse characterization tool** in the polymer industry because:

- **Quality Control:** A single 20-minute DSC run verifies batch-to-batch consistency of $T_g$, $T_m$, and crystallinity in polyolefins, PET, polyamides.
- **Polymer Identification:** Unknown plastics can be identified by their characteristic $T_g$/$T_m$ "fingerprint".
- **Composite Curing:** Epoxy / phenolic / unsaturated-polyester curing profiles are quantified — critical for aerospace and automotive composites.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Purity determination (van't Hoff analysis) and polymorphism screening of drug crystals.
- **Food Industry:** Fat crystallization profiles, cocoa butter polymorphism, starch gelatinization.
- **Metallurgy:** Phase transformation studies, alloy design, solidification analysis.
- **Battery Research:** Thermal runaway analysis of Li-ion cells, SEI layer decomposition temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat-Flow Equation for Heat-Flux DSC

**Starting physical setup:** A single furnace supplies heat to both the sample (pan + contents) and reference (empty pan) through a symmetric thermal resistance disk.

**Step 1 — Define thermal resistances.**
Let $R_s$ and $R_r$ be the thermal resistances from the furnace to the sample pan and from the furnace to the reference pan, respectively. For a well-designed symmetric cell, $R_s = R_r = R$.

**Step 2 — Express the heat flow to each side.**
By the definition of thermal resistance (analogous to Ohm's law for heat), the rate of heat flow into the sample is the temperature difference divided by the resistance:

$$\frac{dQ_s}{dt} = \frac{T_f - T_s}{R_s}$$

Similarly, for the reference:

$$\frac{dQ_r}{dt} = \frac{T_f - T_r}{R_r}$$

**Step 3 — Take the difference.**
The measured differential signal is the difference between the two heat flows. Subtracting the two equations:

$$\frac{dQ_s}{dt} - \frac{dQ_r}{dt} = \frac{T_f - T_s}{R_s} - \frac{T_f - T_r}{R_r}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the symmetry condition $R_s = R_r = R$.**
Under this assumption, the two denominator terms are equal. Combining over a common denominator $R$:

$$\Delta \frac{dQ}{dt} = \frac{1}{R} \left[ (T_f - T_s) - (T_f - T_r) \right] = \frac{T_r - T_s}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the cell constant.**
Define $K = \dfrac{1}{R}$ (the *cell constant* with units $\text{W/K}$). Substituting:

$$\boxed{\frac{dQ}{dt} = K \cdot \Delta T}$$

where $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$ is the measured temperature difference between sample and reference (with sign convention reversed depending on manufacturer). $\square$

> **Engineering Insight:** The cell constant $K$ is **not** a universal physical constant — it depends on the geometry, the disk material, the sample-pan contact resistance, and the purge-gas thermal conductivity. Hence, $K$ must be **calibrated experimentally** using a standard of known enthalpy (typically indium).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Enthalpy of Transition from Peak Area

**Starting relation:** The total heat absorbed (or released) during a transition equals the time integral of the heat-flow rate:

$$\Delta H = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{dQ}{dt} \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Convert the integration variable from time to temperature.**
Using the heating rate $\beta = dT/dt$, we substitute $dt = dT/\beta$:

$$\Delta H = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dQ}{dt} \cdot \frac{1}{\beta} \, dT = \frac{1}{\beta} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dQ}{dt} \, dT$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the integral as the peak area.**
The integral $\displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dQ}{dt} \, dT$ is exactly the area $A_{\text{peak}}$ enclosed between the DSC curve and the (interpolated) baseline over the transition range $[T_1, T_2]$.

**Step 3 — Divide by sample mass to obtain specific enthalpy.**
For a sample of mass $m$:

$$\Delta H = \frac{A_{\text{peak}}}{\beta \cdot m}$$

with $A_{\text{peak}}$ measured in $\text{mJ}$, $\beta$ in $^\circ\text{C/s}$, and $m$ in $\text{mg}$, giving $\Delta H$ in $\text{J/g}$.

$$\boxed{\Delta H = \frac{A_{\text{peak}}}{\beta \cdot m}}$$

This is the **master equation** used in every quantitative DSC analysis. $\square$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Percent Crystallinity for a Semi-Crystalline Polymer

**Premise:** A semi-crystalline polymer contains both crystalline and amorphous domains. The measured enthalpy of fusion $\Delta H_f$ is proportional to the mass fraction of crystalline material.

**Step 1 — Define the 100% crystalline reference enthalpy.**
Let $\Delta H_f^\circ$ be the enthalpy of fusion of a **theoretically 100% crystalline** sample of the same polymer. Published literature values:

| Polymer | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (J/g) |
|---|---|
| Polyethylene (PE) | 293 |
| Polypropylene (PP) | 207 |
| Poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) | 140 |
| Polyamide-6,6 (PA66) | 196 |
| Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) | 82 |

**Step 2 — Formulate the proportionality.**
If $X_c$ is the mass fraction of crystalline material:

$$\Delta H_f = X_c \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $X_c$ and convert to percent.**

$$X_c = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ}$$

$$\boxed{\%X_c = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100}$$

> **Note for students:** This relation assumes that the **amorphous contribution to the melting peak is zero** — i.e., the cold-crystallization peak (if any) has been subtracted from the melting peak. In practice: $\Delta H_f = \Delta H_{m,\text{peak}} - \Delta H_{c,\text{peak}}$ (corrected for any recrystallization during heating).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heat Capacity $C_p$ from the Three-Run Method

**Step 1 — Run a blank** (empty crucibles on both sides) at heating rate $\beta$. The measured differential signal $D_{\text{blank}}(T)$ represents the asymmetry of the cell itself.

**Step 2 — Run a standard** (sapphire, Al₂O₃) of known mass $m_s$ and known $C_p^s(T)$. The measured signal is $D_{\text{std}}(T) = D_{\text{blank}}(T) + \beta \cdot m_s \cdot C_p^s(T)$.

**Step 3 — Run the sample** of mass $m_x$ with unknown $C_p^x(T)$. The measured signal is $D_x(T) = D_{\text{blank}}(T) + \beta \cdot m_x \cdot C_p^x(T)$.

**Step 4 — Subtract blank and take ratio to eliminate instrument factors.**

$$C_p^x(T) = \frac{m_s \cdot C_p^s(T)}{m_x} \cdot \frac{D_x(T) - D_{\text{blank}}(T)}{D_{\text{std}}(T) - D_{\text{blank}}(T)}$$

This **ratio method** is the most accurate way to extract $C_p$ because the instrument's temperature-dependent sensitivity, gas-flow effects, and baseline drift are automatically cancelled out. $\square$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Common DSC Features

| Feature on thermogram | Transition type | Sign | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Step change in baseline** | Glass transition (2nd order) | Neutral (no peak) | Amorphous PS, PMMA, PC |
| **Sharp upward peak** | Melting (fusion) | Endothermic | PE, PP, PET, Nylon 6 |
| **Sharp downward peak** | Cold crystallization | Exothermic | PET (when quenched), iPP |
| **Broad downward peak** | Cure / crosslinking | Exothermic | Epoxy, phenolic, unsaturated polyester |
| **Sharp upward peak, low T** | Desorption / loss of moisture | Endothermic | Hydrated salts, hygroscopic polymers |
| **Sharp downward peak, high T** | Thermal degradation / oxidation | Exothermic | Polyolefins in O₂ above 200°C |
| **Two overlapping peaks** | Polymorphism / multiple crystal forms | Endothermic | Cocoa butter, PVDF (α and β) |

## 3.6 Kissinger Equation (Curing Kinetics — Optional but Important for Composites)

For thermoset curing or polymer crystallization kinetics, the relationship between heating rate $\beta$ and peak temperature $T_p$ is given by the Kissinger equation:

$$\ln \left( \frac{\beta}{T_p^2} \right) = -\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_p} + \text{const}$$

A plot of $\ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ versus $1/T_p$ gives a straight line with slope $-E_a/R$, from which the **activation energy** $E_a$ of the curing process is determined. Multiple DSC runs at different heating rates ($\beta = 5, 10, 15, 20\,^\circ\text{C/min}$) are required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Heat-Flux DSC Instrument Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["Temperature Programmer and Controller"]
        TP[Programmed Temperature Profile T of t]
        FB[Feedback Comparator]
    end

    subgraph FUR["Single Furnace Assembly"]
        FH[Furnace Heater Element]
        TD[Thermal Disk with Sensors]
    end

    subgraph CELL["Sample and Reference Cell"]
        SP[Sample Pan with Polymer]
        RP[Reference Pan Empty]
    end

    subgraph SIG["Signal Conditioning and Display"]
        TC[Thermocouple Junction A and B]
        AMP[Differential Amplifier]
        ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
        PC[Computer with DSC Software]
```
