```mermaid
    end

    GAS[Purge Gas Supply N2 or O2]

    TP --> FH
    FH --> TD
    TD --> SP
    TD --> RP
    SP --> TC
    RP --> TC
    TC --> AMP
    AMP --> ADC
    ADC --> PC
    FB --> FH
    GAS --> CELL
```

**How to read this diagram:** The programmer sends a target temperature vs. time profile to the furnace heater. Heat flows through the thermal disk to both pans. The two thermocouples (one under each pan) produce voltages proportional to $T_s$ and $T_r$. The differential amplifier outputs $\Delta T$, the ADC digitizes it, and the software multiplies by the cell constant $K$ to give a calibrated heat-flow signal. Purge gas (typically **N₂** for inert experiments, **O₂** for oxidative stability) is fed through the cell to remove air and prevent condensation.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Power-Compensation DSC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PWR["Power-Compensation DSC"]
        LF[Left Furnace for Sample]
        RF[Right Furnace for Reference]
        LH[Sample Heater Element]
        RH[Reference Heater Element]
        LS[Sample Temperature Sensor]
        RS[Reference Temperature Sensor]
        CB[Null-Balance Bridge Comparator]
        DIFF[Differential Power Output Delta P]
    end

    S[Sample in Crucible] --> LF
    R[Reference Empty Pan] --> RF
    LF --> LH
    RF --> RH
    LH --> LS
    RH --> RS
    LS --> CB
    RS --> CB
    CB --> LH
    CB --> RH
    CB --> DIFF
```

**How to read this diagram:** The null-balance bridge continuously adjusts the power supplied to **both** the sample and reference heaters so that $T_s = T_r$ at every instant. The differential power $\Delta P$ needed to maintain this equality is the direct output signal. Because the two heaters are independent, the response time is fast and the baseline is very stable.

## 4.3 Typical DSC Thermogram — Annotated Features

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TH["Typical DSC Thermogram for a Semicrystalline Polymer"]
        A[Start of Run at T1]
        B[Glass Transition Tg as Step in Baseline]
        C[Cold Crystallization Peak Exothermic Downward]
        D[Melting Peak Endothermic Upward]
        E[End of Run at T2]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
```

> **Reading guide for examiners:**
> - $A \to B$: baseline region where only $C_p$ heating occurs
> - $B$: glass transition is detected as a **step in the baseline**, not a peak
> - $B \to C$: supercooled amorphous region
> - $C$: cold crystallization — polymer chains reorganize into crystals (exothermic)
> - $C \to D$: partial melting of imperfect crystals
> - $D$: main melting peak — area gives $\Delta H_f$
> - $D \to E$: melt region, only $C_p$ baseline again

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Identifying DSC Transitions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Is there a STEP change in baseline?}
    Q -->|YES| Tg[Glass Transition Tg]
    Q -->|NO| Q2{Is there a PEAK pointing UP?}
    Q2 -->|YES| EN[Endothermic Event: Melting, Vaporization, Desorption]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{Is there a PEAK pointing DOWN?}
    Q3 -->|YES| EX[Exothermic Event: Crystallization, Curing, Oxidation]
```

**Use this flowchart in the lab to interpret your thermogram.** First, look for a baseline step (= $T_g$). If no step, look for a peak. If peak points up (= endotherm), measure $T_m$ from the peak onset. If peak points down (= exotherm), measure $T_c$ from the peak apex.

## 4.5 Schematic — Heat-Flux Cell Cross-Section (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph X["Heat Flux DSC Cell Cross Section Top View"]
        FURN[Outer Furnace Wall]
        DISK[Chromel Constantan Thermal Disk]
        TC1[Chromel Wire to Sample]
        TC2[Alumel Wire to Reference]
        SP[Sample Pan with Lid]
        RP[Reference Pan with Lid]
    end

    FURN --> DISK
    DISK --> SP
    DISK --> RP
    TC1 --> SP
    TC2 --> RP
```

**Note:** The chromel–constantan (or chromel–alumel) thermocouple junctions are welded to the bottom of each pan, allowing precise measurement of $T_s$ and $T_r$. The thermal disk acts as the controlled thermal resistance that produces $\Delta T$ proportional to heat flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC). State the difference between DSC and DTA.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **DSC Definition (1 mark):** Differential Scanning Calorimetry is a thermoanalytical technique that measures the **differential heat flow** (rate of energy transfer) into a sample and a reference material, both subjected to an identical controlled temperature program.

> **DSC vs DTA — Two key differences (2 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | DTA | DSC |
> |---|---|---|
> | Measured quantity | $\Delta T$ (temperature difference) | $\frac{dQ}{dt}$ (heat flow) |
> | Output | Temperature difference plot | Heat-flow versus $T$ (or $t$) |
> | Calibration | Temperature only | Both temperature and enthalpy |
> | Quantitative | Less accurate | Direct $\Delta H$ from peak area |

---

### Q2. What is the glass transition temperature ($T_g$)? How is it identified on a DSC thermogram?  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** $T_g$ is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state due to the onset of long-range segmental motion of polymer chains.

> **Identification on DSC (2 marks):** Unlike melting or crystallization, $T_g$ does **not** appear as a peak. It is detected as a **step change (or shift) in the baseline** of the heat-flow curve, corresponding to a change in $C_p$ of the polymer. The temperature is reported as the **midpoint** of the step, or alternatively as the **onset** of the step (defined by the intersection of the pre-transition and post-transition tangent lines).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write that $T_g$ is a peak. It is **NOT a peak** — it is a **second-order transition** showing only a baseline shift, not a latent-heat event. Examiners deduct full marks for this error.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Heat-Flux DSC and Crystallinity

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Heat-Flux Differential Scanning Calorimeter**. Derive the relation between heat flow and temperature difference.  *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A 5.0 mg sample of a polypropylene (PP) is heated in a DSC at $10\,^\circ\text{C/min}$. The melting peak area is measured as $850\,\text{mJ}$. The enthalpy of fusion of $100\%$ crystalline PP is $\Delta H_f^\circ = 207\,\text{J/g}$. Calculate the **percent crystallinity** of the sample.  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):** A heat-flux DSC consists of a single furnace, a thermally conducting disk (chromel-constantan), sample and reference pans placed symmetrically on the disk, thermocouples welded to the bottom of each pan, a temperature programmer, a differential amplifier, an ADC, and a computer with DSC software. **Purge gas** (N₂ or O₂) is fed through the cell.

2. **Working (2 marks):** Both pans are heated under an identical temperature program. When the sample undergoes a thermal transition, a temperature difference $\Delta T$ develops. The differential amplifier measures $\Delta T$ and the software multiplies it by the cell constant $K$ to obtain heat flow.

3. **Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, with $R_s = R_r = R$:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = K \cdot \Delta T, \quad \text{where} \quad K = \frac{1}{R}$$

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Given data.**
- Sample mass $m = 5.0\,\text{mg} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{g}$
- Heating rate $\beta = 10\,^\circ\text{C/min}$
- Peak area $A_{\text{peak}} = 850\,\text{mJ}$
- $\Delta H_f^\circ = 207\,\text{J/g}$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta H_f$ of the sample using the area formula.**

$$\Delta H_f = \frac{A_{\text{peak}}}{\beta \cdot m}$$

Substituting with consistent units ($\beta$ in $^\circ\text{C/s}$): $\beta = 10/60 = 0.1667\,^\circ\text{C/s}$

$$\Delta H_f = \frac{850\,\text{mJ}}{0.1667\,^\circ\text{C/s} \times 5.0\,\text{mg}}$$

$$\Delta H_f = \frac{850}{0.8333}\,\text{J/g} = 1020\,\text{J/g}$$

**Step 3 — Compute percent crystallinity.**

$$\%X_c = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100 = \frac{102}{207} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%X_c \approx 49.3\%}$$

**Step 4 — Comment (optional but impressive).** A commercial isotactic PP typically exhibits $40\text{–}60\%$ crystallinity, so this value is realistic. (Award +1 mark if such interpretation is provided.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall 1:** Forgetting to convert $\beta$ from $^\circ\text{C/min}$ to $^\circ\text{C/s}$ (or mg to g). This is the most common error and costs 2 marks.
> **Valuation Pitfall 2:** Using $\Delta H_f^\circ = 207\,\text{J/g}$ as the *answer* instead of dividing by it for percent crystallinity.
> **Valuation Pitfall 3:** Reporting a crystallinity $>100\%$ or $<0\%$ without rechecking the calculation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Power-Compensation DSC and Thermal Transitions

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(a)* Explain the principle and construction of a **Power-Compensation DSC**. Compare it with **Heat-Flux DSC** in a tabular form.  *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* With the help of a typical DSC thermogram of a **semi-crystalline polymer**, explain the identification of the following thermal transitions: *(i)* glass transition, *(ii)* cold crystallization, and *(iii)* melting. State the units and sign convention used.  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Principle (2 marks):** In power-compensation DSC, the sample and reference are placed in **two separate furnaces**, each with its own heater and temperature sensor. A control loop continuously adjusts the power to each heater so that both are maintained at the same programmed temperature. The **difference in electrical power** ($\Delta P$) supplied to the two heaters is the measured signal and is directly equal to the differential heat flow.

**2. Construction (2 marks):** Two independent micro-furnaces (low thermal mass), two platinum resistance thermometers (PRTs) or thermocouples, two separate heater coils, a control amplifier, a difference amplifier, ADC, and computer interface. A constantan platform is **not** used (unlike heat-flux).

**3. Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Heat-Flux DSC | Power-Compensation DSC |
|---|---|---|
| Furnaces | One (shared) | Two (independent) |
| Sensor | Thermocouple disk | Two PRTs/heaters |
| Measured quantity | $\Delta T$ (multiplied by $K$) | $\Delta P$ directly |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | High |
| Response time | Slower | Faster |
| Baseline stability | Good | Excellent |
| Typical use | Routine QC, polymers | Pharmaceuticals, biomolecules |

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Sign convention (1 mark):** IUPAC — **Endothermic events plotted UP** (positive $y$-axis), **Exothermic events plotted DOWN** (negative $y$-axis). $x$-axis = Temperature (°C or K), $y$-axis = Heat flow (mW or mJ/s).

**Typical thermogram features (6 marks, 2 each):**

| # | Transition | Thermogram Feature | Identification Method | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Glass transition** $T_g$ | **Step change** in baseline (no peak) | Onset / midpoint / endset of step | Onset of long-range segmental motion, $C_p$ increases |
| (ii) | **Cold crystallization** $T_c$ | **Exothermic peak** pointing **down** | Peak apex temperature | Amorphous material crystallizes upon heating |
| (iii) | **Melting** $T_m$ | **Endothermic peak** pointing **up** | Onset of peak (intersection method) | Latent heat of fusion absorbed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the "peak direction" for endo/exo. Memorize: **ENDOtherm = going UP** (heat goes IN); **EXOtherm = going DOWN** (heat goes OUT).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **DSC — Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute exam prep):**

- **DSC measures $\frac{dQ}{dt}$** — the differential heat flow between sample and reference under a controlled temperature program.
- **Two main designs:** Heat-flux DSC (one furnace, measures $\Delta T \times K$) and Power-compensation DSC (two furnaces, measures $\Delta P$ directly).
- **Master equation:** $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = K \cdot \Delta T$ (heat-flux type).
- **Peak area gives enthalpy:** $\Delta H = \dfrac{A_{\text{peak}}}{\beta \cdot m}$ — this is the single most-tested formula.
- **Crystallinity formula:** $\%X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100$.
- **Sign convention (IUPAC):** Endotherm UP, Exotherm DOWN.
- **Glass transition = step in baseline**, NOT a peak. It is a second-order transition characterized by $\Delta C_p$.
- **Melting = endothermic peak UP**; peak **onset** (not apex) gives the true $T_m$.
- **Crystallization = exothermic peak DOWN**; peak apex gives $T_c$.
- **Indium standard:** $T_m = 156.6\,^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta H_f = 28.45\,\text{J/g}$ — used for both temperature and enthalpy calibration.
- **Sapphire standard** is used for $C_p$ measurement (three-run ratio method).
- **Heating rate** $\beta$ is given in $^\circ\text{C/min}$ — convert to $^\circ\text{C/s}$ when using the $\Delta H$ formula in SI.
- **Purge gas** — N₂ for inert atmosphere, O₂ for oxidation studies (OOT/OIT).
- **Kissinger equation** $\ln(\beta / T_p^2) = -E_a/(R T_p) + \text{const}$ — used for curing/crystallization kinetics.
- **High-sensitivity applications** (pharma, biology) prefer power-compensation DSC.
- **Module 3 cross-link:** DSC is often paired with **TGA** (Module 3 next sub-topic) — TGA gives mass change, DSC gives energy change; together they characterize the full thermal behaviour of a material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Note (2024 Scheme):** For 14-mark questions on DSC, students must include **(i) labelled block diagram, (ii) derivation of $\frac{dQ}{dt} = K \Delta T$, (iii) a worked numerical example, and (iv) a thermogram with all transitions correctly identified.** Skipping any one of these will result in a 2–3 mark deduction. Always state units explicitly — heat flow in mW, $\Delta H$ in J/g, temperature in °C.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermo-gravimetric Analysis (TGA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermo-gravimetric Analysis (TGA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermo-gravimetric Analysis (TGA)** is a branch of thermal analysis techniques in which the **mass of a sample is measured as a function of temperature or time** while the sample is subjected to a controlled temperature program (heating, cooling, or isothermal holding) under a specified atmosphere (inert, oxidizing, or reducing).

According to the **International Confederation for Thermal Analysis and Calorimetry (ICTAC)** nomenclature, TGA records the mass change $(m)$ against temperature $(T)$ or time $(t)$, producing a **thermogravimetric (TG) curve**. The first derivative of this curve with respect to temperature is known as the **Derivative Thermogravimetric (DTG) curve**, given by:

$$\frac{dm}{dT} = f(T)$$

The technique is governed by the standard test methods such as **ASTM E1131** (compositional analysis by TGA) and **ISO 11358** (polymer TGA).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Baking a Cake" Model**

Imagine placing a small piece of butter on a hot pan and tracking how it disappears over time as it melts, evaporates, and finally burns into a black residue. If you had a super-precise weighing scale that recorded the weight every second while the pan's temperature was gradually increased, you would generate a curve very similar to a TGA plot.

- The **x-axis (temperature)** is like "how hot is the pan getting?"
- The **y-axis (mass)** tells you "how much of the original butter is still here?"
- When the butter evaporates, the curve **drops sharply** — this is called a **mass-loss step**.
- The black char left behind is the **residual mass** or **char yield**.

**In a laboratory setting**, instead of butter, we analyze polymers, ceramics, metals, pharmaceuticals, or composites to understand their **thermal stability, decomposition behavior, moisture content, filler content, and oxidative resistance**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The key standard metrics tracked in every TGA experiment are:

- **Onset Temperature ($T_{onset}$)** — the temperature at which the mass loss begins to be detected.
- **Inflection Temperature ($T_{inflection}$)** — the temperature at the steepest point of mass loss (maximum rate).
- **Final Temperature ($T_f$)** — the temperature at which the mass loss step ends.
- **Residual Mass ($m_{res}$)** — the mass remaining after the experiment, often expressed as a percentage.
- **Char Yield (%)** — residual mass at high temperature under inert atmosphere.
- **Ash Content (%)** — residual mass under oxidizing atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Atmosphere Options:** Nitrogen ($N_2$, inert), Argon ($Ar$, inert), Air / Oxygen ($O_2$, oxidizing), and Hydrogen ($H_2$, reducing) — typically maintained at a flow rate of **20–100 mL/min**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A TGA instrument, also called a **thermobalance**, operates through the following structured sequence:

1. **Sample Loading**: A small, precisely weighed sample (typically **1–20 mg**) is placed on a high-precision microbalance pan (platinum, alumina, or quartz).
2. **Atmosphere Control**: A purge gas (inert or reactive) is introduced at a controlled flow rate to prevent unwanted reactions and to sweep decomposition products.
3. **Temperature Program**: The furnace follows a predetermined thermal program:
   - **Dynamic (Ramp) mode**: Linear heating at a constant rate (e.g., **10 °C/min**).
   - **Isothermal mode**: Temperature held constant over time.
   - **Stepwise mode**: Held at successive temperatures with ramps in between.
4. **Continuous Mass Recording**: The microbalance records the sample mass as a function of temperature/time with a sensitivity of typically **0.1 μg to 1 μg**.
5. **Data Plotting**: The TG curve is generated: mass (%) versus temperature (°C) or time (min).
6. **DTG Computation**: The derivative $\frac{dm}{dT}$ is computed mathematically to identify the precise temperature of maximum degradation rate.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why a microbalance?** Because mass changes in TGA experiments are usually very small (micrograms), requiring ultra-sensitive weighing.
- **Why a purge gas?** To maintain a clean, reproducible environment and remove volatile decomposition products (preventing re-condensation on the sample or balance).
- **Why a controlled heating rate?** Because degradation kinetics depend on the heating rate — slower rates allow equilibrium, faster rates show kinetic effects.
- **Why DTG?** It provides sharp, easily identifiable peaks corresponding to each mass-loss event, dramatically improving resolution.

### 2.3 Types of TGA Experiments

| **Type** | **Description** | **Typical Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic TGA** | Sample heated at a constant rate; mass vs. temperature recorded | Decomposition profiling, thermal stability comparison |
| **Isothermal TGA** | Sample held at constant temperature; mass vs. time recorded | Long-term stability, oxidation kinetics, lifetime prediction |
| **Static TGA** | Sample heated in a closed system with no gas flow | Reversible reactions, equilibrium studies |
| **Hi-Res TGA** | Heating rate slows near mass-loss events to improve resolution | Overlapping degradation events |
| **Modulated TGA (MTGA®)** | Superimposes sinusoidal temperature oscillations on linear ramp | Separating overlapping transitions |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formulas, definitions, and key metrics for KTU examination preparation.**

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass Loss | $\Delta m = m_i - m_f$ | mg or % | Total mass lost over a step |
| Percentage Mass Loss | $\% \Delta m = \left(\dfrac{\Delta m}{m_i}\right) \times 100$ | % | Normalized measure of decomposition |
| Residual Mass | $m_{res} = m_f$ | mg | Char yield or ash content |
| Char Yield | $CY = \left(\dfrac{m_{res}}{m_{initial}}\right) \times 100$ | % | Carbonaceous residue under inert atmosphere |
| Ash Content | $AC = \left(\dfrac{m_{final,oxidative}}{m_{initial}}\right) \times 100$ | % | Inorganic residue under $O_2$/air |
| DTG (Derivative) | $DTG = \dfrac{dm}{dT}$ | mg/°C or %/°C | Rate of mass change |
| Heating Rate | $\beta = \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | °C/min | Controls the kinetics |
| Activation Energy (Kissinger) | $\ln\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_p^2}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R T_p} + \text{const.}$ | — | Slope gives $E_a$ |
| Activation Energy (Ozawa) | $\log \beta = -0.4567 \dfrac{E_a}{RT} + \text{const.}$ | — | Iso-conversional method |
| Arrhenius Rate | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Fundamental kinetic equation |
| Onset Determination | Intersection of baseline and tangent at inflection | °C | Convention for $T_{onset}$ |

> **Constants:** Universal gas constant $R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$. All temperatures in TGA should be reported in **°C** on plots and **K** in thermodynamic equations.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

TGA is one of the most widely used characterization tools across industries:

- **Polymer Industry**: Determines thermal stability, service temperature, char yield (fire retardancy), and filler content.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Drug-excipient compatibility, polymorphism, hydrate/solvate analysis, residual solvent quantification.
- **Ceramics & Inorganics**: Precursor decomposition, sintering behavior, oxide formation.
- **Metallurgy**: Oxidation kinetics, corrosion studies, high-temperature phase stability.
- **Composites**: Fiber content determination (ASTM D3171), degradation studies.
- **Environmental Science**: Coal analysis, biomass pyrolysis, waste characterization.
- **Forensic Science**: Identification of polymers, paints, and inks from trace evidence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Kissinger Equation for Activation Energy from TGA Data

The Kissinger method is a **peak-temperature method** used to determine the activation energy $(E_a)$ of a degradation process from DTG peak temperatures at multiple heating rates.

**Starting Point — General Kinetic Equation:**

The rate of a solid-state reaction under non-isothermal TGA conditions is given by:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot f(\alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ is the fractional conversion, $f(\alpha)$ is the reaction model, and $k(T)$ follows the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$k(T) = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 1: Substitute the Arrhenius equation into the kinetic expression:**

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \, e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot f(\alpha)$$

**Step 2: Convert to a function of temperature using heating rate $\beta = dT/dt$:**

Multiplying both sides by $\dfrac{dt}{dT} = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dT} = \frac{A}{\beta} \, e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot f(\alpha)$$

**Step 3: The DTG peak corresponds to the maximum of $d\alpha/dT$.**

At the peak temperature $T_p$, the second derivative is zero:

$$\frac{d^2 \alpha}{dT^2} \bigg|_{T = T_p} = 0$$

**Step 4: Differentiate the rate equation with respect to T and apply the maximum condition.**

The differential form is:

$$\frac{d^2\alpha}{dT^2} = \frac{A}{\beta}\left[\frac{E_a}{RT^2} e^{-E_a/RT} f(\alpha) + e^{-E_a/RT} \frac{df(\alpha)}{dT}\right]$$

For an $n$-th order reaction, $f(\alpha) = (1-\alpha)^n$, and after simplification (assuming the standard Kissinger approximations):

$$\frac{E_a \beta}{R T_p^2} = A \, n \, (1-\alpha_p)^{n-1} \, e^{-E_a / R T_p}$$

**Step 5: Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^2}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R T_p} + \ln\left(\frac{A \, R}{E_a}\right)$$

This is the **Kissinger Equation**:

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^2}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R T_p} + \text{constant}}$$

**Step 6: Practical Use — Linear Plot:**

Run TGA experiments at multiple heating rates $\beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3, \ldots$ (e.g., 5, 10, 15, 20 °C/min). For each rate, identify the DTG peak temperature $T_{p,i}$.

Plot $\ln\left(\dfrac{\beta_i}{T_{p,i}^2}\right)$ on the **y-axis** vs. $\dfrac{1}{T_{p,i}}$ on the **x-axis**.

A straight line is obtained. The slope is $-\dfrac{E_a}{R}$, so:

$$E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:**
> - **Why we differentiate the rate equation**: To locate the maximum — the condition defining $T_p$.
> - **Why we take logarithm**: The Arrhenius exponential becomes linear, enabling graphical determination of $E_a$.
> - **Why we vary $\beta$**: The peak position $T_p$ shifts with heating rate; this shift encodes the kinetic information.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A polymer is subjected to TGA at four heating rates. The corresponding DTG peak temperatures are:

| Heating Rate $\beta$ (°C/min) | $T_p$ (K) | $\beta / T_p^2$ | $\ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ | $1/T_p$ (K⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5   | 623 | $1.288 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-11.260$ | $1.605 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 10  | 643 | $2.418 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-10.628$ | $1.555 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 15  | 658 | $3.466 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-10.270$ | $1.520 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 20  | 670 | $4.453 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-10.020$ | $1.493 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**Step 1: Compute $\beta / T_p^2$ for the first row:**

$$\frac{\beta_1}{T_{p,1}^2} = \frac{5}{(623)^2} = \frac{5}{388129} = 1.288 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2: Take natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(1.288 \times 10^{-5}) = -11.260$$

**Step 3: Compute $1/T_p$:**

$$\frac{1}{623} = 1.605 \times 10^{-3} \; K^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Linear regression of $y = \ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ vs. $x = 1/T_p$:**

The slope is determined from the linear fit. For our four data points, the slope $\approx -11000$ K.

**Step 5: Calculate $E_a$:**

$$E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times R = -(-11000) \times 8.314 \; J/mol$$

$$E_a = 91{,}454 \; J/mol \approx 91.5 \; kJ/mol$$

This value is typical for the thermal degradation activation energy of many engineering polymers such as polyethylene or polypropylene.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common TGA Curve Features

| **Curve Feature** | **Physical Meaning** | **Engineering Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| Initial flat region (room T to ~100 °C) | No mass change | Sample is thermally stable in this range |
| Small mass loss at ~100 °C | Loss of adsorbed moisture or residual solvent | Drying step; useful for moisture content |
| Sharp mass loss step | Major decomposition event (e.g., polymer chain scission) | Service temperature limit |
| Plateau between steps | Stable intermediate phase | Formation of a thermally stable intermediate |
| Second sharp loss | Further decomposition of intermediate | Multiple degradation mechanisms |
| Final residual mass | Char (inert) or ash (oxidative) | Inorganic content, char yield |
| DTG peak | Maximum rate of mass loss | Inflection / peak temperature |
| Shoulder on DTG peak | Overlapping processes | Multiple concurrent reactions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — TGA Instrument Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PC Control and Data Acquisition System] --> B[Microbalance Mechanism]
    B --> C[Sample Pan and Holder]
    D[Purge Gas Supply N2 or Air] --> E[Gas Flow Controller and Mass Flow Meter]
    E --> F[Furnace Assembly]
    F --> C
    G[Temperature Programmer] --> F
    F --> H[Thermocouple Sensor]
    H --> A
    B --> A
    I[Balance Housing with Inert Purge] --> B
    I --> D
    J[Output: TG Curve m vs T] --> A
    K[Output: DTG Curve dm/dT vs T] --> A
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — TGA Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Sample Preparation and Weighing] --> B[Step 2: Loading Sample onto Pan]
    B --> C[Step 3: Set Temperature Program]
    C --> D[Step 4: Set Purge Gas and Flow Rate]
    D --> E[Step 5: Initiate TGA Run]
    E --> F[Step 6: Continuous Data Acquisition]
    F --> G[Step 7: Generate TG Curve]
    G --> H[Step 8: Compute DTG Curve]
    H --> I[Step 9: Identify T onset, T peak, Residual Mass]
    I --> J[Step 10: Report and Interpret]
```

### 4.3 Typical TGA and DTG Curve — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Room Temperature: Initial Mass 100%] --> B[Moisture Loss Region: 80 to 150 C]
    B --> C[Polymer Decomposition Step: 300 to 450 C]
    C --> D[Char Residue Plateau: 30% Remaining]
    D --> E[Optional: Ash under Oxidative Atmosphere: 5% Final]
    
    F[DTG Peak 1: Moisture Loss] --> B
    G[DTG Peak 2: Main Decomposition] --> C
    H[DTG Peak 3: Char Oxidation] --> E
```

### 4.4 Comparison of TGA Modes — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TGA Mode Selection] --> B[Dynamic Mode: Constant Heating Rate]
    A --> C[Isothermal Mode: Constant Temperature over Time]
    A --> D[Stepwise Mode: Multiple Temperature Plateaus]
    A --> E[Modulated TGA: Sinusoidal Temperature Overlay]
    
    B --> B1[Output: Mass vs Temperature]
    C --> C1[Output: Mass vs Time]
    D --> D1[Output: Multi-step Decomposition Profile]
    E --> E1[Output: Separated Reversible and Non-Reversible Events]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model QP]**
**Define Thermo-gravimetric Analysis (TGA). Mention any two applications of TGA.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Thermo-gravimetric Analysis (TGA) is a thermal analysis technique in which the mass of a sample is continuously recorded as a function of temperature or time, while the sample is subjected to a controlled temperature program in a specified atmosphere.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Determination of thermal stability and decomposition temperature** of polymers, ceramics, and pharmaceuticals.
2. **Compositional analysis** of materials, such as determining filler, ash, or moisture content in composites and coal.

(Any two valid applications receive full credit.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model QP]**
**What is a DTG curve? How is it obtained from a TGA curve?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Derivative Thermogravimetric (DTG) curve** is the first derivative of the TGA mass signal with respect to temperature (or time), representing the **rate of mass change** as a function of temperature.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$DTG = \frac{dm}{dT}$$

**How it is obtained:**
The DTG curve is obtained by **mathematically differentiating the original TG curve** with respect to temperature. Modern TGA instruments compute the DTG signal in real time using digital signal processing, displaying peaks at points where the mass loss rate is maximum (inflection points of the TG curve).

**Significance:** The DTG curve is especially useful in resolving overlapping mass-loss events that appear as a single step in the TG curve.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Thermo-gravimetric Analysis with a neat block diagram of the instrument. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample was subjected to TGA at four different heating rates. The DTG peak temperatures are given below. Determine the activation energy using the Kissinger method. **(7 marks)**

| Heating Rate $\beta$ (°C/min) | Peak Temperature $T_p$ (K) |
|---|---|
| 5   | 613 |
| 10  | 633 |
| 15  | 648 |
| 20  | 660 |

Take $R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$.

---

**Model Solution for Q3(a):**

**Principle of TGA:**
TGA is based on the principle of measuring the **change in mass of a material as a function of temperature or time** under a controlled atmosphere. When a sample is heated, it may undergo dehydration, decomposition, oxidation, or other reactions that change its mass. The technique quantitatively records these changes.

**Block Diagram and Components:**

| **Component** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| **Microbalance** | High-precision weighing of the sample (sensitivity 0.1 μg to 1 μg); continuously tracks mass changes. |
| **Sample Pan and Holder** | Holds the sample; typically made of platinum, alumina, or quartz for chemical inertness. |
| **Furnace** | Provides controlled heating with precisely programmed temperature ramps. |
| **Temperature Controller / Programmer** | Sets and maintains the temperature program (linear, isothermal, stepwise). |
| **Thermocouple** | Monitors the actual sample or furnace temperature for accurate T data. |
| **Purge Gas System** | Supplies inert ($N_2$, $Ar$) or reactive (air, $O_2$) gas to control the atmosphere and sweep volatiles. |
| **Data Acquisition System (PC)** | Records mass and temperature signals, plots TG and DTG curves. |
| **Balance Housing** | Thermally isolates and protects the balance from furnace heat and gas flows. |

**Procedure:** A small sample (1–20 mg) is placed on the balance pan. The furnace is heated at a programmed rate under a flowing purge gas. The mass is continuously recorded, and the resulting TG curve plots mass (%) versus temperature (°C).

---

**Model Solution for Q3(b):**

**Step 1: Recall the Kissinger Equation:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^2}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R T_p} + \text{constant}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $\beta / T_p^2$ and $\ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ for each heating rate:**

For $\beta = 5$ °C/min, $T_p = 613$ K:

$$\frac{\beta}{T_p^2} = \frac{5}{613^2} = \frac{5}{375769} = 1.330 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\ln(1.330 \times 10^{-5}) = -11.227$$

For $\beta = 10$ °C/min, $T_p = 633$ K:

$$\frac{\beta}{T_p^2} = \frac{10}{633^2} = \frac{10}{400689} = 2.496 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\ln(2.496 \times 10^{-5}) = -10.598$$

For $\beta = 15$ °C/min, $T_p = 648$ K:

$$\frac{\beta}{T_p^2} = \frac{15}{648^2} = \frac{15}{419904} = 3.572 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\ln(3.572 \times 10^{-5}) = -10.239$$

For $\beta = 20$ °C/min, $T_p = 660$ K:

$$\frac{\beta}{T_p^2} = \frac{20}{660^2} = \frac{20}{435600} = 4.591 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\ln(4.591 \times 10^{-5}) = -9.987$$

**Step 3: Calculate $1/T_p$ for each:**

$$\frac{1}{613} = 1.631 \times 10^{-3} \; K^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{633} = 1.580 \times 10^{-3} \; K^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{648} = 1.543 \times 10^{-3} \; K^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{660} = 1.515 \times 10^{-3} \; K^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Plot $\ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ vs. $1/T_p$ and determine the slope.**

Tabulated values:

| $\beta$ (°C/min) | $T_p$ (K) | $1/T_p$ (K⁻¹) | $\ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5   | 613 | $1.631 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-11.227$ |
| 10  | 633 | $1.580 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-10.598$ |
| 15  | 648 | $1.543 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-10.239$ |
| 20  | 660 | $1.515 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-9.987$ |

Linear regression gives a slope $\approx -10700$ K.

**Step 5: Calculate Activation Energy:**

$$E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times R = -(-10700) \times 8.314 \; J/mol$$

$$E_a = 88{,}960 \; J/mol \approx 89.0 \; kJ/mol$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $T_p$ to Kelvin** — T must always be in K when used in $E_a$ calculations. Mixing °C and K is a frequent mistake.
> 2. **Incorrect $T_p^2$ calculation** — Square the Kelvin temperature, not the original value from the instrument display (which may be in °C).
> 3. **Unit errors in $E_a$** — Always quote $E_a$ in **kJ/mol** for consistency with literature.
> 4. **Plot slope sign confusion** — The slope of the Kissinger plot is **negative**; $E_a$ is **positive** (note the negative sign in the equation).
> 5. **Improper mass loss interpretation** — Always report the temperature range (onset to final), not just the peak.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the various types of TGA experiments (dynamic, isothermal, stepwise) with suitable examples. Compare the resulting curves qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A calcium oxalate monohydrate ($CaC_2O_4 \cdot H_2O$) sample weighing 12.50 mg is subjected to TGA in air. The following stepwise mass losses are observed:

| Step | Temperature Range (°C) | Final Mass (mg) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 – 250 | 10.81 |
| 2 | 350 – 500 | 7.81 |
| 3 | 650 – 850 | 3.50 |

Identify the chemical reaction associated with each step and calculate the theoretical mass losses. Compare with observed values. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q4(a):**

**1. Dynamic TGA:**
- The sample is heated at a **constant linear heating rate** (e.g., 5, 10, 20 °C/min).
- The TG curve plots **mass vs. temperature**.
- **Example:** Studying the thermal degradation of a polymer to determine its decomposition temperature.
- **Curve feature:** Sharp, well-defined mass-loss steps; onset shifts to higher T with increasing $\beta$.

**2. Isothermal TGA:**
- The sample is held at a **constant temperature** and mass is recorded vs. **time**.
- **Example:** Long-term oxidative stability of a polymer at 200 °C.
- **Curve feature:** The TG curve shows an exponential-like decay with time, useful for kinetic studies.

**3. Stepwise TGA:**
- The temperature is increased in **discrete steps** with holding periods.
- **Example:** Quantitative separation of overlapping decomposition events.
- **Curve feature:** A staircase-like pattern, useful for resolving closely-spaced events.

**Comparison:**

| **Mode** | **X-axis** | **Best For** | **Information** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic | Temperature | Decomposition profile | Onset, peak, residual mass |
| Isothermal | Time | Long-term stability, lifetime | Kinetic constants |
| Stepwise | Temperature with hold | Resolving overlapping events | Composition analysis |

---

**Model Solution for Q4(b):**

**Theoretical Mass Loss Calculations for $CaC_2O_4 \cdot H_2O$:**

Molar masses (g/mol): $Ca = 40.08$, $C = 12.01$, $O = 16.00$, $H = 1.008$

$$M(CaC_2O_4 \cdot H_2O) = 40.08 + 2(12.01) + 4(16.00) + 18.02 = 146.12 \; g/mol$$

$$M(CaC_2O_4) = 128.10 \; g/mol$$

$$M(CaCO_3) = 100.09 \; g/mol$$

$$M(CaO) = 56.08 \; g/mol$$

**Step 1: 100 – 250 °C — Loss of water of crystallization:**

$$CaC_2O_4 \cdot H_2O \longrightarrow CaC_2O_4 + H_2O \uparrow$$

Theoretical mass loss:

$$\Delta m_1 = \frac{18.02}{146.12} \times 12.50 = 1.54 \; mg$$

Theoretical remaining mass:

$$m_1 = 12.50 - 1.54 = 10.96 \; mg$$

**Observed:** 10.81 mg — **good agreement** with theoretical 10.96 mg.

**Step 2: 350 – 500 °C — Decomposition to calcium carbonate:**

$$CaC_2O_4 \longrightarrow CaCO_3 + CO \uparrow$$

Theoretical mass loss:

$$\Delta m_2 = \frac{28.01}{146.12} \times 12.50 = 2.40 \; mg$$

Theoretical remaining mass:

$$m_2 = 10.96 - 2.40 = 8.56 \; mg$$

**Observed:** 7.81 mg — within reasonable experimental error; small deviations arise from buoyancy effects and instrument calibration.

**Step 3: 650 – 850 °C — Decomposition to calcium oxide:**

$$CaCO_3 \longrightarrow CaO + CO_2 \uparrow$$

Theoretical mass loss:

$$\Delta m_3 = \frac{44.01}{146.12} \times 12.50 = 3.77 \; mg$$

Theoretical final mass:

$$m_3 = 8.56 - 3.77 = 4.79 \; mg$$

**Observed:** 3.50 mg — small discrepancy, likely due to incomplete decomposition or sample inhomogeneity; theoretical CaO yield is 4.79 mg.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Q4(b) Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the water of crystallization** in the molar mass — many students use only $CaC_2O_4$ as the starting formula and lose marks on Step 1.
> 2. **Mixing up the gases evolved** — $CO$ is released in step 2, $CO_2$ in step 3.
> 3. **Rounding errors** in molar mass; use $M(O) = 16.00$ exactly for clarity.
> 4. **Not stating the atmosphere** — TGA of $CaC_2O_4$ in **nitrogen** gives $CaC_2O_4 \to CaCO_3 \to CaO$, while in **air** the final product is $CaO$. Both pathways should be discussed.
> 5. **Forgetting to compare observed vs. theoretical** — always comment on agreement or deviation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Essential for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep**

- **TGA** = mass versus temperature (or time) under controlled atmosphere.
- **TG curve** plots **% mass** vs. **T (°C)**; **DTG curve** plots **dm/dT** vs. **T**.
- **Sample size:** typically **1–20 mg**; **balance sensitivity:** **0.1 μg**.
- **Atmospheres:** $N_2$ / $Ar$ (inert, for char yield) and $O_2$ / air (oxidative, for ash).
- **Onset, Peak (Inflection), and Final temperatures** define each mass-loss step.
- **Kissinger method** gives $E_a$ from slope of $\ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ vs. $1/T_p$.
- **Five types of TGA**: dynamic, isothermal, static, hi-resolution, modulated.
- **Instrument components**: microbalance, furnace, sample holder, purge gas system, thermocouple, data system.
- **Common applications**: thermal stability, char yield, ash content, composition, decomposition kinetics.
- **Standard norms**: ASTM E1131, ISO 11358, ICTAC nomenclature.
- **Effect of heating rate:** Higher $\beta$ → higher apparent decomposition temperature; peaks shift to higher T.
- **Buoyancy effect:** causes an apparent mass gain at low T; must be corrected with a blank run.
- **DTG advantage:** Resolves overlapping events and identifies weak shoulders.
- **Always use Kelvin** in kinetic equations like Kissinger, Ozawa, and Arrhenius.
- **Report** all results with sample identity, mass, atmosphere, heating rate, and reference standards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Characterization Techniques: Electrical Resistivity in Bulk and Thin Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical Resistivity in Bulk and Thin Films

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Electrical Resistivity

**Electrical resistivity** ($\rho$) is a fundamental intrinsic material property that quantifies how strongly a given material opposes the flow of electric current. It is defined as the resistance of a unit cube of material (1 m × 1 m × 1 m) measured between opposite faces, and is mathematically expressed through **Ohm's Law** in its microscopic form:

$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E} = \frac{1}{\rho} \vec{E}$$

where $\vec{J}$ is the current density (A/m²), $\vec{E}$ is the electric field (V/m), and $\sigma$ is the electrical conductivity (S/m). The **inverse relationship** between resistivity and conductivity ($\rho = 1/\sigma$) is one of the most frequently tested concepts in KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to distinguish between **bulk resistivity** (a 3D volumetric property, $\Omega\cdot$m) and **sheet resistance** (a 2D surface property of thin films, $\Omega/\square$ or $\Omega/sq$). Conflating these two is a guaranteed loss-of-marks trap.

### Bulk vs. Thin-Film Resistivity — A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Bulk Material | Thin Film |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Three-dimensional slab | Two-dimensional layer on substrate |
| Measured Quantity | Resistivity $\rho$ ($\Omega\cdot$m) | Sheet resistance $R_s$ ($\Omega/\square$) |
| Dominant Scattering | Phonon, impurity, grain boundary | Surface scattering, interface scattering, grain boundary (size effect) |
| Typical Thickness | mm to cm range | nm to $\mu$m range |
| Measurement Method | Two-probe / Four-probe on bulk | Four-probe or Van der Pauw on thin film |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are watering your garden with a pipe:

- A **wide, short pipe** allows water to flow easily → low resistance.
- A **narrow, long pipe** restricts water flow → high resistance.
- The **resistivity** is an intrinsic property of the pipe material itself — it does not depend on the pipe's shape. A copper pipe and an iron pipe of the *same* dimensions will still conduct water (i.e., current) very differently.

Similarly, a material with **low resistivity** (like copper: $\rho_{Cu} \approx 1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m) is a "wide pipe" for electrons, while a material with **high resistivity** (like quartz: $\rho \approx 10^{16}\ \Omega\cdot$m) is a "narrow, blocked pipe."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thin-Film Intuition:** A thin film is like spreading butter on a slice of bread. The bread (substrate) is insulating, and only the thin butter layer (film) carries current. So we don't measure "resistance per length" anymore — we measure **resistance per square**, regardless of the square's actual size. This is the origin of the curious unit $\Omega/\square$.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Resistivity of Copper at 20 °C:** $1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m
- **Resistivity of Intrinsic Silicon:** $\approx 2.3 \times 10^{3}\ \Omega\cdot$m
- **Resistivity of Ge (intrinsic):** $\approx 0.46\ \Omega\cdot$m
- **Sheet resistance unit:** $\Omega/\square$ (ohm per square), dimensionally $\Omega$
- **Conversion factor:** $R_s = \rho / t$ where $t$ is the film thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — From Ohm's Law to Measurement

The flow of electrons through a conductor is governed by three interlinked equations. Understanding *why* each step is taken is critical to answering KTU's "explain with reasoning" type questions.

### Step 1: Macroscopic Ohm's Law
For a uniform bar of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$:
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$
This relation tells us resistance depends on **geometry** (L, A) and **material** ($\rho$).

### Step 2: The Motivation for Four-Probe Measurement
In a two-probe setup, the same leads are used to inject current and measure voltage. This means the measured resistance $R_{measured} = R_{sample} + 2R_{contact} + 2R_{lead}$. For **high-resistivity** samples or **thin films**, contact resistance can dominate and corrupt the reading.

### Step 3: The Four-Probe Solution
Separate the current-carrying probes (outer) from the voltage-sensing probes (inner). Since the voltmeter draws negligible current, the voltage drop is measured *only* across the sample segment — **no contact-resistance error**.

### Step 4: Sheet Resistance for Thin Films
For a thin film of thickness $t$, define:
$$R_s = \frac{\rho}{t} \quad \text{(units: } \Omega/\square\text{)}$$
The "$\square$" symbol is dimensionless and indicates that for a *square* geometry of any size, the resistance between opposite edges is simply $R_s$.

### Step 5: Van der Pauw Theorem
For an arbitrarily shaped, thin, flat sample of uniform thickness with four small ohmic contacts at arbitrary points on the periphery, the resistivity is given by the **Van der Pauw equation**:
$$\exp\left(-\frac{\pi d}{\rho} R_{AB,CD}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{\pi d}{\rho} R_{BC,DA}\right) = 1$$
where $R_{AB,CD}$ is the resistance measured by passing current from A to B and sensing voltage from C to D, and $d$ is the film thickness. For a **symmetric sample** (square or circle), $R_{AB,CD} = R_{BC,DA} = R_s$, and the equation simplifies to $\rho = (\pi d / \ln 2) \cdot R_s$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Problem Solving)

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary/Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Resistance of uniform bar | $\Omega$ | Bulk, uniform cross-section |
| 2 | $R_s = \rho / t$ | Sheet resistance | $\Omega/\square$ | Thin film, $t \ll$ lateral dimensions |
| 3 | $\rho = 2\pi s \cdot (V/I)$ | Four-probe (collinear, on infinite half-space) | $\Omega\cdot$m | Sample thickness $\gg$ probe spacing $s$ |
| 4 | $\rho = 2\pi s \cdot F \cdot (V/I)$ | Four-probe with correction factor | $\Omega\cdot$m | Finite sample; $F$ depends on geometry |
| 5 | $\rho = (\pi d / \ln 2) R_s$ | Van der Pauw (symmetric) | $\Omega\cdot$m | $d$ = thickness, square/circular sample |
| 6 | $R_s = (\pi / \ln 2) \cdot (V/I)_{avg}$ | Van der Pauw sheet resistance | $\Omega/\square$ | $R_{AB,CD} = R_{BC,DA}$ |
| 7 | $\sigma = nq\mu$ | Conductivity from carrier concentration | S/m | $n$ = carrier density, $\mu$ = mobility |
| 8 | $R_H = V_H t / (IB)$ | Hall coefficient | m³/C | Used to find carrier type and density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipe symbol rule:** In the table above and throughout these notes, the symbol $\vert$ is *never* written as a bare pipe. It is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Semiconductor Industry:** Sheet resistance is the *primary quality-control metric* for doped wafers and interconnects. Process engineers measure $R_s$ to monitor ion-implant dose uniformity.
2. **Thin-Film Solar Cells:** TCO (transparent conducting oxide) layers like ITO require $R_s < 10\ \Omega/\square$ for efficient current collection.
3. **Microelectronics:** Copper interconnect resistance in ICs is calculated from $\rho_{Cu}$ and the line cross-section. Any deviation signals electromigration failure.
4. **MEMS / Sensors:** Resistive gas sensors and piezoresistive pressure sensors are characterized by their $\rho$ vs. temperature and strain response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sheet Resistance from First Principles

We begin with the standard expression for resistance of a rectangular bar:
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

For a thin film of thickness $t$, width $W$, and length $L$, the cross-sectional area through which current flows is $A = W \cdot t$. Substituting:
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{W \cdot t}$$

We now group the geometric terms to extract the "intrinsic" film property. Rewriting:
$$R = \left(\frac{\rho}{t}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{W}\right)$$

Define **sheet resistance** $R_s = \rho / t$, which is a property of the film material and its thickness only. The ratio $L/W$ is the number of squares that the current must traverse. Therefore:
$$\boxed{R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W} = R_s \cdot N_{\square}}$$

where $N_{\square}$ is the dimensionless **number of squares**. This is why $R_s$ is reported in $\Omega/\square$ — multiplying by the count of squares gives the total resistance.

> **Conversion check:** If a 1 cm × 1 cm film has $R = 10\ \Omega$ and $t = 100$ nm = $10^{-7}$ m, then $R_s = 10\ \Omega/\square$ and $\rho = R_s \cdot t = 10 \times 10^{-7} = 10^{-6}\ \Omega\cdot$m.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Four-Probe Resistivity Formula (Infinite Sample Case)

Consider four collinear probes (1, 2, 3, 4) equally spaced at distance $s$ on a semi-infinite sample. A current $I$ is passed through the outer probes (1 and 4), and the voltage $V$ is measured between the inner probes (2 and 3).

**Step 1:** Model the current injection at probe 1 as a point source. By spherical symmetry, the current density at distance $r$ is:
$$J(r) = \frac{I}{2\pi r^2}$$

**Step 2:** The electric field in the material is:
$$E(r) = \rho \cdot J(r) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r^2}$$

**Step 3:** The potential at any point P at distance $r$ from the source is:
$$V(r) = -\int_{\infty}^{r} E \, dr' = \int_{r}^{\infty} \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (r')^2} dr' = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 4:** The total potential at probe 2 (at distance $s$ from probe 1) is the **superposition** of contributions from both current source (probe 1) and sink (probe 4):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2s)} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s}\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
V_3 &= \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2s)} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} = -\frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} \cdot \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** The measured voltage is $V = V_2 - V_3$:
$$V = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} \cdot \frac{1}{2} - \left(-\frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s}$$

**Step 6:** Solving for resistivity:
$$\boxed{\rho = 2\pi s \cdot \frac{V}{I}}$$

This is the famous **four-probe formula** for an infinitely thick, laterally infinite sample. For finite samples, a **geometric correction factor** $F$ is introduced:
$$\rho = 2\pi s \cdot F \cdot \frac{V}{I}$$

where $F$ depends on the sample's thickness-to-spacing ratio ($t/s$) and lateral extent, and is read from standard ASTM tables (F 84).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Van der Pauw Symmetric Case

The Van der Pauw equation is:
$$\exp\left(-\frac{\pi d R_{AB,CD}}{\rho}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{\pi d R_{BC,DA}}{\rho}\right) = 1$$

For a **symmetric** sample (e.g., a square or circle with symmetric contact placement), the two characteristic resistances are equal:
$$R_{AB,CD} = R_{BC,DA} \equiv R_s$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the equal-resistance condition:
$$2 \exp\left(-\frac{\pi d R_s}{\rho}\right) = 1$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the exponential:
$$\exp\left(-\frac{\pi d R_s}{\rho}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$-\frac{\pi d R_s}{\rho} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2$$

**Step 4:** Solve for resistivity:
$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{\pi d}{\ln 2} \cdot R_s \approx 4.5324 \cdot d \cdot R_s}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back $R_s = \rho / d$ to verify consistency:
$$\rho = \frac{\pi d}{\ln 2} \cdot \frac{\rho}{d} = \frac{\pi}{\ln 2} \rho \quad \text{✓ (identity holds)}$$

The numerical factor $\pi / \ln 2 \approx 4.5324$ is **unique** to the Van der Pauw method and is one of the highest-yield constants in the KTU Module 3 syllabus.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Sample Type | Recommended Method | Key Advantage | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk, large, thick bar | Four-probe (collinear) | Simple, fast | Needs correction factor for finite samples |
| Bulk, irregular shape | Four-probe (with correction) | Geometric flexibility | $F$ lookup required |
| Thin film, arbitrary shape | Van der Pauw | Shape-independent | Requires 4 small contacts at periphery |
| Thin film, rectangular | Four-probe (in-line) | Easier contact layout | Edge effects need correction |
| Ultra-high resistivity | Two-probe with guard ring | Handles $\rho > 10^{10}\ \Omega\cdot$m | Slow, requires electrometer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Two-Probe vs. Four-Probe Setup

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of two-probe and four-probe measurement configurations, highlighting the separation of current injection and voltage sensing paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA[Current Source I] --> leadA1[Lead A]
    srcA --> leadA2[Lead A prime]
    smA[Sample Under Test] --> smA1
    smA1 --> r1[Contact Resistance Rc]
    r1 --> leadA1
    smA1 --> r2[Contact Resistance Rc]
    r2 --> leadA2
    vmA[Voltmeter V] --> smA1
    vmA --> smA1

    srcB[Current Source I] --> leadB1[Outer Probe 1]
    srcB --> leadB4[Outer Probe 4]
    smB[Sample Under Test] --> sp2[Inner Probe 2]
    smB --> sp3[Inner Probe 3]
    sp2 --> vmB[Voltmeter V high impedance]
    sp3 --> vmB
    vmB --> readB[Display V and I]
    readB --> calcB[Compute rho equals 2 pi s V over I]
```

**Interpretation:** In the upper (two-probe) path, the same leads carry current and sense voltage, so contact resistance $R_c$ appears in series with the sample. In the lower (four-probe) path, the voltmeter's high input impedance means virtually no current flows through the inner probes, eliminating the $R_c$ contribution from the voltage reading.

## 4.2 Functional Flow of a Van der Pauw Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Start Measurement]) --> prep[Prepare Square or Circular Sample]
    prep --> contact[Place Four Ohmic Contacts at Periphery A B C D]
    contact --> meas1[Measure Resistance RAB CD]
    meas1 --> meas2[Measure Resistance RBC DA]
    meas2 --> check{Symmetric?}
    check -->|Yes| simple[Use rho equals pi d over ln 2 times Rs]
    check -->|No| numer[Use Numerical Iteration of VdP Equation]
    simple --> out([Output Resistivity rho])
    numer --> out
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram of Measurement Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Electrical Resistivity Measurement] --> B{Bulk or Thin Film}
    B -->|Bulk| C{Bulk Sample Shape}
    C -->|Regular Bar| D[Four Probe Collinear]
    C -->|Irregular| E[Four Probe with Correction Factor F]
    B -->|Thin Film| F{Film Shape}
    F -->|Square or Circle| G[Van der Pauw Method]
    F -->|Long Strip| H[Four Probe In Line]
    F -->|Very High Resistance| I[Two Probe with Guard Ring]
    D --> J[Compute rho equals 2 pi s F V over I]
    E --> J
    G --> K[Compute rho equals pi d over ln 2 times Rs]
    H --> K
    I --> L[Compute R equals V over I]
    J --> M[Output Resistivity]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** Each block represents a *decision node* or *process step* in a typical KTU lab report flow. Examiners often ask students to "draw and explain the experimental setup" — these block diagrams translate directly into a clean, well-labelled schematic for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Sheet Resistance and State Its Significance in Thin-Film Characterization. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Sheet resistance $R_s$ is defined as the resistivity of a thin film divided by its thickness:
$$R_s = \frac{\rho}{t}$$

It is measured in $\Omega/\square$ (ohm per square) and represents the resistance of a *square* segment of the film regardless of its actual size. **Significance:**

1. It is the **primary quality metric** for thin films in semiconductor and solar-cell industries.
2. It is **shape-independent** — the same $R_s$ value applies whether the film is 1 mm² or 1 cm², provided thickness is uniform.
3. It enables quick comparison of films from different deposition runs.

*(Expected length: 3–4 sentences. Examiners award the third mark specifically for a real-world application.)*

### Question 2: Why Is a Four-Probe Method Preferred Over a Two-Probe Method for Resistivity Measurement? [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

In a two-probe setup, the same leads are used to pass current and measure voltage. The measured resistance therefore includes:
$$R_{measured} = R_{sample} + 2R_{contact} + 2R_{lead}$$

For thin films and high-resistivity semiconductors, $R_{contact}$ can be comparable to or larger than $R_{sample}$, leading to **erroneous readings**.

In a four-probe method, current is passed through the outer two probes, and voltage is sensed by the inner two probes connected to a high-impedance voltmeter. Since the voltmeter draws negligible current, **no voltage drop occurs across the contact resistance** of the inner probes. The formula reduces to:
$$\rho = 2\pi s \cdot F \cdot \frac{V}{I}$$

eliminating contact-resistance error and providing an accurate measurement of the *intrinsic* sample resistivity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electrical resistivity of a semi-infinite bulk sample using the four-probe collinear method. State clearly the assumptions made. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A silicon wafer of thickness $t = 500\ \mu$m is measured using a four-probe setup with equidistant probe spacing $s = 1$ mm. A current of $I = 10$ mA is passed through the outer probes, and a voltage of $V = 12.6$ mV is measured between the inner probes. The sample is large enough to assume semi-infinite geometry. Calculate the resistivity and the sheet resistance of the wafer. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Sample is **semi-infinite** (laterally large and thick compared to probe spacing $s$).
2. All four probes are **collinear and equidistant** at spacing $s$.
3. Probes are **point contacts** and **ohmic** (no rectifying junction).
4. Sample is **homogeneous and isotropic**.

**Derivation (recap from Section 3.2):**

For a current $I$ injected at probe 1, the potential at distance $r$ in a semi-infinite medium is:
$$V(r) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$

The potentials at the inner probes 2 and 3 (using superposition with current source at probe 1 and sink at probe 4):
$$V_2 = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2s)} = \frac{\rho I}{4\pi s}$$

$$V_3 = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (2s)} - \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} = -\frac{\rho I}{4\pi s}$$

Therefore:
$$V = V_2 - V_3 = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi s} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\rho = 2\pi s \cdot \frac{V}{I}}$$

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $s = 1$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m
- $I = 10$ mA $= 10^{-2}$ A
- $V = 12.6$ mV $= 12.6 \times 10^{-3}$ V
- $t = 500\ \mu$m $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- Sample is semi-infinite (so $F = 1$).

**Step 1: Compute the resistivity.**
$$\rho = 2\pi s \cdot \frac{V}{I} = 2\pi \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{12.6 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V}{I} &= \frac{12.6 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-2}} = 1.26\ \Omega \\[4pt]
\rho &= 2\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 1.26 = 7.917 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute the sheet resistance.**
$$R_s = \frac{\rho}{t} = \frac{7.917 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 15.83\ \Omega/\square$$

**Final Answer:**
- $\rho \approx 7.92 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega\cdot$m (typical for lightly doped silicon)
- $R_s \approx 15.83\ \Omega/\square$

---

### Question B: [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** State and explain the Van der Pauw theorem. Derive the simplified expression for resistivity when the sample is symmetric. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A square thin film of $n$-type silicon with $R_s = 180\ \Omega/\square$ and thickness $d = 300\ \mu$m is measured using the Van der Pauw method with symmetric contact placement. Calculate the resistivity. If Hall measurement gives $R_H = 250$ cm³/C, determine the carrier concentration and the electron mobility. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Van der Pauw Theorem Statement:**
For a flat sample of arbitrary shape and uniform thickness, with four small ohmic contacts placed on the periphery, the resistivity is given by:
$$\exp\left(-\frac{\pi d}{\rho} R_{AB,CD}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{\pi d}{\rho} R_{BC,DA}\right) = 1$$

**Derivation for symmetric case (from Section 3.3):**

When $R_{AB,CD} = R_{BC,DA} = R_s$, the equation becomes:
$$2 \exp\left(-\frac{\pi d R_s}{\rho}\right) = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \exp\left(-\frac{\pi d R_s}{\rho}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking natural log:
$$-\frac{\pi d R_s}{\rho} = -\ln 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\rho = \frac{\pi d}{\ln 2} \cdot R_s \approx 4.532 \, d \, R_s}$$

The theorem is **shape-independent**, requiring only uniform thickness and small peripheral contacts — a major advantage over the four-probe method which demands regular geometries or correction-factor tables.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $R_s = 180\ \Omega/\square$
- $d = 300\ \mu$m $= 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $R_H = 250$ cm³/C $= 250 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/C

**Step 1: Compute resistivity using Van der Pauw.**
$$\rho = \frac{\pi d}{\ln 2} \cdot R_s = 4.5324 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 180$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= 4.5324 \times 5.4 \times 10^{-2} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.2447\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute carrier concentration from Hall coefficient.**
For an $n$-type semiconductor with single-carrier conduction:
$$n = \frac{1}{q R_H} = \frac{1}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (250 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$n = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-23}} = 2.5 \times 10^{22}\ \text{m}^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Compute electron mobility.**
$$\mu = \frac{\sigma}{nq} = \frac{1}{\rho \, n \, q}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{1}{0.2447 \times 2.5 \times 10^{22} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\[4pt]
   &= \frac{1}{0.9788} \approx 1.022\ \text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s}) \\[4pt]
   &\approx 1022\ \text{cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**
- $\rho \approx 0.245\ \Omega\cdot$m
- $n \approx 2.5 \times 10^{22}\ \text{m}^{-3}$
- $\mu \approx 1022\ \text{cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 3 (Resistivity):**
> 1. **Unit confusion trap:** Writing $R_s$ in $\Omega\cdot$m instead of $\Omega/\square$ — examiners deduct 1 mark immediately because it shows conceptual confusion between bulk and thin-film properties.
> 2. **Skipping superposition:** In four-probe derivations, students often forget to include the contribution from the current *sink* (probe 4) when computing potential at probe 2. This leads to a factor-of-2 error in the final formula. Always show both terms.
> 3. **Van der Pauw simplification:** The equation $\rho = \pi d R_s / \ln 2$ is **only valid for symmetric samples**. Using it for a rectangular or asymmetric sample (even if square-shaped) without stating the symmetry assumption costs full marks in part (a) derivations.
> 4. **Correction factor omission:** For finite samples in four-probe, students frequently write $\rho = 2\pi s (V/I)$ without the $F$ term. State $F = 1$ explicitly when assuming semi-infinite geometry, or use the standard correction-factor table.
> 5. **Sign convention in Hall effect:** Forgetting that $R_H$ is negative for electrons and positive for holes. Examiners look for the carrier-type identification, not just the magnitude.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Bulk resistivity unit:** $\Omega\cdot$m. **Sheet resistance unit:** $\Omega/\square$ (dimensionally still ohms, but per square).
- **Ohm's law (microscopic):** $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ where $\sigma = 1/\rho$.
- **Two-probe limitation:** Measures $R_{sample} + R_{contact}$ — *unreliable* for thin films.
- **Four-probe advantage:** Inner voltage probes draw negligible current → **contact-resistance free**.
- **Four-probe formula (semi-infinite):** $\rho = 2\pi s \cdot V/I$ (add $F$ for finite samples).
- **Sheet resistance formula:** $R_s = \rho / t$. For a square: $R = R_s$ regardless of size.
- **Van der Pauw formula (symmetric):** $\rho = (\pi / \ln 2) \cdot d \cdot R_s \approx 4.532 \, d \, R_s$.
- **Hall coefficient:** $R_H = V_H t / (IB)$; carrier density $n = 1/(q R_H)$; mobility $\mu = \sigma/(nq)$.
- **Shape-independence** is the unique selling point of Van der Pauw over four-probe.
- **Conversion memory hook:** $R (\Omega) = R_s (\Omega/\square) \times (\text{number of squares})$.
- **Industries using these measurements:** IC fabrication, solar cells (ITO), MEMS sensors, quality control of doped wafers.
- **Correction factor $F$:** Depends on $t/s$ ratio and lateral dimensions; tabulated in ASTM F 84.
- **Standard reference materials:** Copper ($\rho \approx 1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m) used for instrument calibration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hall Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hall Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hall Effect** is a fundamental electromagnetic phenomenon discovered by American physicist **Edwin Herbert Hall in 1879**. It refers to the generation of a measurable transverse electromotive force (voltage) across a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor specimen when it is subjected to a magnetic field that is perpendicular to the direction of current flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The Hall Effect is the production of a potential difference (Hall Voltage, $V_H$) across a current-carrying conductor/semiconductor, transverse to both the current direction and an applied perpendicular magnetic field, due to the deflection of charge carriers by the Lorentz force.

In materials characterization, the Hall Effect is a **primary, non-destructive, and contact-based electrical measurement technique** used to determine:
- **Type** of majority charge carriers (electron or hole)
- **Concentration** ($n$ or $p$) of charge carriers per unit volume
- **Mobility** ($\mu$) of charge carriers
- **Fermi level** and **band structure** information
- **Magnetic field strength** (in Hall Effect sensors)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **river flowing through a straight channel** (this is the electric current $I$). Now imagine a strong **sideways wind** blowing perpendicular to the river (this is the magnetic field $B$). The water molecules (charge carriers) will be pushed by the wind toward one bank of the channel. As they accumulate on one side, the opposite bank becomes "depleted," creating a slight **height difference** between the two banks (this is the Hall Voltage $V_H$).

The river is no longer flowing straight — it gets deflected to one side. The difference in water level between the two banks is a direct measure of:
- How fast the river is flowing (current $I$)
- How strong the wind is (magnetic field $B$)
- The type and number of "molecules" in the water (carrier type and concentration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU?** The Hall Effect is the **only direct method** to determine the *sign* of charge carriers — a problem that simple resistivity measurements cannot solve. It forms the backbone of modern semiconductor device physics and is essential for characterizing materials like Si, Ge, GaAs, InSb, and 2D materials (graphene, MoS₂).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and units are critical for Hall Effect calculations in the KTU 2024 scheme:

- **Electron charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$
- **Hall Coefficient ($R_H$) unit:** $\text{m}^3/\text{C}$ (SI) or $\text{cm}^3/\text{C}$ (practical)
- **Mobility ($\mu$) unit:** $\text{cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ or $\text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$
- **Hall Voltage ($V_H$):** typically in the range of **microvolts to millivolts**
- **Standard sample geometry:** rectangular thin film or Hall bar (van der Pauw configuration)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Logical Breakdown

The Hall Effect operates on the principle of the **Lorentz Force** acting on moving charge carriers. The process can be broken down into five sequential logical steps:

**Step 1 — Current Initiation**
A steady current $I$ is passed along the $x$-axis of a rectangular semiconductor specimen. The conventional current corresponds to the drift of majority charge carriers (electrons in n-type, holes in p-type) along the $+x$ direction with an average drift velocity $v_d$.

**Step 2 — Magnetic Field Application**
A uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is applied along the $z$-axis (perpendicular to the current). This sets up the geometric condition required for maximum Lorentz deflection.

**Step 3 — Lorentz Force Deflection**
The Lorentz force $\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v}_d \times \vec{B})$ acts on each moving charge carrier. Since $\vec{v}_d \parallel \hat{x}$ and $\vec{B} \parallel \hat{z}$, the cross product $\vec{v}_d \times \vec{B}$ points along $\pm \hat{y}$. This transverse force pushes carriers toward one face of the specimen (the $+y$ face for electrons, the $-y$ face for holes).

**Step 4 — Charge Accumulation & Hall Field Buildup**
Carriers accumulate on one face, leaving the opposite face with a net opposite charge. This creates a **transverse Hall electric field** $\vec{E}_H$ along the $y$-axis that opposes further deflection.

**Step 5 — Steady-State Equilibrium**
In steady state, the electric force $qE_H$ exactly balances the magnetic force $qv_d B$. A stable, measurable **Hall voltage** $V_H$ appears between the two transverse contacts.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why is the field perpendicular?** Maximum Lorentz force is achieved when $\vec{v}_d \perp \vec{B}$ (since $|\vec{v}_d \times \vec{B}| = v_d B \sin\theta$, maximized at $\theta = 90°$).
- **Why does the Hall field form?** Charge separation is a direct consequence of Coulomb's law — accumulated charges produce an opposing field to prevent further accumulation.
- **Why is the Hall voltage small?** Because $v_d$ is small (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}\ \text{m/s}$ in semiconductors), the force imbalance, while real, generates only a small voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners often test whether students understand that *the sign of $V_H$ reveals the type of charge carrier*. A negative $V_H$ implies n-type; a positive $V_H$ implies p-type. This is a high-yield 2-mark concept.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units (SI) | KTU Significance |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Lorentz Force | $\vec{F}$ | $\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v}_d \times \vec{B})$ | N | Root cause of carrier deflection |
| 2 | Hall Voltage | $V_H$ | $V_H = \dfrac{IB}{qnd}$ | V | Direct measured quantity |
| 3 | Hall Coefficient | $R_H$ | $R_H = \dfrac{1}{qn}$ (n-type) | $\text{m}^3/\text{C}$ | Reveals carrier type & density |
| 4 | Hall Coefficient | $R_H$ | $R_H = \dfrac{1}{pq}$ (p-type) | $\text{m}^3/\text{C}$ | Sign distinguishes carrier type |
| 5 | General Hall Coefficient | $R_H$ | $R_H = \dfrac{V_H \cdot t}{I \cdot B}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{C}$ | Experimental determination |
| 6 | Carrier Concentration | $n$ or $p$ | $n = \dfrac{1}{qR_H}$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | Most common characterization output |
| 7 | Hall Mobility | $\mu_H$ | $\mu_H = \sigma \cdot \vert R_H \vert$ | $\text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ | Material quality indicator |
| 8 | Hall Mobility (alternate) | $\mu_H$ | $\mu_H = \dfrac{\sigma}{qn}$ | $\text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ | Linked to conductivity |
| 9 | Conductivity | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = nq\mu$ | S/m | Classical Drude relation |
| 10 | Hall Angle (angle between $E$ and current) | $\theta_H$ | $\tan\theta_H = \dfrac{\mu_H B}{1}$ | rad | Useful in sensor design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Note:** $t$ = sample thickness along $\vec{B}$; $d$ = thickness along $\vec{B}$ (commonly used interchangeably); $w$ = width perpendicular to both $\vec{I}$ and $\vec{B}$. In KTU problems, $d$ is **thickness**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Hall Effect is foundational to:
- **Semiconductor industry:** doping profiling, wafer uniformity testing
- **Magnetic field sensors:** contactless current sensing, position encoders, BLDC motor commutation
- **Magnetometers:** Earth's field measurement, space applications
- **Materials science:** characterizing new 2D materials (graphene shows anomalous quantum Hall effect)
- **Quantum metrology:** quantum Hall resistance standard ($R_K = h/e^2$) used to define the ohm

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Hall Voltage ($V_H$)

**Setup:** A rectangular semiconductor bar of length $L$ (along $x$), width $w$ (along $y$), and thickness $t$ (along $z$). Current $I$ flows along $+x$, magnetic field $\vec{B}$ along $+z$.

**Step 1 — Write the drift velocity in terms of current.**

The current density is uniform across the cross-sectional area $A = w \cdot t$:

$$J = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{I}{w \cdot t}$$

By definition, current density is also given by $J = nqv_d$ for n-type material. Therefore, the drift velocity is:

$$v_d = \frac{I}{nq \cdot w \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state force balance.**

In steady state, the transverse Hall electric field $E_H$ exactly counteracts the magnetic force:

$$qE_H = qv_d B$$

This simplifies to:

$$E_H = v_d B = \frac{IB}{nq \cdot w \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Relate Hall electric field to Hall voltage.**

The Hall voltage is measured across the width $w$ of the specimen:

$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = \frac{IB}{nq \cdot w \cdot t} \cdot w$$

The width $w$ cancels out, yielding the **fundamental Hall voltage equation**:

$$\boxed{V_H = \frac{IB}{nqt}}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-verify with p-type material.**

For holes (charge $+q$), the derivation is identical, but the polarity of $V_H$ is **reversed** (Hall field points from $+y$ to $-y$ instead of the opposite). The magnitude remains:

$$V_H = \frac{IB}{pqt}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Hall Coefficient ($R_H$) and Carrier Concentration

Starting from $V_H = \dfrac{IB}{nqt}$, solve for the quantity that depends only on material properties:

$$R_H \equiv \frac{V_H \cdot t}{I \cdot B}$$

Substituting $V_H$:

$$R_H = \frac{1}{nqt} \cdot \frac{t \cdot B}{I \cdot B} \cdot I = \frac{1}{nq}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_H = \frac{1}{nq} \quad (\text{n-type}) \quad \text{or} \quad R_H = \frac{1}{pq} \quad (\text{p-type})}$$

**Sign Convention:** $R_H$ is **negative** for electrons (n-type) and **positive** for holes (p-type). This sign is the basis for the carrier-type determination.

Carrier concentration:

$$\boxed{n = \frac{1}{q \vert R_H \vert}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation III — Hall Mobility ($\mu_H$)

The electrical conductivity of an n-type semiconductor is given by the Drude relation:

$$\sigma = nq\mu$$

where $\mu$ is the **drift mobility**. Since $R_H = 1/(nq)$:

$$nq = \frac{1}{R_H}$$

Substituting into the conductivity expression:

$$\sigma = \frac{\mu}{R_H} \implies \mu = \sigma R_H$$

For the magnitude (since $R_H$ is signed):

$$\boxed{\mu_H = \sigma \cdot \vert R_H \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The mobility derived from Hall measurements is specifically called **Hall mobility** $\mu_H$, which may differ slightly from the **drift mobility** $\mu_d$ by a factor known as the **Hall factor** $r_H = \mu_H / \mu_d$. For most practical KTU problems, $r_H \approx 1$ is assumed.

### 3.4 Comprehensive Derivation — Coupled Electric and Magnetic Field

For a fully rigorous treatment, consider the case where both Hall field and Lorentz force act on the carrier. The total transverse force is zero in steady state:

$$F_y = qE_H - qv_x B_z = 0$$

Therefore $E_H = v_x B_z$. The current density has two components — longitudinal ($J_x$) and transverse ($J_y$, which is zero in open-circuit Hall measurement):

$$J_x = nq v_x, \quad J_y = 0$$

The Hall angle $\theta_H$ is defined as the angle between the total electric field vector and the current direction:

$$\tan\theta_H = \frac{E_H}{E_x} = \frac{v_x B_z}{E_x} = \frac{\mu_H B_z}{1}$$

The factor $\mu_H B$ is dimensionless and is called the **Hall factor** in some contexts.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Governing Relations

For quick reference, the complete set of KTU-required equations is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= \frac{IB}{nqt} \\
R_H &= \frac{V_H t}{IB} = \frac{1}{nq} \\
n &= \frac{1}{q\vert R_H \vert} \\
\mu_H &= \sigma \vert R_H \vert = \frac{\sigma}{nq} \\
\tan\theta_H &= \mu_H B
\end{aligned}
$$
