# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Hysteresis: Why the Loop Exists

The M-H loop is a macroscopic manifestation of microscopic **magnetic domain dynamics**. A ferromagnetic material is partitioned into **Weiss domains** — small regions (typically 1–100 μm) within which all atomic magnetic moments are aligned in the same direction due to the **exchange interaction** (quantum mechanical coupling between neighboring spins).

### Step-by-Step Domain Theory of Hysteresis:

1. **Unmagnetized State (H = 0):** Domains are randomly oriented such that the net magnetization of the bulk material is zero. Domain walls (Bloch walls or Néel walls) separate adjacent domains.

2. **Initial Magnetization Curve (virgin curve):** As a small positive $H$ is applied, domains with magnetization vectors most closely aligned to $H$ grow at the expense of less favorably oriented domains through **domain wall motion** (reversible). Magnetization increases linearly with $H$.

3. **Sudden Domain Wall Jumps (Barkhausen Effect):** At critical field values, domain walls overcome pinning sites (lattice defects, impurities, grain boundaries) and **irreversibly** jump to new positions. This produces small, discontinuous steps in magnetization — the **Barkhausen noise**.

4. **Domain Rotation:** Once wall motion is exhausted, further increases in $H$ cause the magnetization vectors inside each domain to **rotate coherently** toward the field direction. This is energetically costly and requires stronger fields.

5. **Approach to Saturation (M → Mₛ):** At very high $H$, all domain vectors are essentially aligned with $H$. The magnetization asymptotically approaches $M_s$, which is an intrinsic property determined by the atomic magnetic moment per unit volume: $M_s = n \cdot \mu_{atom}$, where $n$ is the number density of magnetic atoms.

6. **Field Reduction (H: Mₛ → 0):** When $H$ is reduced back to zero, the domains do **not** fully return to their original random configuration. Some domains remain pinned in the field direction, leaving a residual **remanent magnetization $M_r$**.

7. **Reverse Saturation (H → −Hₛ):** To bring $M$ back to zero, a reverse field of magnitude $H_c$ (coercivity) must be applied. Continuing to increase the reverse field eventually aligns all domains in the opposite direction, reaching $-M_s$.

8. **Loop Closure:** Cycling $H$ from $+H_s$ back to $+H_s$ closes the loop. The enclosed area represents **hysteresis loss** — energy dissipated as heat per unit volume per cycle.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** required to solve Module 4 M-H loop problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Formula | Description / Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B = \mu_0 (H + M)$ | Fundamental magnetic constitutive relation linking $B$, $H$, $M$ | $B$: T, $H$: A/m, $M$: A/m |
| 2 | $B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Definition of permeability $\mu$ and relative permeability $\mu_r$ | H/m (dimensionless for $\mu_r$) |
| 3 | $M = \chi_m H$ | Magnetic susceptibility relation (linear regime) | $\chi_m$ dimensionless |
| 4 | $\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m$ | Relation between relative permeability and susceptibility | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | Vacuum permeability constant | H/m |
| 6 | $W_h = \oint H \, dB$ | Hysteresis energy loss per unit volume per cycle | J/m³ |
| 7 | $W_h \approx \oint M \, dH$ | Approximation for high-permeability materials (since $B \approx \mu_0 M$) | J/m³ |
| 8 | $M_s = \frac{N \mu_{atom}}{V}$ | Saturation magnetization from atomic moment density | A/m |
| 9 | $\mu_B = \frac{e\hbar}{2m_e}$ | Bohr magneton magnitude | J/T |
| 10 | $\chi_m = \frac{M}{H}$ | Definition of differential susceptibility (from M-H loop slope) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $S = \frac{M_r}{M_s}$ | Squareness ratio (rectangularity of loop) | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 12 | $P_h = f \cdot V \cdot W_h$ | Total power loss in a magnetic core (Steinmetz equation, simplified) | W |
| 13 | $\mu_i = \lim_{H \to 0} \frac{B}{H}$ | Initial permeability from initial slope of B-H or M-H curve | H/m |
| 14 | $\mu_{max} = \left(\frac{B}{H}\right)_{max}$ | Maximum permeability (steepest slope point of B-H loop) | H/m |
| 15 | $T_C$: $\chi_m \propto \frac{1}{T - T_C}$ | Curie-Weiss law for paramagnetic regime above $T_C$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In numerical problems, when asked to compute **hysteresis loss**, examiners expect the integration $\oint H \, dB$ to be approximated as the **geometric area** enclosed by the loop. For symmetric loops approximated as ellipses: $W_h \approx \pi \cdot H_c \cdot B_r$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

The M-H loop is not merely an academic curiosity. Its parameters directly dictate material selection in real engineering systems:

- **Hard Magnetic Materials (Permanent Magnets):** NdFeB, SmCo, AlNiCo, hard ferrites. High $H_c$ (>$10^5$ A/m), high $M_r$, large $W_h$. Used in motors, generators, MRI machines, loudspeakers, hard disk drives, and magnetic separators.

- **Soft Magnetic Materials (Transformer Cores, Inductors):** Silicon steel (Fe-Si), permalloy (Ni-Fe), soft ferrites (MnZn, NiZn). Low $H_c$ (<$10^2$ A/m), high $\mu_i$, narrow loop, small $W_h$. Used in transformers, chokes, motors, and electromagnetic shielding to **minimize energy loss**.

- **Magnetic Recording Media:** Hard disk platters and magnetic tapes require high $M_r/M_s$ ratio (high squareness) to retain sharp bit transitions. The M-H loop's squareness $S$ dictates storage density.

- **Magnetostrictive Sensors & Actuators:** Materials like Terfenol-D (Tb-Dy-Fe) exhibit large magnetostriction; their M-H loops are characterized in actuator design.

- **Biomedical Applications:** Magnetic nanoparticles (e.g., Fe₃O₄) for hyperthermia cancer treatment, MRI contrast agents, and targeted drug delivery are characterized by their M-H loops to ensure safe magnetic response.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between M-H Loop and B-H Loop

### Statement:
Show that for a high-permeability soft magnetic material, the B-H loop area is approximately $\mu_0$ times the M-H loop area.

### Given:
- $B = \mu_0(H + M)$
- For soft magnetic materials, $M \gg H$ (since $\mu_r \gg 1$, meaning $\chi_m \gg 1$)

### Derivation:

Starting from the constitutive relation:

$$
B = \mu_0 H + \mu_0 M
$$

Since for soft magnetic materials $\vert M \vert \gg \vert H \vert$, the first term is negligible in comparison:

$$
B \approx \mu_0 M
$$

This is the **soft material approximation** used in transformer design. Now, consider the hysteresis loss integral (energy dissipated per cycle per unit volume):

$$
W_h^{(B)} = \oint H \, dB
$$

Apply the chain rule. Since $B$ is a function of $H$ along the loop:

$$
dB = \mu_0 \, dM + \mu_0 \, dH
$$

Therefore:

$$
W_h^{(B)} = \oint H \left( \mu_0 \, dM + \mu_0 \, dH \right) = \mu_0 \oint H \, dM + \mu_0 \oint H \, dH
$$

Evaluate the second integral. Since $H$ is a single-valued (though hysteretic) function that returns to its starting value after one complete cycle, the integral $\oint H \, dH = 0$ (analogous to $\oint x \, dx = \frac{1}{2}[x^2] = 0$ over a closed path).

Thus:

$$
W_h^{(B)} = \mu_0 \oint H \, dM = \mu_0 \cdot W_h^{(M)}
$$

This proves that **the B-H loop area is $\mu_0$ times the M-H loop area** for soft magnetic materials.

$$
\boxed{W_h^{(B)} \approx \mu_0 \cdot W_h^{(M)}}
$$

**Physical Significance:** This is why soft magnetic materials are characterized by their M-H loops using VSMs (which measure $M$ directly) rather than B-H analyzers (which measure $B$). The conversion is trivial, and the M-H loop directly yields the magnetization physics without the vacuum contribution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hysteresis Loss for an Elliptical Loop Approximation

### Statement:
A soft magnetic material exhibits an M-H loop that is approximately elliptical with major semi-axis $M_s$ (vertical) and minor semi-axis $H_s$ (horizontal). Determine the hysteresis loss per cycle per unit volume.

### Given:
- Loop is an ellipse: $\frac{M^2}{M_s^2} + \frac{H^2}{H_s^2} = 1$
- Hysteresis loss definition: $W_h = \oint M \, dH$ (M-H loop approximation)

### Derivation:

Parametrize the ellipse using:
- $H = H_s \cos\theta$
- $M = M_s \sin\theta$
- where $\theta$ goes from $0$ to $2\pi$ as we trace the loop once.

Then:
- $dH = -H_s \sin\theta \, d\theta$

Therefore:
$$
W_h = \oint M \, dH = \int_0^{2\pi} (M_s \sin\theta) \cdot (-H_s \sin\theta) \, d\theta
$$

$$
W_h = -M_s H_s \int_0^{2\pi} \sin^2\theta \, d\theta
$$

Use the standard trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$
W_h = -M_s H_s \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} \, d\theta
$$

$$
W_h = -\frac{M_s H_s}{2} \left[ \theta - \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} \right]_0^{2\pi}
$$

Evaluate the boundary terms:
- $\sin(2\pi \cdot 2) = \sin(4\pi) = 0$
- $\sin(0) = 0$
- $\theta \vert_0^{2\pi} = 2\pi$

Thus:

$$
W_h = -\frac{M_s H_s}{2} \cdot (2\pi - 0 - 0 + 0) = -\pi M_s H_s
$$

The negative sign indicates the loop is traced clockwise; the magnitude is the energy dissipated:

$$
\boxed{W_h = \pi M_s H_s}
$$

Wait — this is the result if the loop were a perfect ellipse with axes $M_s$ and $H_s$. The result should be corrected for the **correct sign convention** (hysteresis loss is positive energy dissipated):

$$
W_h = \pi M_s H_s \quad \text{[J/m³ per cycle]}
$$

**Caveat:** For a real B-H loop, the analogous result is $W_h^{(B)} = \pi H_c B_r$, where $H_c$ is the coercivity and $B_r$ is the remanent flux density. This is the **Steinmetz approximation** frequently quoted in transformer design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Susceptibility from Initial Slope of M-H Curve

### Statement:
A paramagnetic material exhibits $M = \chi H$ in weak fields. Show that the initial slope of the virgin M-H curve equals the magnetic susceptibility.

### Given:
- $M = \chi_m H$ (linear regime for paramagnets and for soft ferromagnets in weak fields)

### Derivation:

The differential susceptibility is defined as:

$$
\chi_{diff} = \frac{dM}{dH}
$$

For the linear regime where $M = \chi_m H$:

$$
\chi_{diff} = \frac{d}{dH}(\chi_m H) = \chi_m
$$

Therefore, the **slope of the initial linear portion of the M-H curve** equals the magnetic susceptibility $\chi_m$. The relative permeability follows as:

$$
\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** For an unmagnetized ferromagnet, the initial M-H curve starts with a small slope (low $\mu_r$). As $H$ increases and domain walls begin to move, the slope steepens (high $\mu_r$). Near saturation, the slope flattens again as $M \to M_s$. Thus $\mu_r$ is **field-dependent**, in stark contrast to paramagnets where $\chi_m$ is a constant.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A ferromagnetic sample is measured in a VSM. The following data are extracted from the M-H loop:
- Saturation magnetization: $M_s = 5.0 \times 10^5$ A/m
- Remanent magnetization: $M_r = 3.0 \times 10^5$ A/m
- Coercivity: $H_c = 2.0 \times 10^4$ A/m

**Calculate:**
(a) The squareness ratio $S$.
(b) The hysteresis loss per unit volume per cycle, assuming an elliptical loop approximation.
(c) Classify the material (soft vs. hard magnetic).

### Solution:

**(a) Squareness Ratio:**

$$
S = \frac{M_r}{M_s} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^5}{5.0 \times 10^5} = 0.6
$$

**(b) Hysteresis Loss (Elliptical Approximation):**

Using the derived formula $W_h = \pi M_s H_c$:

$$
W_h = \pi \times (5.0 \times 10^5) \times (2.0 \times 10^4)
$$

$$
W_h = \pi \times 1.0 \times 10^{10} = 3.1416 \times 10^{10} \text{ J/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{W_h \approx 3.14 \times 10^{10} \text{ J/m}^3}
$$

**(c) Classification:**

Since $H_c = 2.0 \times 10^4$ A/m = 20,000 A/m, and typical hard magnetic materials have $H_c > 10^5$ A/m, while soft materials have $H_c < 10^3$ A/m, this material falls in the **intermediate range**. With $H_c \approx 2 \times 10^4$ A/m, it is a **semi-hard magnetic material** (e.g., suitable for hysteresis motors, magnetic recording media).

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Reference for KTU:** Hard: $H_c > 10^4$ A/m; Semi-hard: $10^2 < H_c < 10^4$ A/m; Soft: $H_c < 10^2$ A/m. Always memorize these thresholds for material classification questions.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an M-H Loop Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply Unit] -->|DC current| B[Electromagnet]
    B -->|H field generation| C[Sample Holder]
    C -->|Vibrating mechanism| D[Vibration Driver]
    D -->|Mechanical oscillation| C
    C -->|Induced voltage in pickup coils| E[Lock-in Amplifier]
    E -->|Signal conditioning| F[Data Acquisition System]
    F -->|Plotting M vs H| G[M-H Loop Output]
    H[Field Controller] -->|H magnitude setpoint| B
    H -->|Sweep control| F
    I[Helium Cryostat or Room Temp Stage] -->|Temperature control| C
    subgraph Magnetometer Core
    B
    C
    D
    E
    end
    subgraph Control & Readout
    A
    H
    F
    G
    end
    subgraph Sample Environment
    I
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **power supply** drives current through the **electromagnet**, generating a controllable magnetic field $H$ at the sample location.
- The **field controller** sweeps $H$ from $-H_s$ to $+H_s$ (and back) in discrete steps or continuously.
- The **vibration driver** oscillates the sample sinusoidally, inducing an alternating voltage in stationary pickup coils (Faraday's law of induction).
- The induced voltage is **proportional to the sample's magnetic moment $m$**, not $H$. Dividing by the sample volume yields $M$.
- The **lock-in amplifier** extracts the weak signal from noise using phase-sensitive detection.
- The **DAS** records $(H, M)$ pairs and plots the M-H loop in real time.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Anatomy of the M-H Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: H = 0, M = 0 Origin] -->|H increases| B[Initial Magnetization Curve]
    B -->|Approaches saturation| C[Saturation Point Ms at Hs]
    C -->|H decreases to 0| D[Remanent Magnetization Mr]
    D -->|H reverses to Hc| E[Coercivity Point Hc where M = 0]
    E -->|H continues negative| F[Negative Saturation minus Ms]
    F -->|H returns to positive Hs| G[Loop Closure back to Ms]
    G --> A
    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#f99,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#9ff,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The loop is traced as $H$ is swept from $0 \to +H_s \to 0 \to -H_s \to 0 \to +H_s$.
- **Ms** = saturation magnetization (top of loop)
- **Mr** = remanent magnetization (right intercept on M-axis)
- **Hc** = coercivity (left intercept on H-axis, negative side)
- The **enclosed area** = hysteresis loss per cycle

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: Soft vs. Hard Magnetic Material Loops

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soft Magnetic Material] --> A1[Low Hc]
    A --> A2[High Mu_r]
    A --> A3[Narrow tall loop]
    A --> A4[Low hysteresis loss]
    B[Hard Magnetic Material] --> B1[High Hc]
    B --> B2[Low to moderate Mu_r]
    B --> B3[Wide loop with large area]
    B --> B4[High hysteresis loss]
    A -->|Example: Silicon Steel| A5[Transformer cores]
    A -->|Example: Permalloy| A6[Magnetic shielding]
    B -->|Example: NdFeB| B5[Permanent magnets]
    B -->|Example: AlNiCo| B6[Speakers, sensors]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**Q: Define the term "coercivity" as applied to a ferromagnetic material. How does its magnitude differ between soft and hard magnetic materials?**

**Model Answer:**
Coercivity ($H_c$) is the magnitude of the reverse magnetic field intensity required to reduce the residual magnetization of a magnetized ferromagnetic material to zero. It is a measure of the material's resistance to demagnetization and is determined from the M-H loop as the H-intercept on the descending branch where $M = 0$.

- **Soft magnetic materials** (e.g., silicon steel, permalloy) have **low coercivity** ($H_c < 10^2$ A/m) because their domain walls move easily with low energy barriers. They are easily magnetized and demagnetized.
- **Hard magnetic materials** (e.g., NdFeB, SmCo) have **high coercivity** ($H_c > 10^4$ A/m, up to $10^6$ A/m) due to strong domain wall pinning by defects, grain boundaries, and magnetocrystalline anisotropy. They resist demagnetization, making them suitable as permanent magnets.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Q: What is the significance of the squareness ratio $S = M_r/M_s$ in characterizing magnetic materials?**

**Model Answer:**
The squareness ratio $S = M_r/M_s$ quantifies how "square" (rectangular) the M-H loop is, indicating the degree to which a material retains its magnetization after the external field is removed.

- For $S$ approaching 1 (square loop): The material is well-suited for **magnetic recording media** (hard disks, tapes) and **permanent magnets**, since it retains nearly all its saturation magnetization upon field removal.
- For $S$ approaching 0 (thin loop): The material is suitable for **transformer cores, inductors, and motors**, since it can be rapidly switched without retaining stray magnetization.

The squareness ratio is influenced by microstructure: random polycrystalline samples tend to have $S \approx 0.5$, while grain-aligned or single-crystal samples approach $S \to 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neatly labelled diagram, describe the M-H loop (hysteresis loop) of a ferromagnetic material. Clearly indicate the following parameters on the diagram: (i) Saturation magnetization $M_s$, (ii) Remanent magnetization $M_r$, (iii) Coercivity $H_c$, and (iv) Initial magnetization curve. Explain the physical significance of the area enclosed by the loop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon steel core used in a transformer exhibits the following parameters extracted from its M-H loop:
- $M_s = 1.6 \times 10^6$ A/m
- $M_r = 8.0 \times 10^5$ A/m
- $H_c = 50$ A/m

Calculate: (i) The squareness ratio $S$, (ii) The hysteresis loss per cycle per unit volume using the elliptical approximation, (iii) Classify the material as soft, semi-hard, or hard, and justify your answer. If the transformer operates at 50 Hz, what is the hysteresis power loss per cubic meter of core?

---

### Model Answer to Question A:

**(a) Description of M-H Loop:**

The M-H loop of a ferromagnetic material is a closed curve obtained by plotting the magnetization $M$ on the y-axis against the applied field intensity $H$ on the x-axis as $H$ is cycled through a complete magnetization-demagnetization cycle.

```
            M |
              |        +-----+
       M_s    |       /       \
              |      /         \
              |     /           \
              |    /             \
       M_r    |---*               \
              |   |                \
              |   |                 \
       -------+---*-----------------*---  H
             0|   H_c              +H_s
              |   -|               |
              |    |               |
       -M_r   |    |               *
              |    |                \
              |    |                 \
       -M_s   |    +-----            *
              |          \         /
              |           \       /
              |            +-----+
```

**Parameters:**
- **(i) Saturation Magnetization $M_s$:** The maximum magnetization attained at high applied field; all domains are aligned parallel to $H$.
- **(ii) Remanent Magnetization $M_r$:** The residual magnetization remaining in the material when $H$ is reduced to zero after saturation.
- **(iii) Coercivity $H_c$:** The reverse field intensity required to reduce $M$ to zero from the remanent state.
- **(iv) Initial Magnetization Curve:** The trajectory from $M = 0$, $H = 0$ (virgin state) to $M = M_s$ as $H$ is increased for the first time. This curve lies outside the hysteresis loop.

**Physical Significance of the Enclosed Area:**
The area enclosed by the M-H loop represents the **hysteresis energy loss per unit volume per cycle**:

$$
W_h = \oint H \, dM \quad \text{[J/m³ per cycle]}
$$

This energy is dissipated as heat due to irreversible domain wall motion, Barkhausen jumps, and domain rotation against anisotropy. Minimizing this loss is critical for energy-efficient transformer and motor design, favoring soft magnetic materials with narrow loops.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $M_s = 1.6 \times 10^6$ A/m
- $M_r = 8.0 \times 10^5$ A/m
- $H_c = 50$ A/m

**(i) Squareness Ratio:**

$$
S = \frac{M_r}{M_s} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^5}{1.6 \times 10^6} = 0.5
$$

**(ii) Hysteresis Loss (Elliptical Approximation):**

Using the derived formula $W_h = \pi M_s H_c$:

$$
W_h = \pi \times (1.6 \times 10^6) \times (50)
$$

$$
W_h = \pi \times 8.0 \times 10^7 = 2.513 \times 10^8 \text{ J/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{W_h \approx 2.51 \times 10^8 \text{ J/m}^3 \text{ per cycle}}
$$

**(iii) Classification:**

Since $H_c = 50$ A/m $= 5 \times 10^1$ A/m, which is **less than** $10^2$ A/m, the material qualifies as a **soft magnetic material**. This is consistent with silicon steel's typical use in transformer cores, where low coercivity and low hysteresis loss are paramount.

**Hysteresis Power Loss at 50 Hz:**

For a volume $V$ of core material, the power loss is:

$$
P_h = f \cdot V \cdot W_h
$$

Per unit volume ($V = 1$ m³):

$$
P_h = 50 \, \text{Hz} \times 1 \, \text{m}^3 \times 2.513 \times 10^8 \, \text{J/m}^3
$$

$$
P_h = 1.257 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{P_h \approx 12.57 \text{ W/cm}^3}
$$

This is an enormous power density, illustrating why transformer cores are designed as thin laminated sheets (to reduce eddy current losses) and why core cooling is essential.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the operating principles, sensitivity, and typical applications of the following magnetometers: (i) Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM), (ii) SQUID Magnetometer, and (iii) Alternating Gradient Magnetometer (AGM). Present your answer in a structured tabular format with at least four differentiating parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with relevant equations the following terms in the context of M-H loop analysis: (i) Magnetic susceptibility $\chi_m$, (ii) Relative permeability $\mu_r$, (iii) Initial permeability $\mu_i$, and (iv) Curie temperature $T_C$. How does $\chi_m$ vary with temperature for (a) a paramagnetic material above $T_C$ and (b) a ferromagnetic material below $T_C$?

---

### Model Answer to Question B:

**(a) Comparison of Magnetometers:**

| Parameter | VSM (Vibrating Sample Magnetometer) | SQUID (Superconducting Quantum Interference Device) | AGM (Alternating Gradient Magnetometer) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Sample vibrates sinusoidally in a uniform field; induced voltage in pickup coils is proportional to magnetic moment | Uses Josephson junctions in a superconducting ring to detect flux changes with extreme sensitivity | Sample experiences alternating field gradient producing mechanical force; detected by piezoelectric transducer |
| **Sensitivity** | $\sim 10^{-5}$ emu ($\sim 10^{-8}$ A·m²) | $\sim 10^{-8}$ emu ($\sim 10^{-11}$ A·m²) | $\sim 10^{-8}$ emu ($\sim 10^{-11}$ A·m²) |
| **Maximum Field** | Typically up to 3 T (room temperature), higher with cryocooled versions | Up to 7 T (superconducting magnet) | Up to ~2 T |
| **Typical Sample Form** | Bulk pieces, thin films, powders (any shape) | Very small masses, weakly magnetic samples, single crystals | Small mass (~1–10 mg) preferred |
| **Measurement Time** | Minutes per loop | Minutes per point; long loop acquisition | Fastest (seconds per point) |
| **Temperature Range** | 4 K to 1000 K (with accessories) | 1.8 K to 400 K standard; 1000 K optional | 77 K to 1000 K |
| **Best Use Case** | Routine M-H loops, B-H analysis, susceptibility vs. T | Ultra-weak signals (paramagnets, superconductors, dilute systems) | Fast screening, small samples, quality control |

**(b) Definitions and Temperature Dependence:**

**(i) Magnetic Susceptibility $\chi_m$:**

$$
\chi_m = \frac{M}{H}
$$

A dimensionless quantity that measures the degree of magnetization of a material in response to an applied magnetic field. For vacuum, $\chi_m = 0$; for ferromagnets, $\chi_m$ can reach $10^4$–$10^6$.

**(ii) Relative Permeability $\mu_r$:**

$$
\mu_r = \frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = 1 + \chi_m
$$

The ratio of the material's permeability to that of free space. For ferromagnets, $\mu_r \gg 1$; for diamagnets, $\mu_r < 1$ (slightly).

**(iii) Initial Permeability $\mu_i$:**

$$
\mu_i = \lim_{H \to 0^+} \frac{B}{H}
$$

The slope of the B-H curve (or $\mu_0(1+\chi_m)$) in the very low-field limit, dominated by reversible domain wall motion in ferromagnets. It is a critical parameter for soft magnetic materials used at low signal levels (e.g., audio transformers, chokes).

**(iv) Curie Temperature $T_C$:**

The critical temperature above which a ferromagnetic material loses its ordered magnetic state and becomes paramagnetic. Above $T_C$, thermal energy overcomes the exchange interaction that aligns atomic moments.

**Temperature Dependence of $\chi_m$:**

**(a) Paramagnetic regime (above $T_C$):** Curie's law modified by Weiss molecular field theory:

$$
\chi_m = \frac{C}{T - T_C}
$$

where $C$ is the Curie constant. As $T \to T_C^+$ (from above), $\chi_m \to \infty$, signaling the onset of spontaneous magnetization.

**(b) Ferromagnetic regime (below $T_C$):** Spontaneous magnetization $M_s(T)$ follows the **Weiss molecular field model**:

$$
M_s(T) = M_s(0) \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{T}{T_C}\right)^2 \right]^{1/2}
$$

(approximate form near $T_C$). $\chi_m$ becomes **field-dependent and history-dependent** (hysteretic) below $T_C$. At $T = 0$ K, $M_s$ is maximum; as $T \to T_C^-$, $M_s \to 0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing M-H loop with B-H loop:** Always state which quantity is on each axis. If the problem gives B-H data, convert using $M = B/\mu_0 - H$ before applying M-H formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** $H_c$ in A/m, $M_s$ in A/m (or emu/cm³ for CGS). Mixing SI and CGS costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Sign errors in hysteresis loss:** The integral $\oint H \, dM$ is taken as a positive quantity representing energy dissipated. Students often compute negative values due to loop orientation; take the absolute value or magnitude.
> 4. **Squareness ratio = 1 misconception:** Perfect squareness ($S = 1$) is an idealization never achieved in practice. Real hard magnets have $S \approx 0.8$–$0.95$.
> 5. **Steinmetz equation misuse:** $W_h = \pi H_c B_r$ applies to **B-H loops**, not M-H loops. For M-H loops, use $W_h \approx \pi H_c M_r$ or the elliptical approximation $W_h \approx \pi H_c M_s$ depending on loop shape.
> 6. **Forgetting the factor of $\mu_0$:** When converting between M-H and B-H loops, always include the factor $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Takeaways — Read This Before Every Exam:**

- ✅ The **M-H loop** is a plot of magnetization $M$ vs. applied field $H$ for a complete magnetization cycle.
- ✅ Four critical loop parameters: **$M_s$** (saturation), **$M_r$** (remanence), **$H_c$** (coercivity), **$S = M_r/M_s$** (squareness).
- ✅ **Loop area = hysteresis loss** $W_h = \oint H \, dM$ [J/m³ per cycle].
- ✅ **Elliptical approximation:** $W_h \approx \pi M_s H_c$ (for M-H loops) or $W_h \approx \pi H_c B_r$ (for B-H loops).
- ✅ **Soft materials**: low $H_c$, narrow loop, low loss → transformer cores, inductors.
- ✅ **Hard materials**: high $H_c$, wide loop, high loss → permanent magnets, recording media.
- ✅ **Magnetometers:** VSM (routine), SQUID (ultra-sensitive), AGM (fast), B-H analyzer (industrial cores).
- ✅ **Key relations:** $B = \mu_0(H+M)$; $M = \chi_m H$; $\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m$.
- ✅ **Curie temperature $T_C$**: Above $T_C$, ferromagnets become paramagnets; $\chi_m \propto 1/(T-T_C)$.
- ✅ **Domain theory**: Hysteresis arises from irreversible domain wall motion and rotation against pinning sites and anisotropy.
- ✅ **Barkhausen effect**: Discontinuous jumps in M during magnetization due to domain wall depinning.
- ✅ **Physical constant:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m; $\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ J/T.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Dependent Magnetization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Temperature Dependent Magnetization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Temperature Dependent Magnetization (TDM)** refers to the variation of the spontaneous magnetization (M) and magnetic susceptibility (χ) of a magnetically ordered material (ferro-, ferri-, or antiferro-magnetic) as a function of absolute temperature (T). The phenomenon is governed by the balance between **exchange energy** (which favors parallel spin alignment) and **thermal agitation energy** (which randomizes spin orientation). The transition temperature at which a material loses its long-range magnetic order is called the **Curie Temperature (T_C)** for ferro/ferrimagnets and the **Néel Temperature (T_N)** for antiferromagnets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Ferro-/Ferri-magnetic → **Curie Temperature (T_C)**
> - Antiferro-magnetic → **Néel Temperature (T_N)**
> - Paramagnetic phase exists only **above** T_C or T_N

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Building)

Imagine a classroom of disciplined students (magnetic dipoles) sitting in perfectly aligned rows (**ferromagnetic order** at low T). As the classroom gets hot (rising temperature), students get restless — they fidget, then shuffle, then eventually stand up and walk around chaotically (**paramagnetic state at T > T_C**). The exact "temperature" at which complete disorder sets in is the **Curie Temperature**. For antiferromagnets, imagine two rows of students facing opposite directions (alternating spin up/down) — at T_N, this alternating pattern dissolves.

> [!NOTE]
> The thermal energy at T_C is approximately **k_B T_C** (Boltzmann constant × Curie temperature), which equals the exchange energy **J** that holds spins together. This is why materials with stronger exchange interactions have **higher** ordering temperatures.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Boltzmann Constant**: $k_B = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Bohr Magneton**: $\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24} \text{ J/T}$
- **Ordering Temperatures of Common Materials**:
  - **Iron (Fe)**: $T_C = 1043 \text{ K}$
  - **Cobalt (Co)**: $T_C = 1394 \text{ K}$
  - **Nickel (Ni)**: $T_C = 627 \text{ K}$
  - **Gd**: $T_C = 293 \text{ K}$ (near room temperature)
  - **MnO (antiferro)**: $T_N = 116 \text{ K}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Temperature Regimes

A ferromagnetic material exhibits **three distinct thermal regimes**:

### Regime I: Low Temperature (T → 0 K)
- Magnetization reaches its **saturation value** $M(0) = N g_J J \mu_B$
- Spin waves (magnons) are excited thermally
- Bloch's $T^{3/2}$ Law governs the decrease:
$$M(T) = M(0)\left[1 - \alpha T^{3/2}\right]$$
where $\alpha$ is a material-specific constant.

### Regime II: Intermediate Temperature (0 < T < T_C)
- Magnetization decreases rapidly as T approaches $T_C$
- The critical behavior follows a power law near $T_C$:
$$M(T) = M(0)\left[\frac{T_C - T}{T_C}\right]^{\beta}, \quad \beta \approx 0.33$$

### Regime III: High Temperature (T > T_C)
- Material becomes **paramagnetic**
- Susceptibility follows the **Curie-Weiss Law**

## 2.2 Weiss Molecular Field Theory (The Foundation)

Pierre-Ernest Weiss (1907) proposed that spins in a ferromagnet experience an **internal molecular field** proportional to the magnetization:
$$\vec{H}_{mol} = \lambda \vec{M}$$
where $\lambda$ is the **molecular field constant** (units: A·m²/J or T·m³/kg).

The **effective field** experienced by each spin is:
$$\vec{H}_{eff} = \vec{H}_{ext} + \lambda \vec{M}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Applicable Regime |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\chi = \dfrac{C}{T - \theta}$ | Curie-Weiss Law | $T > T_C$ (ferro) / $T > T_N$ (antiferro) |
| 2 | $\chi = \dfrac{C}{T + \theta}$ | Modified Curie-Weiss | Antiferromagnets above $T_N$ ($\theta < 0$) |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{N \mu_0 \mu_B^2 g^2 J(J+1)}{3 k_B}$ | Curie Constant | Universal |
| 4 | $T_C = \lambda C$ | Curie Temperature | Weiss mean-field |
| 5 | $T_N = -\lambda C$ | Néel Temperature | Antiferro mean-field |
| 6 | $M(T) = M(0)\left[1 - \alpha T^{3/2}\right]$ | Bloch's Law | Low T ferromagnets |
| 7 | $M(T) = M(0)\left[\dfrac{T_C - T}{T_C}\right]^{\beta}$ | Critical Power Law | Near $T_C$ |
| 8 | $H_{mol} = \lambda M$ | Molecular Field | All ordered phases |
| 9 | $B_J(x) = \dfrac{2J+1}{2J}\coth\left[\dfrac{(2J+1)x}{2J}\right] - \dfrac{1}{2J}\coth\left[\dfrac{x}{2J}\right]$ | Brillouin Function | Mean-field solution |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{3 k_B T_C}{N g^2 \mu_B^2 J(J+1)}$ | Molecular field constant | Derived |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Notation Note:** In all formulas above, $\chi$ denotes magnetic susceptibility, $N$ is the number density of magnetic atoms per unit volume, $g$ is the Landé g-factor, and $J$ is the total angular momentum quantum number. **Never confuse the table pipe symbol with absolute value bars in your answer scripts — use proper $\vert$ notation.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Magnetic Refrigeration (MagRef)**: Materials like Gd-Si-Ge exhibit large magnetocaloric effect near $T_C$ — used in eco-friendly cooling.
2. **Temperature Sensors & Thermometers**: Curie temperature switches (Curie switches) used in oven thermostats.
3. **Hard Disk Drives**: Recording heads use materials with controlled $T_C$ to optimize writing efficiency.
4. **MRI Contrast Agents**: Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles behave differently above and below blocking temperature.
5. **Spintronic Devices**: Tunneling Magnetoresistance (TMR) depends critically on operating temperature relative to $T_C$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Curie-Weiss Law

**Starting Point**: Langevin's classical theory of paramagnetism gives the susceptibility as:

$$\chi = \frac{M}{H} = \frac{N \mu_0 \mu^2}{3 k_B T} = \frac{C_L}{T}$$

where $C_L$ is the **Langevin Curie constant** for classical dipoles.

**Step 1 — Incorporate the Weiss Molecular Field**:

The Langevin theory assumes spins respond only to the **external field** $H$. Weiss corrected this by replacing $H$ with the **effective field** $H_{eff} = H + \lambda M$:

$$M = \frac{C_L}{T}(H + \lambda M)$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic Rearrangement**:

Rearrange to isolate $M$ on the left-hand side:

$$M = \frac{C_L H}{T} + \frac{\lambda C_L M}{T}$$

$$M - \frac{\lambda C_L M}{T} = \frac{C_L H}{T}$$

$$M\left(1 - \frac{\lambda C_L}{T}\right) = \frac{C_L H}{T}$$

$$M\left(\frac{T - \lambda C_L}{T}\right) = \frac{C_L H}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Susceptibility**:

$$\chi = \frac{M}{H} = \frac{C_L}{T - \lambda C_L}$$

Defining $C = C_L$ and $\theta = \lambda C_L$, we obtain the celebrated **Curie-Weiss Law**:

$$\boxed{\chi = \frac{C}{T - \theta}}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Curie Temperature**:

At $T = \theta = \lambda C$, the susceptibility **diverges** ($\chi \to \infty$), signaling the onset of spontaneous magnetization. Therefore:

$$T_C = \theta = \lambda C$$

**Physical Insight**: The divergence of $\chi$ at $T_C$ is a hallmark of a **second-order phase transition** — the susceptibility formally becomes infinite because an infinitesimal external field can produce a finite magnetization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Curie Constant in Quantum Form

For atoms with total angular momentum $J$, Langevin's susceptibility is generalized using **quantum statistics** (Brillouin function $B_J$). For the high-temperature limit ($x \ll 1$):

$$B_J(x) \approx \frac{(J+1)}{3J} x$$

The effective magnetic moment squared per atom is:

$$\mu_{eff}^2 = g^2 \mu_B^2 J(J+1)$$

Substituting into the classical Curie formula:

$$\chi = \frac{N \mu_0 \mu_{eff}^2}{3 k_B T} = \frac{N \mu_0 g^2 \mu_B^2 J(J+1)}{3 k_B T}$$

Comparing with $\chi = C/T$, we identify:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{N \mu_0 g^2 \mu_B^2 J(J+1)}{3 k_B}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Molecular Field Constant λ

From $T_C = \lambda C$, solving for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{T_C}{C} = \frac{3 k_B T_C}{N \mu_0 g^2 \mu_B^2 J(J+1)}$$

For Iron (Fe) at $T_C = 1043$ K, with $J = 1$ and $g = 2$:

$$\lambda_{Fe} \approx \frac{3 (1.38 \times 10^{-23})(1043)}{N (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(2)^2 (9.27 \times 10^{-24})^2 (1)(2)}$$

$$\lambda_{Fe} \approx 5 \times 10^3 \text{ (in SI units of mol. field const.)}$$

This enormous value (equivalent to a molecular field of $\sim 1000$ Tesla) reveals that the **molecular field is not a true magnetic field** — it is a quantum mechanical exchange interaction.

---

## 3.4 Critical Exponent Analysis (Near T_C)

For $T \to T_C^-$, the spontaneous magnetization vanishes as:

$$M(T) \propto \left(\frac{T_C - T}{T_C}\right)^{\beta}$$

- **Mean-field prediction**: $\beta = 1/2$
- **Experimental (3D Heisenberg)**: $\beta \approx 0.33$
- **Experimental (3D Ising)**: $\beta \approx 0.325$

Similarly, the susceptibility diverges:

$$\chi(T) \propto \begin{cases} (T - T_C)^{-\gamma}, & T > T_C \\ (T_C - T)^{-\gamma'}, & T < T_C \end{cases}$$

with $\gamma \approx 1.24$ (3D Heisenberg) and $\gamma_{MF} = 1$ (mean-field).

> [!NOTE]
> The mean-field theory (Weiss) gives $\beta = 1/2$ and $\gamma = 1$, which is qualitatively correct but quantitatively inaccurate near $T_C$. Modern renormalization group theory provides the correct critical exponents.

---

## 3.5 Antiferromagnet Temperature Dependence

For antiferromagnets, the molecular field experienced by sublattice A from sublattice B is **negative**:

$$H_A^{mol} = -\lambda M_B, \quad H_B^{mol} = -\lambda M_A$$

The Néel temperature emerges as:

$$T_N = \lambda C$$

and the susceptibility above $T_N$ follows:

$$\chi = \frac{C}{T + \theta}, \quad \theta = T_N \quad (\text{positive value in formula})$$

**Below $T_N$**, the susceptibility of an antiferromagnet is **anisotropic**:
- Parallel to spin axis: $\chi_\parallel = \chi_\perp(T_N) \cdot (1 + \cos 2\phi)$ [constant]
- Perpendicular to spin axis: $\chi_\perp \propto T$ [increases linearly with T]

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Thermal Magnetic Behavior Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Magnetic Material at T=0] --> B{Exchange vs Thermal Energy}
    B --> C[Strong Exchange J > kT]
    B --> D[Thermal kT > Exchange J]
    C --> E[Ordered Phase]
    D --> F[Disordered Paramagnetic Phase]
    E --> G{Spin Alignment Type}
    G --> H[Ferrromagnetic Tc]
    G --> I[Antiferromagnetic TN]
    G --> J[Ferrimagnetic Tcomp]
    H --> K[Below Tc Parallel Spins]
    I --> L[Below TN Antiparallel Spins]
    J --> M[Below Tcomp Unbalanced Antiparallel]
    K --> N[M = M0 1 minus alpha T3/2]
    L --> O[Chi parallel constant, Chi perp increases]
    M --> P[Magnetization Minimum at Tcomp]
    N --> Q[T approaches Tc]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Order Disappears at Critical T]
    R --> S[Chi = C over T minus theta Curie Weiss]
    S --> T[Paramagnetic Phase Stable]
    T --> U[Spin Randomization Dominant]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Curie-Weiss Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[Sample Material] --> Proc1[Apply External Field H]
    Proc1 --> Meas1[Measure Magnetization M vs T]
    Meas1 --> Calc1[Compute Susceptibility Chi]
    Calc1 --> Fit1[Fit to Chi = C over T minus theta]
    Fit1 --> Ext1[Extract Curie Constant C]
    Fit1 --> Ext2[Extract Weiss Constant theta]
    Ext1 --> Cross1{C > 0?}
    Ext2 --> Cross2{theta > 0?}
    Cross1 --> Cross2
    Cross2 --> Yes1[Ferromagnetic Candidate]
    Cross2 --> No1[Antiferromagnetic Candidate]
    Yes1 --> Output1[Tc = theta identified]
    No1 --> Output2[TN = minus theta identified]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic: M(T) Behavior of Different Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Plot M vs T] --> Branch{Material Class}
    Branch --> FerroPath[Ferromagnetic]
    Branch --> AntiferroPath[Antiferromagnetic]
    Branch --> FerriPath[Ferrimagnetic]
    FerroPath --> F1[M drops to zero at TC]
    FerroPath --> F2[Above TC: Paramagnetic]
    AntiferroPath --> AF1[Susceptibility peak at TN]
    AntiferroPath --> AF2[Above TN: Curie Weiss with negative theta]
    FerriPath --> FR1[Compensation point Tcomp]
    FerriPath --> FR2[M can momentarily reach zero before TC]
    F1 --> Result[Compare to M0 1 minus T over TC power beta]
    AF1 --> Result
    FR1 --> Result
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above render as **block-level functional architectures** illustrating the decision logic of magnetic phase transitions, rather than attempting to draw the actual $M(T)$ curves (which would require graphical plots). The diagrams serve as **mental maps** for the KTU exam — drawing such a flowchart in your answer script can earn partial credit even if numerical work has errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define Curie Temperature. How does the susceptibility of a ferromagnetic material behave just above the Curie temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Curie Temperature ($T_C$)** is defined as the critical temperature above which a ferromagnetic material loses its spontaneous magnetization and transitions to a paramagnetic state. At $T_C$, the thermal agitation energy ($k_B T_C$) overcomes the exchange interaction energy that aligns adjacent atomic magnetic moments.

Just **above** the Curie temperature, the magnetic susceptibility follows the **Curie-Weiss Law**:

$$\chi = \frac{C}{T - T_C}$$

where $C$ is the Curie constant. As $T \to T_C^+$ from above, $\chi \to \infty$, indicating a **second-order phase transition**. This divergent behavior allows precise experimental determination of $T_C$ via AC susceptibility measurements.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Distinguish between Curie temperature and Néel temperature. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Curie Temperature ($T_C$) | Néel Temperature ($T_N$) |
|---------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| Material | Ferromagnetic / Ferrimagnetic | Antiferromagnetic |
| Order below | Spontaneous magnetization | Antiparallel spin ordering |
| Susceptibility above | $\chi = C/(T - T_C)$ | $\chi = C/(T + T_N)$ |
| Example | Iron ($T_C = 1043$ K) | MnO ($T_N = 116$ K) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Curie-Weiss law for the magnetic susceptibility of a ferromagnetic material above the Curie temperature using the Weiss molecular field concept. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ferromagnetic material has $T_C = 750$ K, with the following parameters: $N = 8.5 \times 10^{28}$ atoms/m³, $J = 1/2$, $g = 2$. Calculate: (i) The Curie constant $C$, and (ii) The molecular field constant $\lambda$. Comment on the magnitude of $\lambda$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** — Refer to the detailed derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The key steps are:
- Langevin classical paramagnetic susceptibility: $\chi = N \mu_0 \mu^2 / (3 k_B T)$
- Weiss effective field: $H_{eff} = H + \lambda M$
- Substitution and rearrangement
- Final result: $\chi = C/(T - \theta)$ with $T_C = \lambda C$

**Assumptions**: (i) Each spin experiences an effective field proportional to macroscopic magnetization, (ii) $T > T_C$ so thermal energy dominates, (iii) Spins are non-interacting except through the mean molecular field.

**(b) [7 Marks]** — Numerical Calculation:

**Given**: $T_C = 750$ K, $N = 8.5 \times 10^{28}$ m⁻³, $J = 1/2$, $g = 2$

**Step 1**: Compute $J(J+1) = (1/2)(3/2) = 3/4$

**Step 2**: Apply Curie constant formula:

$$C = \frac{N \mu_0 g^2 \mu_B^2 J(J+1)}{3 k_B}$$

$$C = \frac{(8.5 \times 10^{28})(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(2)^2 (9.27 \times 10^{-24})^2 (3/4)}{3 (1.38 \times 10^{-23})}$$

**Step 3**: Calculate numerator:
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6}$ T·m/A
- $g^2 = 4$
- $\mu_B^2 = (9.27 \times 10^{-24})^2 = 8.595 \times 10^{-47}$ J²/T²
- $J(J+1) = 0.75$
- Numerator $= (8.5 \times 10^{28})(1.2566 \times 10^{-6})(4)(8.595 \times 10^{-47})(0.75)$
- Numerator $\approx 2.755 \times 10^{-23}$

**Step 4**: Calculate denominator:
- $3 k_B = 3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 4.14 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K

**Step 5**: Final answer for $C$:

$$C = \frac{2.755 \times 10^{-23}}{4.14 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 0.665 \text{ (SI units)}$$

**Step 6**: Molecular field constant:

$$\lambda = \frac{T_C}{C} = \frac{750}{0.665} \approx 1127.8 \text{ (m·kg/s²·A² in appropriate SI)}$$

**Step 7**: Comment on magnitude: The value $\lambda \approx 1128$ is **enormously large** — corresponding to a molecular field of $\sim 1000$ Tesla. This confirms that the molecular field is **not a true magnetic dipole field**, but rather a quantum mechanical **exchange interaction** arising from the Coulomb repulsion and Pauli exclusion principle (Heisenberg, 1928).

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the temperature dependence of spontaneous magnetization in ferromagnetic materials in (i) the low-temperature regime and (ii) near the Curie temperature. Include relevant equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the Néel temperature? Compare the temperature dependence of susceptibility of an antiferromagnetic material below and above $T_N$ with appropriate diagrams.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** — Temperature Regimes of Ferromagnetic Magnetization:

**(i) Low-Temperature Regime (0 K < T < 0.5 T_C)**:

At low temperatures, the magnetization is well-approximated by **Bloch's $T^{3/2}$ Law**:

$$M(T) = M(0)\left[1 - \alpha T^{3/2}\right]$$

This law arises from the excitation of **spin waves (magnons)** — collective excitations of the spin lattice. The $T^{3/2}$ dependence comes from the magnon density of states in 3D, $D(\omega) \propto \omega^2$. The constant $\alpha$ is related to the spin-wave stiffness $D$ as:

$$\alpha = \frac{0.0587}{S}\left(\frac{k_B}{D}\right)^{3/2}$$

where $S$ is the spin quantum number. For iron at low T, $\alpha \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻³/².

**(ii) Near the Curie Temperature ($T \to T_C^-$)**:

The magnetization vanishes according to a **power law**:

$$M(T) = M(0)\left[\frac{T_C - T}{T_C}\right]^{\beta}$$

- **Mean-field (Weiss) prediction**: $\beta = 1/2$
- **Experimental value (3D Heisenberg)**: $\beta \approx 0.33$

This is a **continuous (second-order) phase transition**. The magnetization curve is **vertical** in slope at $T = T_C$ and shows a smooth approach to zero. The mean-field theory overestimates $\beta$ because it ignores **spin fluctuations** that become important near $T_C$.

**Comparison Table**:
| Regime | Power Law | Exponent | Origin |
|--------|-----------|----------|--------|
| $T \to 0$ | $T^{3/2}$ | Bloch | Magnon excitations |
| $T \to T_C$ | $(T_C - T)^{\beta}$ | $\beta = 0.33$ | Critical fluctuations |

**(b) [7 Marks]** — Néel Temperature and Antiferromagnetic Susceptibility:

**Definition**: The **Néel Temperature ($T_N$)** is the critical temperature below which an antiferromagnetic material exhibits long-range antiparallel spin ordering of equal magnitude, and above which it becomes paramagnetic.

**Above $T_N$**: The susceptibility follows the modified Curie-Weiss law:

$$\chi = \frac{C}{T + T_N}$$

The Weiss constant $\theta = -T_N$ is **negative**, reflecting the antiferromagnetic exchange interaction $J < 0$.

**At $T = T_N$**: $\chi$ reaches a **cusp-like maximum** — this is the signature experimental feature used to identify $T_N$.

**Below $T_N$**: The susceptibility becomes **anisotropic**:
- $\chi_\parallel$ (parallel to spin axis) = constant
- $\chi_\perp$ (perpendicular to spin axis) = increases as $T \to 0$

For a polycrystalline sample, an average is observed:

$$\chi_{poly} = \frac{\chi_\parallel + 2\chi_\perp}{3}$$

**Diagrammatic Description** (text-based representation for answer script):

```
Chi (susceptibility) ↑                    Peak at TN
              |                  /\
              |                 /  \
              |                /    \
              |   Chi perp    /      \    Chi parallel (constant)
              |             /        \___________
              |____________/_________________________→ T
                          TN        →0
```

The $\chi$ vs $T$ curve has a **maximum at $T_N$** and decreases on either side. Above $T_N$, $\chi$ follows $C/(T+T_N)$ and decreases monotonically. Below $T_N$, the perpendicular component rises while the parallel component stays flat.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Curie-Weiss**: Students frequently write $\chi = C/(T - T_C)$ for antiferromagnets above $T_N$. **CORRECT** formula is $\chi = C/(T + T_N)$ since the exchange $J$ is negative.
> 2. **Confusing $T_C$ with $T_N$**: Always verify the material type before applying the formula.
> 3. **Forgetting $\mu_0$**: The SI form of Curie constant requires $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A. CGS unit confusion leads to factor-of-4π errors.
> 4. **Misidentifying the Susceptibility Exponent**: Near $T_C$, $\chi \sim (T - T_C)^{-\gamma}$ with $\gamma = 1.24$ (not 1.0). Mean-field gives $\gamma = 1$, which is **wrong** experimentally.
> 5. **Mixing Up Bloch Law and Critical Power Law**: Bloch's $T^{3/2}$ law is valid **only at low T**, NOT near $T_C$. KTU answers that conflate these are penalized.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Temperature Dependent Magnetization**

- **Curie Temperature $T_C$**: Temperature at which ferromagnet → paramagnet transition occurs
- **Néel Temperature $T_N$**: Temperature at which antiferromagnet → paramagnet transition occurs
- **Curie-Weiss Law** (Ferro, $T > T_C$): $\chi = C/(T - T_C)$
- **Modified Curie-Weiss** (Antiferro, $T > T_N$): $\chi = C/(T + T_N)$
- **Curie Constant**: $C = N \mu_0 g^2 \mu_B^2 J(J+1) / (3 k_B)$
- **Molecular Field**: $H_{mol} = \lambda M$; $\lambda$ is a quantum mechanical exchange constant, NOT a true field
- **Relation**: $T_C = \lambda C$ (ferromagnet), $T_N = \lambda C$ in magnitude (antiferromagnet)
- **Bloch's Law** (low T): $M(T) = M(0)[1 - \alpha T^{3/2}]$; from magnon excitations
- **Critical Power Law** (near $T_C$): $M(T) \propto (T_C - T)^{\beta}$ with $\beta \approx 0.33$
- **Susceptibility Divergence**: At $T = T_C$, $\chi \to \infty$ — second-order phase transition
- **Engineering Relevance**: Magnetic refrigeration (magnetocaloric effect at $T_C$), Curie switches, MRI contrast agents, hard disk recording heads
- **Mnemonic**: "**F**erromagnet = **C**urie, **A**ntiferromagnet = **N**éel" (F-C, A-N)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Dependent Magnetization, Measurements Using AC Susceptibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Time Dependent Magnetization, Measurements Using AC Susceptibility # MODULE 4: TIME-DEPENDENT MAGNETIZATION & AC SUSCEPTIBILITY ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Time-Dependent Magnetization — Formal Definition In real magnetic materials, the response of magnetization **M(t)** to an applied magnetic field **H(t)** is not instantaneous. When the magnetic field is changed abruptly, the magnetization takes a finite, measurable time to reach its new equilibrium value. This phenomenon is termed **Time-Dependent Magnetization** (or **Magnetic After-Effect / Magnetic Relaxation**). Mathematically, if a small step field $H_0$ is applied at $t = 0$, the magnetization evolves as: $$M(t) = M_{\text{eq}} + \Delta M \cdot f(t)$$ where $M_{\text{eq}}$ is the equilibrium magnetization and $f(t)$ is a decay function. The characteristic time constant $\tau$ governing this evolution is called the **relaxation time**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEPET412 Module 4** > Time-dependent magnetization is the *physical root cause* behind frequency-dependent AC susceptibility. Without understanding relaxation, the dispersive ($\chi'$) and absorptive ($\chi''$) responses of a material cannot be interpreted. ### 1.2 AC Susceptibility — Formal Definition **AC Susceptibility** ($\chi_{\text{ac}}$) is defined as the complex, frequency-dependent linear response coefficient relating the time-varying magnetization $M(t)$ to a small sinusoidal driving field $H(t) = H_0 \cos(\omega t)$. For a small alternating field of amplitude $H_0$ and angular frequency $\omega$: $$H(t) = H_0 \cos(\omega t)$$ The steady-state magnetization response is sinusoidal but generally **phase-shifted** by an angle $\delta$ relative to the driving field: $$M(t) = M_0 \cos(\omega t - \delta)$$ The complex susceptibility is therefore defined as: $$\chi_{\text{ac}}(\omega) = \chi'(\omega) - i\,\chi''(\omega)$$ where: - $\chi'(\omega)$ — the **in-phase (real) component** (dispersive response, energy storage) - $\chi''(\omega)$ — the **out-of-phase (imaginary) component** (absorptive response, energy dissipation) - $i = \sqrt{-1}$ > [!NOTE] > The sign convention $\chi_{\text{ac}} = \chi' - i\chi''$ is the physics convention used in KTU-recommended texts on magnetism. Engineering texts may use $+i\chi''$; both are equivalent provided the time-dependence convention $e^{-i\omega t}$ is consistent. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heavy Boat & Wavy Water" Picture Imagine a small boat floating on a wavy sea: | Element in Boat Analogy | Magnetic Counterpart | |---|---| | Wavy water (oscillating up-down) | AC magnetic field $H(t)$ | | Boat (responding to waves) | Magnetization $M(t)$ | | Boat's vertical position (in-step with waves) | In-phase component $\chi'(\omega)$ | | Boat's rocking lag behind the wave | Out-of-phase component $\chi''(\omega)$ | If the waves are **slow** (low frequency, $\omega \to 0$), the boat easily follows the wave — the response is large and in-phase ($\chi' \approx \chi_{\text{dc}}$, $\chi'' \approx 0$). If the waves are **fast** (high frequency, $\omega \to \infty$), the boat cannot keep up — it lags behind, and energy is dissipated as the boat pitches against the wave's restoring force ($\chi'$ decreases, $\chi''$ peaks). > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for Exams:** "**Low-ω → Big χ' (storage); High-ω → Big χ'' (loss).**" This single mnemonic answers most conceptual questions on AC susceptibility. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Vacuum permeability:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T·m/A}$ - **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ - **Gyromagnetic ratio (electron):** $\gamma_e = -1.7608 \times 10^{11} \text{ rad/(s·T)}$ - **Typical measurement frequency range:** $\mathbf{10\,\text{Hz} \leq f \leq 10\,\text{kHz}}$ (most common); extended systems reach **mHz to MHz**. - **Typical AC field amplitude:** $\mathbf{0.1\,\text{Oe} \leq H_0 \leq 10\,\text{Oe}}$ (kept low to stay in the linear response regime). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Origins of Time-Dependent Magnetization Time-dependent magnetization arises from several microscopic mechanisms. For KTU-level analysis, the three most important are: ### A. Néel Relaxation (Single-Domain Particles) In a single-domain nanoparticle with magnetic anisotropy energy $E_A = K_u V \sin^2\theta$ (where $K_u$ is the anisotropy constant, $V$ is particle volume, $\theta$ is the angle between magnetization and easy axis), the magnetization can thermally hop over the anisotropy barrier $E_B = K_u V$. The characteristic relaxation time follows the **Néel–Arrhenius law**: $$\tau_N = \tau_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{E_B}{k_B T}\right) = \tau_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{K_u V}{k_B T}\right)$$ The attempt time $\tau_0 \approx 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-11}$ s. ### B. Domain Wall Motion (Bulk Ferromagnets) Pinning of domain walls at defects and grain boundaries produces time lags when the applied field changes. ### C. Spin-Glass / Cluster Glass Dynamics Below the freezing temperature $T_f$, magnetic moments freeze into disordered configurations, leading to slow, glassy relaxation over seconds to hours. > [!NOTE] > For superparamagnetic particles, when the measurement time $t_{\text{meas}} \approx \tau_N$, the system crosses the **Blocking Temperature** $T_B$, defined by $t_{\text{meas}} = \tau_0 \exp(K_u V / k_B T_B)$. ## 2.2 Linear Response Theory — Foundation of AC Susceptibility If we apply a small perturbation $\delta H(t)$ to a system initially at equilibrium, the linear response gives: $$\delta M(t) = \int_0^{\infty} R(\tau)\, \delta H(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$ where $R(\tau)$ is the **response function** (causal, $R(\tau<0) = 0$). The Fourier transform of $R(\tau)$ gives the complex susceptibility: $$\chi_{\text{ac}}(\omega) = \int_0^{\infty} R(\tau)\, e^{i\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$ This implies the **Kramers–Kronig relations** connecting the real and imaginary parts: $$\chi'(\omega) = \frac{1}{\pi}\,\mathcal{P}\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{\chi''(\omega')}{\omega' - \omega}\, d\omega'$$ $$\chi''(\omega) = -\frac{1}{\pi}\,\mathcal{P}\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{\chi'(\omega')}{\omega' - \omega}\, d\omega'$$ where $\mathcal{P}$ denotes the Cauchy principal value. ## 2.3 Debye Relaxation Model (Single-Time-Constant Case) For a system with a **single relaxation time** $\tau$ and DC susceptibility $\chi_0$ and high-frequency (adiabatic) susceptibility $\chi_\infty$, the response is: $$R(t) = \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{\tau}\, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad t \geq 0$$ The corresponding complex susceptibility is: $$\chi_{\text{ac}}(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + i\omega\tau}$$ Separating real and imaginary parts: $$\chi'(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}$$ $$\chi''(\omega) = \frac{(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)\,\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Debye Peak Property:** $\chi''(\omega)$ attains its maximum when $\omega\tau = 1$, i.e., at the **characteristic relaxation frequency** $\omega_c = 1/\tau$. This is the **single most exam-relevant result** in AC susceptibility. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Driving field | $H(t) = H_0 \cos(\omega t)$ | A/m or Oe | AC excitation | | 2 | Magnetization response | $M(t) = M_0 \cos(\omega t - \delta)$ | A/m | Phase-lagged output | | 3 | Complex susceptibility | $\chi_{\text{ac}} = \chi' - i\chi''$ | Dimensionless | Linear response | | 4 | Real part (Debye) | $\chi'(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \dfrac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}$ | — | Energy storage | | 5 | Imaginary part (Debye) | $\chi''(\omega) = \dfrac{(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}$ | — | Energy loss | | 6 | Peak condition | $\omega\tau = 1$ | — | $\chi''$ is maximum | | 7 | Néel–Arrhenius law | $\tau_N = \tau_0 \exp(K_u V / k_B T)$ | s | Thermal activation | | 8 | Blocking temperature | $T_B = \dfrac{K_u V}{k_B \ln(t_{\text{meas}}/\tau_0)}$ | K | Superparamagnetism limit | | 9 | Curie–Weiss law | $\chi_{\text{dc}} = C/(T - \theta)$ | — | Paramagnetic baseline | | 10 | Loss tangent | $\tan\delta = \chi''/\chi'$ | — | Dissipation ratio | | 11 | Permeability relation | $\mu(\omega) = \mu_0 \big[1 + \chi_{\text{ac}}(\omega)\big]$ | H/m | Linked to $\chi$ | | 12 | Frequency dispersion | $\Delta\chi' = \chi'_0 - \chi'_\infty$ | — | Susceptibility drop | > [!TIP] > Note the table above deliberately avoids vertical pipes inside math expressions (e.g., `\vert x \vert` style) so the markdown table renders cleanly in PDF/HTML exports. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of AC Susceptibility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Nanoparticle characterization** | Distinguishes superparamagnetic from blocked states via $T_B$ extraction | | **Biomedical diagnostics** | Magnetic nanoparticle hyperthermia dosimetry, MPI tracers | | **Earth & planetary science** | Identifying magnetic minerals (magnetite, pyrrhotite) in rocks | | **Spintronics & thin films** | Probing spin-pumping, FMR-related phenomena | | **Geochronology** | Paleomagnetic dating via Néel relaxation of single-domain grains | | **Materials quality control** | Verifying soft ferrites for transformer cores (loss tangent) | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Step Response to Frequency Response (Debye Model) ### Step 1 — Physical Setup Apply a small DC field $H_0$ at $t=0$. The magnetization $M(t)$ approaches equilibrium exponentially. The differential equation governing the relaxation is **first-order** with a single time constant $\tau$: $$\tau \frac{dM}{dt} + M(t) = \chi_0 H_0$$ where $\chi_0$ is the **isothermal (DC) susceptibility** (response on timescales $\gg \tau$) and $\tau$ is the relaxation time. ### Step 2 — Solve the Time-Domain Equation For constant $H_0$ and initial condition $M(0) = 0$: $$\frac{dM}{dt} + \frac{M}{\tau} = \frac{\chi_0 H_0}{\tau}$$ The integrating factor is $e^{t/\tau}$. Multiplying both sides: $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[M(t) e^{t/\tau}\right] = \frac{\chi_0 H_0}{\tau} e^{t/\tau}$$ Integrate from $0$ to $t$: $$M(t) e^{t/\tau} - M(0) = \frac{\chi_0 H_0}{\tau} \int_0^{t} e^{t'/\tau}\, dt'$$ $$M(t) = \chi_0 H_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$ This is the standard exponential saturation of magnetization. ### Step 3 — Switch to a Sinusoidal Drive Now let $H(t) = H_0 e^{i\omega t}$ (using the complex representation; physical $H = H_0 \cos(\omega t) = \Re\{H_0 e^{i\omega t}\}$). The ODE becomes: $$\tau \frac{dM}{dt} + M(t) = \chi_0 H_0 e^{i\omega t}$$ ### Step 4 — Seek a Steady-State Solution of the Form $M(t) = \chi(\omega) H_0 e^{i\omega t}$ Substituting: $$\tau \cdot i\omega \chi(\omega) H_0 e^{i\omega t} + \chi(\omega) H_0 e^{i\omega t} = \chi_0 H_0 e^{i\omega t}$$ Cancel $H_0 e^{i\omega t}$: $$\chi(\omega) \big(1 + i\omega\tau\big) = \chi_0$$ ### Step 5 — Include the Adiabatic Contribution $\chi_\infty$ For a fully general linear response (with non-zero $\chi_\infty$ at infinite frequency): $$\chi(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + i\omega\tau}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate $(1 - i\omega\tau)$: $$\chi(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \frac{(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)(1 - i\omega\tau)}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}$$ ### Step 6 — Separate Real and Imaginary Parts $$\chi'(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}$$ $$\chi''(\omega) = \frac{(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)\,\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}$$ This completes the **Debye derivation**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Locating the Maximum of $\chi''(\omega)$ ### Step 1 — Set Up the Optimization Differentiate $\chi''(\omega)$ with respect to $\omega$ and equate to zero: $$\frac{d\chi''}{d\omega} = (\chi_0 - \chi_\infty) \cdot \frac{d}{d\omega}\!\left[\frac{\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}\right] = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Derivative Using the quotient rule with $u = \omega\tau$ and $v = 1 + \omega^2 \tau^2$: $$\frac{d}{d\omega}\!\left[\frac{\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}\right] = \frac{\tau(1 + \omega^2 \tau^2) - \omega\tau \cdot 2\omega\tau^2}{(1 + \omega^2 \tau^2)^2}$$ Simplify the numerator: $$\tau + \omega^2 \tau^3 - 2\omega^2 \tau^3 = \tau - \omega^2 \tau^3 = \tau(1 - \omega^2 \tau^2)$$ ### Step 3 — Set Numerator to Zero and Solve $$\tau(1 - \omega^2 \tau^2) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega^2 \tau^2 = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\omega_c \tau = 1}$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate the Maximum Value Substitute $\omega\tau = 1$ into $\chi''(\omega)$: $$\chi''_{\max} = \frac{(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)(1)}{1 + 1} = \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Conclusion:** The peak of $\chi''(\omega)$ occurs at $\omega_c = 1/\tau$ with amplitude $(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)/2$. This is the standard Debye result quoted in KTU answer keys. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kramers–Kronig Consistency Check For the Debye $\chi'(\omega)$ and $\chi''(\omega)$, verify the relation $\chi'(\omega \to \infty) = \chi_\infty$. Compute: $$\lim_{\omega \to \infty} \chi'(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \lim_{\omega \to \infty} \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2} = \chi_\infty + 0 = \chi_\infty \quad\checkmark$$ Compute: $$\lim_{\omega \to 0} \chi'(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \chi_0 - \chi_\infty = \chi_0 \quad\checkmark$$ This confirms the **low-frequency limit equals DC susceptibility** — a required consistency for any valid AC susceptibility model. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Blocking Temperature from AC Susceptibility For an ensemble of single-domain particles with volume $V$ and anisotropy $K_u$, AC susceptibility measurements at multiple frequencies $f_i$ yield peak positions in $\chi''(T)$ at the blocking temperatures $T_B(f_i)$. Using Néel–Arrhenius: $$\tau_{\text{meas}}(f_i) = \frac{1}{2\pi f_i} = \tau_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{K_u V}{k_B T_B(f_i)}\right)$$ Take the natural logarithm: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi f_i}\right) = \ln\tau_0 + \frac{K_u V}{k_B T_B(f_i)}$$ A plot of $\ln(1/f_i)$ vs. $1/T_B$ is **linear**, with slope $K_u V / k_B$ and intercept $\ln\tau_0$. This is the standard **frequency-shift method** for extracting anisotropy energy from AC data. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Mechanism to AC Response | Material System | Dominant Relaxation Mechanism | $\chi'(T)$ Behavior | $\chi''(T)$ Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | Paramagnet | Curie's law; no relaxation | Curie–Weiss drop with T | Negligible (no loss) | | Superparamagnet (above $T_B$) | Néel hopping (fast) | Curie-like at $\omega\tau \ll 1$ | Weak, broad | | Blocked nanoparticle | Anisotropy barrier (slow) | Reduced, history-dependent | Peak near $T_B$ | | Spin glass | Broad distribution of $\tau$ | Plateau, then cusp at $T_f$ | Frequency-dependent cusp near $T_f$ | | Soft ferrite | Domain wall pinning | High $\chi'$ at low $f$ | Loss peak at MHz | | Hard ferromagnet | Domain wall + rotation | Small $\chi'$ | Small loss | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — AC Susceptometer Signal Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR Osc[Function Generator
1 Hz to 100 kHz
Sinusoidal H0 cos wt] DriveCoil[Primary Drive Coil
Produces AC Field H0] Sample[Sample Stage
Inside Cryostat
10 K to 400 K] PickupA[Pickup Coil A
Balanced Configuration] PickupB[Pickup Coil B
Counter-Wound for Null] Preamp[Low-Noise Preamplifier
Gain 10 to 1000] Lockin[Digital Lock-In Amplifier
Reference at frequency w] Phase[Phase Detector
Output: X in-phase, Y quad] DAQ[Data Acquisition
X, Y vs T, f] CPU[Computer Analysis
Fit to Debye Model] Osc --> DriveCoil DriveCoil --> Sample Sample --> PickupA Sample --> PickupB PickupA --> Preamp PickupB --> Preamp Preamp --> Lockin Osc --> Lockin Lockin --> Phase Phase --> DAQ DAQ --> CPU ``` > [!NOTE] > The pickup coils A and B are wound in opposition. When no sample is present, the induced voltages cancel exactly (null). The sample breaks the symmetry, producing a net signal proportional to $M(t)$. This is the standard **astatic (compensated) coil design**. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Data Reduction: From Raw Voltages to $\chi'$ and $\chi''$ ```mermaid flowchart TD RawV[Raw Lock-In Outputs
X raw, Y raw] Calib[Calibration Constant K
From Reference Sample
Units: emu per V] Mass[Sample Mass m, Density rho] FieldCal[H0 from Pickup Calibration
in A per m or Oe] ChiPrime[Compute chi prime
chi prime = K times X divided by H0m] ChiDouble[Compute chi double prime
chi double prime = K times Y divided by H0m] SweepTemp[Temperature Sweep
2 K to 400 K] SweepFreq[Frequency Sweep
10 Hz to 10 kHz] Plot2D[2D Plot
chi prime, chi double prime vs T at fixed f] DebyeFit[Fit Debye Model
Extract tau, chi 0, chi infinity] RawV --> Calib Calib --> ChiPrime Calib --> ChiDouble FieldCal --> ChiPrime FieldCal --> ChiDouble Mass --> ChiPrime Mass --> ChiDouble SweepTemp --> Plot2D SweepFreq --> Plot2D Plot2D --> DebyeFit ``` ## 4.3 Physical Concept Map — Why Time Dependence Forces Two Susceptibilities ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Magnetization M has finite relaxation time tau] SlowDr[At low frequency: omega tau less than 1
M follows H in phase] FastDr[At high frequency: omega tau greater than 1
M lags behind H] InPhase[In-Phase Component chi prime
Energy Stored Reversibly] OutPhase[Out-of-Phase Component chi double prime
Energy Dissipated as Heat] Debye[Debye Equation:
chi prime chi infinity plus chi0 minus chi inf over 1 plus omega squared tau squared] Debye2[Debye Equation:
chi double prime equals chi0 minus chi inf times omega tau over 1 plus omega squared tau squared] Cross[Kramers-Kronig Relations
Couple chi prime and chi double prime] Start --> SlowDr Start --> FastDr SlowDr --> InPhase FastDr --> OutPhase InPhase --> Debye OutPhase --> Debye2 Debye --> Cross Debye2 --> Cross ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Lock-In Amplifier Principle (How X and Y Are Extracted) ```mermaid flowchart LR Sig[Sample Signal
Vs t equals A cos wt minus delta] Mix1[Multiplier 1
Reference cos wt] Mix2[Multiplier 2
Reference sin wt] LPF1[Low-Pass Filter 1
Time Constant 100 ms] LPF2[Low-Pass Filter 2
Time Constant 100 ms] Xout[X Output
Equals A cos delta
Proportional to chi prime] Yout[Y Output
Equals A sin delta
Proportional to chi double prime] Sig --> Mix1 Sig --> Mix2 Mix1 --> LPF1 Mix2 --> LPF2 LPF1 --> Xout LPF2 --> Yout ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The trigonometric identity used here is: > $$A\cos(\omega t - \delta)\cos(\omega t) = \tfrac{A}{2}\cos\delta + \tfrac{A}{2}\cos(2\omega t - \delta)$$ > The high-frequency term is removed by the low-pass filter, leaving $X = A\cos\delta$. Similarly, multiplying by $\sin(\omega t)$ yields $Y = A\sin\delta$. Thus, $\tan\delta = Y/X = \chi''/\chi'$. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **Define complex AC susceptibility. What do its real and imaginary components represent physically?** **Model Answer:** AC susceptibility $\chi_{\text{ac}}(\omega) = \chi'(\omega) - i\,\chi''(\omega)$ describes the linear response of a material's magnetization $M$ to a small sinusoidal magnetic field $H = H_0\cos(\omega t)$. The **real part $\chi'(\omega)$** represents the **in-phase component**, corresponding to reversible energy storage. The **imaginary part $\chi''(\omega)$** represents the **out-of-phase component**, corresponding to irreversible energy dissipation (loss). Together they fully characterize how a magnetic material behaves at a given frequency. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **State the Néel–Arrhenius law and explain the concept of blocking temperature $T_B$.** **Model Answer:** The Néel–Arrhenius law gives the relaxation time for the magnetization of a single-domain particle over its anisotropy barrier $E_B = K_u V$: $$\tau_N = \tau_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{K_u V}{k_B T}\right)$$ The **blocking temperature** $T_B$ is defined as the temperature at which the relaxation time equals the experimental measurement time $t_{\text{meas}}$: $$T_B = \frac{K_u V}{k_B \ln(t_{\text{meas}}/\tau_0)}$$ Below $T_B$, the particle is magnetically "blocked" (stable); above $T_B$, it is **superparamagnetic** (thermal fluctuations randomize $M$). --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Derive the Debye equations for the complex susceptibility $\chi_{\text{ac}}(\omega) = \chi'(\omega) - i\,\chi''(\omega)$ of a system with a single relaxation time $\tau$. Start from the first-order relaxation ODE. **[(7 Marks)]** **(b)** A paramagnetic sample has $\chi_0 = 0.05$, $\chi_\infty = 0.001$, and a single relaxation time $\tau = 10^{-3}$ s. Calculate the frequency at which $\chi''$ peaks, the peak value of $\chi''$, and the value of $\chi'$ at that frequency. **[(7 Marks)]** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Governing Equation (1 Mark):** For a small driving field $H(t)$, the magnetization relaxes exponentially. The first-order linear ODE is: $$\tau\,\frac{dM}{dt} + M(t) = \chi_0 H(t)$$ **Step 2 — Apply Sinusoidal Drive (1 Mark):** Let $H(t) = H_0 e^{i\omega t}$ and seek $M(t) = \chi(\omega) H_0 e^{i\omega t}$. Substituting: $$\tau \cdot i\omega\,\chi(\omega) H_0 e^{i\omega t} + \chi(\omega) H_0 e^{i\omega t} = \chi_0 H_0 e^{i\omega t}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\chi(\omega)$ (1 Mark):** $$\chi(\omega)\,(1 + i\omega\tau) = \chi_0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \chi(\omega) = \frac{\chi_0}{1 + i\omega\tau}$$ **Step 4 — Include Adiabatic Term $\chi_\infty$ (1 Mark):** The general form admits a non-zero high-frequency limit: $$\chi(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + i\omega\tau}$$ **Step 5 — Rationalize the Denominator (1 Mark):** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 - i\omega\tau)$: $$\chi(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \frac{(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)(1 - i\omega\tau)}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}$$ **Step 6 — Separate Real and Imaginary Parts (2 Marks):** $$\boxed{\chi'(\omega) = \chi_\infty + \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}}$$ $$\boxed{\chi''(\omega) = \frac{(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)\,\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2}}$$ #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $\chi_0 = 0.05$, $\chi_\infty = 0.001$, $\tau = 10^{-3}$ s. **Step 1 — Peak Frequency (2 Marks):** The peak in $\chi''$ occurs at $\omega_c \tau = 1$: $$\omega_c = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}\,\text{s}} = 10^3 \text{ rad/s}$$ In Hz: $f_c = \omega_c/(2\pi) = 159.15\,\text{Hz}$ **Step 2 — Peak Value of $\chi''$ (3 Marks):** At $\omega\tau = 1$: $$\chi''_{\max} = \frac{(\chi_0 - \chi_\infty)(1)}{1 + 1} = \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{2} = \frac{0.05 - 0.001}{2} = \frac{0.049}{2} = 0.0245$$ **Step 3 — Value of $\chi'$ at $\omega_c$ (2 Marks):** $$\chi'(\omega_c) = \chi_\infty + \frac{\chi_0 - \chi_\infty}{1 + 1} = 0.001 + \frac{0.049}{2} = 0.001 + 0.0245 = 0.0255$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** > A common mistake is using $f_c = 1/\tau$ instead of $\omega_c = 1/\tau$. The Debye formula uses **angular frequency** $\omega$ (in rad/s), not $f$ (in Hz). If the question asks for "frequency" without specifying, give both values: $\omega_c = 10^3$ rad/s and $f_c \approx 159$ Hz. **2 marks are routinely lost here.** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram the experimental setup for measuring AC susceptibility. Explain the role of the lock-in amplifier. **[(7 Marks)]** **(b)** The Néel–Arrhenius relaxation times for a nanoparticle sample are measured at three frequencies. The blocking temperatures are: $T_B(10\,\text{Hz}) = 25\,\text{K}$, $T_B(100\,\text{Hz}) = 32\,\text{K}$, $T_B(1000\,\text{Hz}) = 41\,\text{K}$. Determine the anisotropy energy $K_u V$ and the attempt time $\tau_0$, assuming $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **[(7 Marks)]** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** The complete setup consists of: 1. **Function Generator** — produces reference sinusoidal voltage at frequency $f$ (1 Hz to 100 kHz). 2. **Drive Coil (Primary)** — converts voltage into AC magnetic field $H(t) = H_0 \cos(\omega t)$ at the sample position. 3. **Sample Stage** — held in a cryostat for temperature control (e.g., 2 K to 400 K). 4. **Compensated Pickup Coils (Secondary A & B)** — wound in opposition; sample-induced voltage imbalance is the signal. 5. **Preamplifier** — low-noise voltage amplification (gain 10 to 1000). 6. **Lock-In Amplifier** — extracts the in-phase ($X$) and quadrature ($Y$) components of the signal using the reference frequency. 7. **Data Acquisition and Computer** — records $X$, $Y$ vs. $T$ and $f$. **Role of Lock-In Amplifier (4 Marks):** The lock-in amplifier multiplies the preamplified signal by two reference waveforms: $\cos(\omega t)$ and $\sin(\omega t)$. After low-pass filtering, it produces: $$X = A\cos\delta \propto \chi'(\omega)$$ $$Y = A\sin\delta \propto \chi''(\omega)$$ This **phase-sensitive detection** technique: - Extracts signals buried in noise (rejection up to 100 dB above noise floor). - Directly yields the **complex susceptibility** without Fourier transform. - Provides the **phase angle** $\delta$ between $M$ and $H$ from $\tan\delta = Y/X = \chi''/\chi'$. #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Linear Form of Arrhenius Equation (2 Marks):** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi f_i}\right) = \ln\tau_0 + \frac{K_u V}{k_B T_B(f_i)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Left-Hand Side for Each Frequency (2 Marks):** For $f_1 = 10$ Hz, $T_B = 25$ K: $\ln(1/2\pi \cdot 10) = \ln(0.01592) = -4.140$ For $f_2 = 100$ Hz, $T_B = 32$ K: $\ln(1/2\pi \cdot 100) = \ln(0.001592) = -6.442$ For $f_3 = 1000$ Hz, $T_B = 41$ K: $\ln(1/2\pi \cdot 1000) = \ln(1.592 \times 10^{-4}) = -8.745$ **Step 3 — Compute Right-Hand Side Coefficients (1 Mark):** Compute $1/T_B$ (in K$^{-1}$): $1/25 = 0.0400$, $1/32 = 0.03125$, $1/41 = 0.02439$ **Step 4 — Linear Regression (2 Marks):** Fit $y = a + b x$ where $y = \ln(1/2\pi f)$ and $x = 1/T_B$. Using two-point slope from points $(0.0400, -4.140)$ and $(0.02439, -8.745)$: $$b = \frac{-4.140 - (-8.745)}{0.0400 - 0.02439} = \frac{4.605}{0.01561} = 295\,\text{K}$$ Therefore: $$K_u V = b \cdot k_B = 295 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 4.07 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{J}$$ **Step 5 — Intercept (for verification):** $$\ln\tau_0 = y - b x = -4.140 - 295 \times 0.0400 = -4.140 - 11.80 = -15.94$$ $$\tau_0 = e^{-15.94} \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s}$$ > [!NOTE] > The extracted $\tau_0 \sim 10^{-7}$ s is somewhat larger than the textbook canonical value of $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-11}$ s, suggesting non-ideal Néel behavior (e.g., distribution of particle sizes or collective effects). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** > Students often forget that the right-hand side of the Arrhenius equation uses $T_B$ (blocking temperature), **not the measurement temperature**. Also, the natural logarithm argument must be the **time period** $1/(2\pi f)$, not $1/f$. Both errors cost 1 to 2 marks routinely. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Time-dependent magnetization** arises when the system's response time $\tau$ is comparable to the measurement time.
