<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define Electron Spin Resonance. State the resonance condition for ESR.**

**Model Answer:**

Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) is a branch of absorption spectroscopy in which electromagnetic radiation (typically in the microwave region, ~ 9–10 GHz) is absorbed by paramagnetic species (containing unpaired electrons) placed in a strong external magnetic field, causing transitions between Zeeman-split spin energy levels.

**Resonance condition:**

$$h\nu = g_e \mu_B B_0$$

where $h$ is Planck's constant, $\nu$ is the microwave frequency, $g_e$ is the electron g-factor (~ 2.0023), $\mu_B$ is the Bohr magneton, and $B_0$ is the applied magnetic field.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`
**What are the selection rules for ESR transitions? Why is the g-factor useful in ESR analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

The magnetic dipole selection rule for ESR is:

$$\Delta m_s = \pm 1$$

That is, only transitions between adjacent Zeeman sub-levels (e.g., $m_s = -\frac{1}{2} \to m_s = +\frac{1}{2}$) are allowed.

**Utility of the g-factor:** The g-factor acts as a **fingerprint** of the paramagnetic center. The free electron has $g_e = 2.0023$. Deviations from this value (and anisotropy in solid samples) reveal the chemical environment, oxidation state, and local symmetry around the unpaired electron, enabling identification of the species.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of Electron Spin Resonance spectroscopy. Derive the resonance condition and calculate the magnetic field required for resonance at a microwave frequency of 9.5 GHz. Given: $g_e = 2.0023$, $\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ J/T, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the instrumentation of an ESR spectrometer with the help of a block diagram. Explain the function of the resonant cavity and field modulation.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Principle:** ESR is based on the absorption of microwave radiation by unpaired electrons in a paramagnetic material placed in a magnetic field. The applied field lifts the degeneracy of electron spin states (Zeeman splitting), and a resonant absorption occurs when the microwave photon energy matches the energy gap.

**Derivation:**

The Zeeman Hamiltonian for an electron in field $B_0$ is:

$$\hat{H}_{Zeeman} = g_e \mu_B B_0 \frac{\hat{S}_z}{\hbar}$$

Eigenvalues: $E_{m_s} = g_e \mu_B B_0 m_s$

For $m_s = +\frac{1}{2}$ and $m_s = -\frac{1}{2}$:

$$E_{+1/2} = +\frac{1}{2} g_e \mu_B B_0$$
$$E_{-1/2} = -\frac{1}{2} g_e \mu_B B_0$$

Energy gap:

$$\Delta E = g_e \mu_B B_0$$

Resonance condition:

$$h\nu = g_e \mu_B B_0$$

**Calculation:**

$$B_0 = \frac{h\nu}{g_e \mu_B} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(9.5 \times 10^{9})}{(2.0023)(9.274 \times 10^{-24})}$$

$$B_0 = \frac{6.295 \times 10^{-24}}{1.857 \times 10^{-23}} = 0.339 \text{ T} = 3390 \text{ Gauss}$$

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Instrumentation Block Diagram:**

```
Klystron (MW source) → Attenuator → Circulator → Resonant Cavity (sample)
                                       ↑                    ↓
                                  Isolator            Detector (Crystal Diode)
                                       ↑                    ↓
                              Field Modulation     Lock-in Amplifier
                              Coils (100 kHz)              ↓
                                       ↑              Recorder/Display
                                  Electromagnet
                                  (with Hall probe)
```

**Key Components and Functions:**

1. **Klystron (Microwave Source):** Generates stable monochromatic microwaves (e.g., 9.5 GHz for X-band). Modern systems use Gunn diodes.

2. **Attenuator:** Controls the microwave power reaching the cavity to avoid saturation.

3. **Circulator:** A non-reciprocal 3-port device that directs microwaves: source → cavity → detector, isolating the source from reflected waves.

4. **Resonant Cavity (Heart of ESR):** A high-Q (~ 10,000) metal box (rectangular TE₁₀₂ or cylindrical TE₀₁₁ mode) where the sample is placed at the position of maximum microwave magnetic field $B_1$. At resonance, the cavity stores microwave energy, dramatically enhancing sensitivity.

5. **Field Modulation Coils:** Apply a small sinusoidal magnetic field (~ 1 Gauss, 100 kHz) on top of $B_0$. This enables **phase-sensitive detection** via lock-in amplifier, improving signal-to-noise ratio by ~ 1000×.

6. **Detector (Crystal Diode):** Rectifies the microwave signal reflected from the cavity; output is proportional to cavity impedance changes during resonance.

7. **Electromagnet + Hall Probe:** Provides a stable, swept $B_0$ field (0 to ~ 1 T) with precise field measurement.

8. **Lock-in Amplifier + Recorder:** Outputs the **first-derivative ESR spectrum** (d(absorption)/dB vs B).

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is hyperfine splitting in ESR? Derive an expression for the number of hyperfine lines produced by N equivalent nuclei of spin I. Illustrate with the example of the methyl radical (•CH₃).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss five important applications of ESR spectroscopy in materials science and chemistry. Explain why ESR gives no signal for diamagnetic materials.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Hyperfine Splitting:** When the unpaired electron interacts with a magnetic nucleus (with nuclear spin $I \neq 0$) in its vicinity, the local magnetic field at the electron is modified. This causes each Zeeman line to split into $(2NI + 1)$ components called hyperfine lines.

**Derivation:**

The hyperfine Hamiltonian is:

$$\hat{H}_{hf} = A \vec{S} \cdot \vec{I}$$

The energy shift due to hyperfine interaction is:

$$\Delta E_{hf} = A m_s M_I$$

where $M_I$ is the total nuclear spin quantum number. For $N$ equivalent nuclei of spin $I$:

- $M_I$ ranges from $-NI$ to $+NI$ in unit steps.
- Number of distinct $M_I$ values = $2NI + 1$.
- Each distinct $M_I$ gives a unique line.

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{Number of hyperfine lines} = 2NI + 1}$$

**Intensity Distribution:** Follows Pascal's triangle (binomial coefficients $\binom{N}{k}$).

**Example — Methyl Radical (•CH₃):**
- 3 equivalent ¹H nuclei: $N = 3$, $I = 1/2$
- Number of lines = $2(3)(1/2) + 1 = 4$
- Intensity ratio = 1 : 3 : 3 : 1 (Pascal's triangle for $N = 3$)
- Each line is separated by ~ 23 Gauss (hyperfine coupling constant $A_H$ for ¹H in •CH₃)

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Five Applications of ESR:**

1. **Free Radical Detection and Quantification:** ESR is the gold standard for detecting and quantifying free radicals in polymers (oxidative degradation), food products (antioxidant assays like DPPH scavenging), and biological systems (oxidative stress in diseases).

2. **Transition Metal Ion Characterization:** Identifies oxidation states, coordination geometry, and ligand field symmetry in catalysts, coordination complexes, and minerals. E.g., distinguishing Cu²⁺ (d⁹) from Cu⁺ (d¹⁰) — only Cu²⁺ is ESR-active.

3. **Defect Analysis in Semiconductors and Solids:** ESR detects paramagnetic defects such as silicon dangling bonds (P_b centers), vacancies in diamond (GR1 center), and color centers in ionic crystals. Critical for quality control of electronic materials.

4. **Spin Labeling in Biology/Pharmacy:** Nitroxide spin labels (e.g., TEMPO) are covalently attached to biomolecules (proteins, membranes). The ESR spectrum reports on local environment — polarity, viscosity, and conformational changes.

5. **Dosimetry and Radiation Damage:** ESR of alanine pellets is used for measuring ionizing radiation dose in medical, food irradiation, and nuclear industries.

**Why No Signal for Diamagnetic Materials:**

Diamagnetic materials have **all electrons paired** (two electrons with opposite spins in the same orbital). For each paired electron, the magnetic moments of the two spins **cancel exactly**, giving a net spin $S = 0$. Since ESR requires a transition between spin states (with $\Delta m_s = \pm 1$), and no such states exist for $S = 0$, **no resonant absorption occurs**. Diamagnetic materials are therefore "ESR-silent."

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of the g-factor:** Always use $g_e \approx 2.0023$ (positive). Students often confuse it with the nuclear g-factor ($g_N \approx 5.585$ for ¹H).
> 2. **Resonance condition units:** Ensure $h\nu$ and $g_e \mu_B B_0$ are in **the same energy units** (Joules). Don't mix CGS and SI — KTU prefers SI.
> 3. **Hyperfine line formula:** Use $2NI + 1$ for *N equivalent nuclei*. For *non-equivalent* nuclei, the count multiplies: $(2n_1 I_1 + 1)(2n_2 I_2 + 1) \cdots$. Students often misapply the simple formula to mixed systems.
> 4. **Selection rule:** $\Delta m_s = \pm 1$ (NOT $\pm 2$). Higher-order forbidden transitions are extremely weak and rarely relevant.
> 5. **X-band vs Q-band:** Remember that as frequency increases (Q-band ~ 35 GHz), the resonance field $B_0$ also increases proportionally — a common board-exam trap.
> 6. **Drawing the block diagram:** Always include the **circulator** and **field modulation** — these are signature ESR components that distinguish ESR from NMR or UV-Vis spectrometers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ ESR = absorption of **microwaves** by **unpaired electrons** in a magnetic field.
- ✅ Resonance condition: $h\nu = g_e \mu_B B_0$ — this is the single most important equation.
- ✅ Operating band: **X-band (9.5 GHz, ~ 0.34 T)** is standard; Q-band (35 GHz), W-band (95 GHz) also used.
- ✅ Free electron g-factor: $g_e = 2.0023$ — deviations identify the chemical environment.
- ✅ Selection rule: $\Delta m_s = \pm 1$ (magnetic dipole transition).
- ✅ Boltzmann population difference at 300 K, X-band ≈ **0.08%** → ESR is intrinsically low sensitivity.
- ✅ Hyperfine splitting formula: $2NI + 1$ lines; intensities follow Pascal's triangle.
- ✅ Number of lines reveals number and identity of nearby magnetic nuclei — key for structure determination.
- ✅ Three relaxation parameters: $T_1$ (spin-lattice), $T_2$ (spin-spin), $T_2^*$ (effective, including inhomogeneity).
- ✅ Saturation: high microwave power → population equalization → signal vanishes.
- ✅ Diamagnetic materials (all electrons paired) → **NO ESR signal** — they are "silent."
- ✅ Paramagnetic materials (one or more unpaired electrons) → ESR signal present.
- ✅ Spin-lattice relaxation: energy → lattice vibrations (phonons).
- ✅ Spin-spin relaxation: energy redistribution among neighboring spins.
- ✅ Instrumentation essentials: Klystron/Gunn source → Attenuator → Circulator → Resonant Cavity → Detector → Lock-in Amplifier.
- ✅ Resonant cavity has Q-factor ~ 10,000 — amplifies weak signal dramatically.
- ✅ Field modulation (100 kHz) + lock-in detection = ~ 1000× SNR improvement.
- ✅ Output is **first-derivative** of absorption (because of lock-in detection at modulation frequency).
- ✅ Applications: free radical detection, transition metal analysis, defect studies, spin labeling, dosimetry.
- ✅ Common free radicals studied: DPPH, TEMPO, •CH₃, •OH, nitroxides, radical anions/cations of organic molecules.
- ✅ ESR detects **only unpaired electrons** — it is a selective, qualitative + quantitative technique for paramagnetic species.
- ✅ Difference from NMR: NMR probes nuclear spins (~ MHz-GHz); ESR probes electron spins (~ GHz).
- ✅ Difference from UV-Vis: UV-Vis probes electronic transitions in **all** molecules; ESR probes only paramagnetic ones.
- ✅ Use $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ J/T for KTU numerical problems (SI units).
- ✅ Remember: $\gamma_e = g_e \mu_B / \hbar = 1.760859 \times 10^{11}$ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ — useful in Larmor frequency derivations.
- ✅ ESR spectra are usually plotted as **first derivatives** vs $B_0$ (in Gauss), not as absorption vs $B_0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Characterization Techniques: UV-VIS Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optical Characterization Techniques: UV-VIS Spectroscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **UV-Vis Spectroscopy (Ultraviolet-Visible Spectroscopy)** is an absorption-based optical characterization technique that measures the attenuation of electromagnetic radiation in the **UV region (10–400 nm)** and the **visible region (400–750 nm)** as it passes through a sample, providing qualitative identification of chromophoric groups and quantitative determination of analyte concentration via the **Beer-Lambert Law**.

The technique is grounded in the interaction of photons with valence-shell electrons of molecules, ions, or complexes, inducing **electronic transitions** between quantized energy levels. Because the energy of absorbed photons is uniquely characteristic of the electronic structure, the resulting spectrum acts as a molecular "fingerprint" within the UV-Vis window.

### 1.2 Electromagnetic Spectrum Positioning

The UV-Vis range lies between the higher-energy vacuum-UV and the lower-energy near-infrared (NIR) region. KTU examiners frequently test this positioning.

| Region | Wavelength Range (nm) | Photon Energy Range (eV) |
|---|---|---|
| Vacuum UV | 10 – 200 | 6.2 – 124 |
| Near UV (UV-C, UV-B) | 200 – 300 | 4.1 – 6.2 |
| Quartz UV (UV-A) | 300 – 400 | 3.1 – 4.1 |
| **Visible** | **400 – 750** | **1.65 – 3.1** |
| Near IR | 750 – 2500 | 0.5 – 1.65 |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stained-Glass Window"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a stained-glass window. White light (containing all visible wavelengths) strikes the glass from one side, and only specific colors pass through while others are absorbed by the metal-oxide pigments embedded in the glass. UV-Vis spectroscopy is the precise scientific version of this — the sample behaves like the glass, the spectrophotometer is the light source, and the detector counts *which* wavelengths passed and *how many* were absorbed.

A red solution, for instance, transmits red wavelengths (~620–750 nm) and absorbs its complementary color (green, ~500–560 nm). The spectrophotometer mathematically converts this selective absorption into an absorption spectrum.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Planck's Constant (h)** = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Speed of Light in vacuum (c)** = **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ)** = **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- **Molar Absorptivity (ε)** is conventionally reported in **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**
- **Wavenumber (ṽ)** = 1/λ, in **cm⁻¹**
- **Absorbance (A)** is **dimensionless** (a logarithmic ratio)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Always quote wavelength (λ) in nanometres (nm) and use **molar absorptivity (ε)** — not the path-length-dependent absorbance — when comparing chromophore strengths across molecules. The product **ε × λₘₐₓ** is sometimes used as a normalized "oscillator strength" parameter in advanced KTU numerical questions.

### 1.5 Types of Electronic Transitions

Electronic transitions in organic and inorganic chromophores follow the ordering (energy-wise):

$$\sigma \to \sigma^* > n \to \sigma^* \approx \pi \to \pi^* < n \to \pi^*$$

The asterisk (\*) denotes an anti-bonding molecular orbital. π→π\* and n→π\* transitions dominate the readily accessible UV-Vis region (200–700 nm) and are the principal transitions examined in **PEPET412** problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" Behind the Measurement

The spectrophotometer passes a **monochromatic** beam of intensity **I₀** through a sample of path length **ℓ** (typically 1 cm in standard quartz cuvettes). The transmitted intensity **I** is recorded at each wavelength, and the ratio **I/I₀** is converted to **Transmittance (T)**, then to **Absorbance (A)** via logarithm.

**Step-by-step logic chain:**

1. The polychromatic source emits broad-spectrum radiation (e.g., a deuterium lamp for UV, tungsten-halogen for visible).
2. The **monochromator** (prism or diffraction grating) isolates a single wavelength.
3. The beam is split — part goes through the **sample cell**, the other through the **reference cell** (containing pure solvent).
4. Two photodetectors measure I (sample) and I₀ (reference) **simultaneously**, canceling out solvent absorption and source-fluctuation noise (this is the **double-beam** design).
5. The electronics compute A = log(I₀/I) and plot it against wavelength.

### 2.2 Beer-Lambert Law — The Mathematical Heart of UV-Vis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beer-Lambert Law (KTU High-Yield Core Equation):**
> A = ε · c · ℓ
> where **A = absorbance (dimensionless)**, **ε = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)**, **c = molar concentration (mol·L⁻¹)**, **ℓ = optical path length (cm)**.

The Beer-Lambert law is a **consequence of two additive sub-laws**:
- **Beer's Law** (1825): A ∝ c (concentration)
- **Lambert's Law** (1760): A ∝ ℓ (path length)

**Validity Limits** (frequently tested as 3-mark conceptual questions):

| Limitation | Reason |
|---|---|
| c ≤ 0.01 M | Above this, solute–solute interactions and refractive-index changes cause deviation |
| Monochromatic radiation | Real instruments have finite bandwidth Δλ; large Δλ causes "polychromatic deviation" |
| No scattering/turbidity | Particles deflect light, falsely elevating absorbance |
| No fluorescence/photochemistry | Emitted light or sample degradation breaks the steady-state assumption |
| Homogeneous solution | Precipitates or phase boundaries invalidate the linear model |

### 2.3 Energy of the Absorbed Photon

The energy gap between the ground state and the excited electronic state is related to absorption wavelength by:

$$E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

For a wavelength of 500 nm (green light):

$$E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})}{500 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.97 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \approx 2.48 \text{ eV}$$

In spectroscopy, energy is often expressed per mole of photons (**Einstein's relation**):

$$E = \frac{N_A \cdot h \cdot c}{\lambda} = \frac{0.1196}{\lambda \text{ (in m)}} \text{ J/mol}$$

### 2.4 Wavenumber Conversion

$$\tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{\nu}{c}$$

A wavelength of 500 nm corresponds to **ṽ = 20,000 cm⁻¹**, a unit commonly seen in IR/VIS crossover problems.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmittance | T = I / I₀ | dimensionless (or %) | Direct measurement |
| Absorbance | A = −log₁₀ T = log₁₀(I₀/I) | dimensionless | Linear quantitation |
| Beer-Lambert Law | A = ε c ℓ | — | Quantitative analysis |
| Photon Energy | E = hc/λ | J or eV | Transition assignment |
| Per-mole Energy | E = N_A hc/λ | J·mol⁻¹ | Stoichiometric calcs |
| Wavenumber | ṽ = 1/λ | cm⁻¹ | Spectral axis conversion |
| Limit of Detection | LOD = 3σ / εℓ | mol·L⁻¹ | Method validation |
| Limit of Quantitation | LOQ = 10σ / εℓ | mol·L⁻¹ | Method validation |
| Specific Absorptivity | a = ε / M | L·g⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | Comparing across compounds |
| Isobestic Point | A₁(λ*) = A₂(λ*) | nm | Kinetic/equilibrium studies |
| Resolution | R = λ/Δλ | dimensionless | Instrument specification |
| Absorbance Sum (mixture) | A_total(λ) = Σ εᵢ cᵢ ℓ | — | Multi-component analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** UV-Vis is the workhorse in pharmaceutical quality control (paracetamol assays), water-quality testing (nitrate/nitrite at 220/275 nm), semiconductor band-gap determination via Tauc plots, dye concentration in textile effluent, and food-colorant certification. KTU examiners link these applications to the "engineer-society-economy" outcomes in the 2024 scheme.

### 2.6 Instrument Components — Block-Level Architecture

A modern UV-Vis spectrophotometer consists of five functional blocks:

1. **Radiation Source** — Deuterium (D₂) lamp for 190–400 nm; Tungsten-Halogen lamp for 320–1100 nm. The lamp-housing is a sealed, thermally stabilized compartment.
2. **Monochromator** — Comprises an entrance slit, a dispersing element (grating or prism), and an exit slit. Wavelength is scanned by rotating the grating.
3. **Sample Compartment** — Holds matched quartz (not glass — glass absorbs UV below 350 nm) cuvettes of standard 1 cm path length.
4. **Detector** — Photomultiplier tube (PMT) for high-sensitivity work; photodiode-array (PDA) for simultaneous multi-wavelength capture in modern instruments.
5. **Signal Processor & Display** — Logarithmic amplifier converts I to A; microcontroller drives the scan and displays the spectrum on a PC or built-in screen.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Beer's + Lambert's Law to A = ε c ℓ

We will derive the unified Beer-Lambert expression from first principles, with every algebraic transition written out in full.

**Step 1 — Define an infinitesimal absorption event**

Consider a thin slice of the absorbing medium of thickness **dℓ**, oriented perpendicular to a monochromatic beam of intensity **I**. The probability of a photon being absorbed in this slice is proportional to the number of absorbing molecules encountered, i.e., to the concentration **c** and the slice thickness **dℓ**.

**Step 2 — Write the differential equation**

The fractional decrease in intensity dI/I is proportional to c·dℓ:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -k \cdot c \cdot d\ell$$

**Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that intensity *decreases* as the beam travels further. The proportionality constant k depends on the wavelength, the absorbing species, and the solvent.

**Step 3 — Integrate across the full cell path**

Integrate from the entry face (ℓ = 0, intensity I₀) to the exit face (ℓ = ℓ, intensity I):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -k \cdot c \cdot \int_{0}^{\ell} d\ell$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals**

The left side yields the natural logarithm of the ratio; the right side yields the product k·c·ℓ:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -k \cdot c \cdot \ell$$

**Step 5 — Convert from natural log to base-10 logarithm**

Using ln(x) = 2.303 · log₁₀(x):

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot c \cdot \ell}{2.303}$$

**Step 6 — Define absorbance**

By definition, **A = log₁₀(I₀/I) = −log₁₀(I/I₀)**. The negative sign in Step 5 cancels this definition:

$$A = \frac{k}{2.303} \cdot c \cdot \ell$$

**Step 7 — Identify the constant ε**

The grouped constant k/2.303 is named the **molar absorptivity (ε)**, a wavelength-dependent property intrinsic to the chromophore. It is the absorbance of a 1 mol·L⁻¹ solution in a 1 cm cell:

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight for KTU Board:** This derivation reveals that absorbance is *additive* and *independent* of source intensity — doubling the lamp brightness leaves A unchanged. This is why UV-Vis is a *ratio* technique, robust against source drift.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Multi-Component Mixture (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A solution contains two absorbing species, X and Y, in a 1 cm cell. At 280 nm: ε_X = 1500 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, ε_Y = 800 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, and A₂₈₀ = 0.620. At 320 nm: ε_X = 300 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, ε_Y = 2000 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, and A₃₂₀ = 0.940. Calculate [X] and [Y].

**Solution (every step shown):**

**Step 1 — Set up the two simultaneous equations from Beer-Lambert Law**

At 280 nm:

$$A_{280} = \varepsilon_X(280) \cdot c_X \cdot \ell + \varepsilon_Y(280) \cdot c_Y \cdot \ell$$

$$0.620 = 1500 \cdot c_X \cdot 1 + 800 \cdot c_Y \cdot 1$$

At 320 nm:

$$A_{320} = \varepsilon_X(320) \cdot c_X \cdot \ell + \varepsilon_Y(320) \cdot c_Y \cdot \ell$$

$$0.940 = 300 \cdot c_X \cdot 1 + 2000 \cdot c_Y \cdot 1$$

**Step 2 — Solve by Cramer's rule**

Determinant of the coefficient matrix:

$$D = (1500)(2000) - (800)(300) = 3{,}000{,}000 - 240{,}000 = 2{,}760{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Solve for c_X**

Replace the first column with the absorbance column:

$$D_X = (0.620)(2000) - (800)(0.940) = 1240 - 752 = 488$$

$$c_X = \frac{D_X}{D} = \frac{488}{2{,}760{,}000} = 1.768 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for c_Y**

Replace the second column with the absorbance column:

$$D_Y = (1500)(0.940) - (0.620)(300) = 1410 - 186 = 1224$$

$$c_Y = \frac{D_Y}{D} = \frac{1224}{2{,}760{,}000} = 4.435 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$c_X = 1.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L} \quad ; \quad c_Y = 4.43 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Photon Energy at λₘₐₓ

**Problem:** A chromophore absorbs maximally at 254 nm. Calculate the energy of the absorbed photon in (a) joules, (b) electron-volts, and (c) kJ·mol⁻¹.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Energy in joules**

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s})(2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s})}{254 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

$$E = 7.82 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J per photon}$$

**Part (b) — Energy in electron-volts**

Using 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J:

$$E = \frac{7.82 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 4.88 \text{ eV}$$

**Part (c) — Energy per mole of photons**

$$E_{mol} = N_A \cdot h \cdot \frac{c}{\lambda} = (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \times 7.82 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/mol}$$

$$E_{mol} = 4.71 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/mol} = 471 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Key Equations | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Find concentration from A | c = A / (εℓ) | Mixing up ε values at different λ |
| Find ε from A and c | ε = A / (cℓ) | Wrong path length unit (use cm, not mm) |
| Mixture quantitation | 2×2 linear system | Swapping matrix rows in Cramer's rule |
| Energy of transition | E = hc/λ | λ must be in metres for SI J |
| % Transmittance ↔ A | A = −log₁₀(T); T = 10^(−A) | T given in %, must divide by 100 first |
| Isobestic analysis | A_total(λ*) = const | Mis-identifying λ* from the spectrum |
| Limit of Detection | LOD = 3σ/S | σ is the standard deviation of blank |
| Tauc plot (band gap) | (αhν)² vs hν | Wrong exponent (2 for direct, ½ for indirect) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Double-Beam UV-Vis Spectrophotometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Source D2 Lamp UV] --> S1[Beam Splitter]
    A2[Source W Lamp Visible] --> S1
    S1 --> S2[Monochromator Grating and Slits]
    S2 --> S3[Chopper 50 Hz]
    S3 --> S4[Sample Cell 1 cm Quartz]
    S3 --> S5[Reference Cell Solvent]
    S4 --> S6[Detector PMT]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Log Amplifier]
    S7 --> S8[Microcontroller and ADC]
    S8 --> S9[Spectrum Plot A versus lambda]
    S8 --> S10[Concentration c from Beer Lambert]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Information-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Photons from Source] --> stepB[Monochromation Wavelength Selection]
    stepB --> stepC[Reference vs Sample Channel]
    stepC --> stepD[Intensity Detection I and I0]
    stepD --> stepE[Logarithmic Conversion A equals log I0 over I]
    stepE --> stepF[Data Buffer and Baseline Correction]
    stepF --> stepG[Spectral Output Absorbance vs Wavelength]
    stepF --> stepH[Quantitative Output Concentration from Beer Lambert]
    stepG --> stepI[Qualitative Analysis Chromophore Identification]
    stepH --> stepJ[Quantitative Analysis Analyte Concentration]
```

### 4.3 Electronic Transition Energy-Level Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Sigma Bonding MO] -->|sigma to sigma star| L4[Sigma Antibonding MO high energy]
    L2[Pi Bonding MO] -->|pi to pi star| L5[Pi Antibonding MO]
    L3[Non Bonding n] -->|n to pi star| L5
    L3 -->|n to sigma star| L4
    L2 -->|pi to pi star| L4
    style L1 fill:#dbeafe
    style L2 fill:#fef3c7
    style L3 fill:#fee2e2
    style L4 fill:#dcfce7
    style L5 fill:#ede9fe
```

### 4.4 Typical UV-Vis Spectrum — Annotated Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[X axis Wavelength nm 200 to 800] --> P2[Region 1 UV C 200 to 280 nm]
    P2 --> P3[Region 2 UV B 280 to 320 nm]
    P3 --> P4[Region 3 UV A 320 to 400 nm]
    P4 --> P5[Region 4 Visible 400 to 750 nm]
    P5 --> P6[Lambda max Position of Peak Absorbance]
    P6 --> P7[A max Peak Absorbance Value]
    P7 --> P8[Shoulder Sub peak]
    P8 --> P9[Baseline Corrected A equals 0 at Non Absorbing Regions]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Schematics?** Per the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10** protocol, complex physical drawings (e.g., a labelled cut-away of a grating monochromator) are rendered as **block-level functional architecture flows** to maximize clarity in text-based delivery. The above four diagrams comprehensively map the (a) hardware, (b) data flow, (c) quantum-mechanical origins, and (d) spectral features of UV-Vis spectroscopy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define molar absorptivity. How does it differ from specific absorbance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full marks mapping):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Molar absorptivity (ε)** is the absorbance of a solution of unit molar concentration (1 mol·L⁻¹) measured in a cell of unit path length (1 cm). It is a constant characteristic of the chromophore at a given wavelength and is expressed in **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**.

> **Specific absorbance (a)** is the absorbance of a solution of unit concentration expressed in **grams per litre** (g·L⁻¹) in a 1 cm cell. It is related to ε by the molecular mass M:
> $$\varepsilon = a \times M$$

> **Key distinction:** ε is normalized per mole, while a is normalized per gram. Therefore, ε depends on the molecular weight of the absorbing species, but a does not. For polymer chemists, a is preferred; for small-molecule pharmacology, ε is standard. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the Beer-Lambert law. Mention any two limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Beer-Lambert Law:** The absorbance of a homogeneous solution is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the optical path length:
> $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell$$

> **Limitations (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
> 1. **High concentration limit:** Above ~0.01 M, intermolecular interactions and refractive index changes cause negative deviation from linearity.
> 2. **Polychromatic radiation:** Real instruments have finite spectral bandwidth; if Δλ is comparable to the absorption peak width, the law fails (the A–c plot curves downward).
> 3. **Scattering and turbidity** invalidate the assumption that attenuation is purely absorptive.
> 4. **Fluorescence or photochemical decomposition** of the sample during measurement breaks the steady-state assumption.
> 5. **Chemical equilibria** (pH-dependent speciation) can shift the chromophore identity, producing curved A–c plots. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **double-beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer**. List the essential components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Beer-Lambert law from first principles. A 5 × 10⁻⁴ M solution shows an absorbance of 0.85 in a 1 cm cell at 280 nm. Calculate the molar absorptivity. If the same solution is measured in a 2 cm cell, what will be the new absorbance? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

> **1. Source (1 mark):** A **deuterium (D₂) lamp** emits continuous radiation from 190–400 nm (UV region), and a **tungsten-halogen lamp** covers 320–1100 nm (visible/NIR). The lamps are housed in a temperature-stabilized compartment.

> **2. Monochromator (1.5 marks):** A combination of entrance slit, **diffraction grating** (or prism), and exit slit. Rotation of the grating by a stepper motor scans the wavelength. Stray-light filters suppress higher-order diffraction.

> **3. Beam splitter & chopper (1.5 marks):** A **half-silvered mirror** divides the monochromatic beam into two equal halves — one through the sample cell, one through the reference cell (containing pure solvent). A rotating **chopper** (typically 25–50 Hz) alternately directs light through the two paths so that the detector receives sample and reference signals in rapid alternation.

> **4. Sample & reference compartments (1 mark):** Matched **quartz cuvettes** of 1 cm path length. Quartz is used because ordinary glass absorbs strongly below 350 nm.

> **5. Detector (1 mark):** A **photomultiplier tube (PMT)** converts the transmitted photons into a proportional current, with internal amplification of 10⁶–10⁷. Modern instruments also use **photodiode arrays (PDA)** for simultaneous full-spectrum acquisition.

> **6. Signal processor & display (1 mark):** A **logarithmic amplifier** computes A = log(I₀/I) from the alternating detector signal. The result is plotted as **A vs λ** on a PC screen or sent to a printer.

> **Advantage of double-beam design:** Real-time cancellation of source-intensity drift, solvent absorption, and electronic noise — making it the industry standard for high-precision quantitative work. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

> **Derivation (4 marks) — see SECTION 3.1 of these notes for the full 7-step derivation.** Briefly recap:
> - Differential form: dI/I = −k·c·dℓ
> - Integrate from I₀ to I: ln(I/I₀) = −k·c·ℓ
> - Convert to base-10 log and identify ε: A = ε·c·ℓ

> **Numerical part (3 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Find ε:**
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{A}{c \cdot \ell} = \frac{0.85}{(5 \times 10^{-4})(1)} = 1700 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$$

> **Step 2 — Find A in 2 cm cell:**
> $$A_{new} = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell_{new} = 1700 \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 2 = 1.70$$

> **Answer:** ε = 1.7 × 10³ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹; A(new) = 1.70. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Discuss the different types of **electronic transitions** observed in UV-Vis spectroscopy, with a suitable energy-level diagram. Give one example chromophore for each transition type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solution of a drug in a 1 cm cell has an absorbance of 0.420 at 240 nm. The same solution, when diluted 1:5 with solvent, gives an absorbance of 0.094 at the same wavelength. Verify Beer-Lambert law compliance and calculate the molar absorptivity of the drug at 240 nm, given that the original concentration is 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ M. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

> **1. σ → σ\* transition (1 mark):** Excitation of an electron from a sigma-bonding MO to its anti-bonding counterpart. **High energy** — typically observed at λ < 200 nm (deep UV, vacuum UV region). **Example:** alkanes like n-hexane, which absorb around 150 nm.

> **2. n → σ\* transition (1 mark):** Excitation of a non-bonding (lone pair) electron to a sigma anti-bonding MO. **Moderate energy**, λ ≈ 150–250 nm. **Example:** saturated amines, alcohols, alkyl halides (e.g., trimethylamine λₘₐₓ ≈ 227 nm).

> **3. π → π\* transition (1.5 marks):** Excitation of a pi-bonding electron to a pi anti-bonding MO. **Strong intensity** (ε ≈ 10,000 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), λ ≈ 200–400 nm. **Example:** ethylene (λₘₐₓ ≈ 171 nm), benzene (λₘₐₓ ≈ 254 nm with characteristic vibronic fine structure).

> **4. n → π\* transition (1.5 marks):** Excitation of a non-bonding electron on a heteroatom (O, N, S) to a pi anti-bonding MO. **Weak intensity** (ε ≈ 10–100 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), λ ≈ 270–350 nm, often the most diagnostic transition for carbonyls. **Example:** acetone (λₘₐₓ ≈ 279 nm), benzaldehyde (~280 nm and 320 nm).

> **5. d–d and charge-transfer transitions (1 mark):** Important in **inorganic** and **coordination chemistry**; d–d transitions are Laporte-forbidden (ε ≈ 1–1000) but cause the visible color of many transition-metal complexes (e.g., [Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺ blue, λₘₐₓ ≈ 810 nm). Charge-transfer transitions are very intense (ε ≈ 10,000–50,000), e.g., in KMnO₄.

> **6. Energy-level diagram (1 mark):** Show MO ladder: σ < π < n < π\* < σ\*, with arrows from occupied to vacant levels. The relative energies should match the ordering σ→σ\* > n→σ\* ≈ π→π\* > n→π\*. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

> **Step 1 — Apply Beer-Lambert law to original solution (1.5 marks):**
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{A_1}{c_1 \cdot \ell} = \frac{0.420}{(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(1)} = 1680 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$$

> **Step 2 — Concentration of the diluted solution (1 mark):**
> The 1:5 dilution means c₂ = c₁/5 = (2.5 × 10⁻⁴)/5 = 5.0 × 10⁻⁵ M.

> **Step 3 — Predicted absorbance for diluted solution (1.5 marks):**
> $$A_{2,\text{predicted}} = \varepsilon \cdot c_2 \cdot \ell = 1680 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 1 = 0.0840$$

> **Step 4 — Compare with measured value (1 mark):**
> Predicted = 0.0840; Measured = 0.0940. The values differ by ~12%, **which is beyond typical experimental error** (±1–2%). The deviation suggests either polychromatic-radiation effects, instrument stray light, or chemical interaction (e.g., aggregation) at the original concentration.

> **Step 5 — Conclusion (2 marks):** Strictly speaking, Beer-Lambert law is **not** perfectly obeyed for this system. However, if the deviation is attributed to experimental noise, the average ε ≈ 1700 ± 100 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ is a useful working value. For rigorous quantitative work, a calibration curve should be constructed at multiple concentrations spanning the working range.

> **Answer:** ε ≈ 1.68 × 10³ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹; minor deviation observed; calibration-curve verification recommended. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in UV-Vis Problems (PEPET412 — Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in path length:** Cuvettes are *standardized* at 1 cm, but a few problems specify 0.5 cm or 5 cm. If you write ℓ = 1 always, you lose **1–2 marks instantly**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ conversion:** Energy in eV must be divided by the eV–J conversion factor, not multiplied. A wrong direction costs **full marks on sub-part (b)**.
>
> 3. **% Transmittance vs fractional T:** If the data is "T = 25%", the value to plug into A = −log T is **0.25, not 25**. This single decimal-point error is the most frequent cause of "nearly-right" answers.
>
> 4. **Cramer's rule row/column swap:** In multi-component analysis, swapping rows and columns in the determinant gives the wrong concentrations. Always write the **wavelengths in rows** and **components in columns** consistently.
>
> 5. **Confusing absorbance and absorptivity:** A is dimensionless, ε has units. Examiners deduct **0.5 mark** if units are missing or wrong in the final answer.
>
> 6. **Drawing the monochromator without a grating:** A "monochromator" block with no mention of the dispersing element or slits is incomplete — deduct **0.5–1 mark** in instrument-diagram questions.
>
> 7. **Mixing up transitions:** Saying "π→π\* is the lowest energy transition" is **wrong** — n→π\* is lower in energy for carbonyls. Examiners test this distinction specifically.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for UV-Vis Spectroscopy (KTU 2024 PEPET412 Module 4):**

- **Spectral region:** UV = 100–400 nm; Visible = 400–750 nm; total UV-Vis = 200–800 nm (practical).
- **Beer-Lambert Law:** **A = ε c ℓ** — the central equation. Memorize both the form and the units.
- **A is dimensionless**; ε in L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹; c in mol·L⁻¹; ℓ in cm.
- **A = log(I₀/I) = −log T** — the bridge between the measured quantity (T or I/I₀) and the analytical quantity (A).
- **Photon energy:** **E = hc/λ**; in eV, divide by 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹; per mole, multiply by N_A.
- **Transition ordering (energy, high → low):** σ→σ\* > n→σ\* ≈ π→π\* > n→π\*.
- **π→π\* is intense** (ε ~ 10⁴); **n→π\* is weak** (ε ~ 10–100); useful diagnostic for carbonyls.
- **Double-beam design** cancels source drift and solvent absorption; standard for quantitative work.
- **Key components:** D₂ + W lamps → Monochromator (grating) → Beam splitter → Sample/Reference cells (quartz, 1 cm) → PMT/PDA detector → Log amplifier → Spectrum.
- **Quartz cuvettes** are mandatory below 350 nm; glass absorbs UV.
- **Validity range of Beer-Lambert:** c ≤ 0.01 M; monochromatic light; no scattering; no fluorescence; homogeneous solution.
- **Mixture analysis** uses 2×2 linear systems at two λ values (Cramer's rule).
- **Limit of Detection:** **LOD = 3σ/εℓ**; **LOQ = 10σ/εℓ** — standard figures of merit in method validation.
- **Tauc plot:** (αhν)ⁿ vs hν gives the **optical band gap** of semiconductors; n = 2 for direct, ½ for indirect transitions.
- **Isobestic point:** A wavelength where two species in equilibrium have the same ε — useful for kinetic studies and as an internal check on purity.
- **Woodward-Fieser rules** predict λₘₐₓ for conjugated dienes/enones — not derivable by formula, but used qualitatively in structural assignments.
- **Engineering applications:** pharmaceutical assays, water-quality testing, semiconductor band-gap, dye concentration, food colorants, forensic toxicology.
- **Instrument abbreviations to memorize:** D₂ (deuterium), W (tungsten), PMT (photomultiplier tube), PDA (photodiode array), ADC (analog-to-digital converter).
- **NEP 2020 outcome link:** UV-Vis is the workhorse "Optical Method" tied to **CO3** of PEPET412 (analyze optical absorption spectra for material identification and quantification).
- **Examiner hot-spots:** (i) derivation of Beer-Lambert; (ii) two-component mixture numerical; (iii) instrument block diagram with component functions; (iv) energy calculation in eV and kJ/mol; (v) transition-type identification from sample name.

> **Final Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, **always** label units, **always** show the substitution step before computing the numerical answer, and **always** mention at least one assumption (e.g., "assuming Beer-Lambert law is obeyed"). These three habits alone will secure an additional **2–3 marks** beyond raw correctness in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy** is a non-destructive, vibrational spectroscopic technique used to identify chemical bonds, functional groups, and molecular structures of materials by measuring the absorption of infrared radiation as a function of wavenumber. Unlike dispersive IR spectrometers, FTIR employs a **Michelson interferometer** to modulate the broadband IR source, and the resulting **interferogram** is mathematically converted into a conventional IR spectrum using the **Fourier Transform (FT)** algorithm.

The fundamental physical principle rests on the interaction of infrared photons (with energies matching molecular vibrational transitions, typically **4000 cm⁻¹ to 400 cm⁻¹**) with the electric dipole moment of covalent bonds. When the frequency of incident IR radiation equals the natural vibrational frequency of a bond, selective absorption occurs, producing a characteristic absorption band in the spectrum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FTIR is classified under **Module 4 — Spectroscopic Techniques for Material Characterization**. The emphasis is on the working principle, instrumentation, and the role of the Michelson interferometer in generating the interferogram.

### 1.2 Mathematical Working Range

| Region | Wavenumber Range | Information Obtained |
|---|---|---|
| Near-IR | 12500 – 4000 cm⁻¹ | Overtones & combination bands |
| Mid-IR (FTIR Core) | 4000 – 400 cm⁻¹ | Fundamental vibrations — **most diagnostic** |
| Far-IR | 400 – 10 cm⁻¹ | Skeletal vibrations, lattice modes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fingerprint of Molecules"

Imagine a tuning fork that hums at a unique pitch. If you walk into a room full of tuning forks (each representing a different chemical bond like C=O, O-H, N-H, C-H) and sing a chord of all possible notes simultaneously, **only those forks whose natural pitch matches a sung note will vibrate and absorb that energy**. By listening to *which notes were absorbed*, you can deduce exactly which tuning forks are present.

> This is precisely what FTIR does to a molecule:
> - The "chord of all notes" → **broadband polychromatic IR beam**.
> - The "tuning forks" → **chemical bonds with specific vibrational frequencies**.
> - The "missing notes in the chord" → **absorption peaks in the FTIR spectrum**.
> - The "fingerprint" of every molecule is unique because no two molecules (except enantiomers) have identical sets of vibrating bonds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Fourier"?** The detector does **not** directly measure intensity vs. wavenumber. Instead, it records an **interferogram** (intensity vs. optical path difference — a function of *time/position*). To extract the spectrum (intensity vs. *frequency*), we must apply the **Fourier Transform**, a mathematical operation that decomposes any time-domain signal into its constituent frequencies.

## 1.4 Types of Molecular Vibrations — The Origin of IR Absorption

Every covalent bond behaves as a spring connecting two masses (Hooke's Law). The energy of vibrational motion is **quantized**:

$$E_{vib} = \left(v + \tfrac{1}{2}\right) h\nu$$

where $v$ = vibrational quantum number (0, 1, 2, …), $h$ = Planck's constant, and $\nu$ = vibrational frequency. The selection rule requires $\Delta v = \pm 1$, meaning transitions occur between adjacent vibrational levels.

The two broad classes of vibrations are:

- **Stretching vibrations** — change in bond length (symmetric & asymmetric).
- **Bending vibrations** — change in bond angle (scissoring, rocking, wagging, twisting).

A vibration is **IR-active only if it produces a change in the dipole moment** of the molecule ($\partial \mu / \partial q \neq 0$). Symmetric, non-polar bonds (e.g., N₂, O₂, homonuclear diatomics) are **IR-inactive**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physical Basis of FTIR — Why Use Fourier Transform?

A **dispersive (grating) IR spectrometer** scans one wavelength at a time using a monochromator. This is slow (takes ~15 minutes for a full scan), has poor signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and cannot use narrow slits without losing throughput (the **Jacquinot advantage** of FTIR exploits this).

FTIR overcomes these limitations by the **Fellgett (multiplex) advantage**:

- All wavenumbers are measured **simultaneously** (multiplexing).
- This gives an SNR improvement of $\sqrt{N}$ over dispersive instruments, where $N$ is the number of resolution elements.
- Rapid scanning allows signal averaging — typically 32 to 1024 co-added scans.

## 2.2 Instrumentation — Building Blocks of an FTIR Spectrometer

The FTIR spectrometer consists of five essential subsystems, each performing a critical function:

| Subsystem | Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **IR Source** | Emit broadband polychromatic IR radiation | Globar (SiC), Nernst glower, Tungsten-halogen (NIR) |
| **Interferometer** | Modulate IR beam into an interferogram | Michelson interferometer (beam splitter, fixed mirror, moving mirror) |
| **Sample Compartment** | Hold sample in IR beam path | KBr pellet holder, ATR crystal, gas cell |
| **Detector** | Convert IR signal to electrical signal | DTGS (room temp.), MCT (liquid N₂ cooled) |
| **Computer** | Perform Fourier transform & display spectrum | FT algorithm, libraries (e.g., KnowItAll, HR Spectra) |

### 2.2.1 The Michelson Interferometer — Heart of the FTIR

The Michelson interferometer is the core of every FTIR. It consists of:

1. **Beam splitter** — a thin film (typically germanium-coated KBr or CaF₂) that transmits 50% and reflects 50% of the incoming IR beam.
2. **Fixed mirror** — stationary, at distance $L_1$ from beam splitter.
3. **Moving mirror** — translates along the optical axis at distance $L_2(t)$ from beam splitter.
4. **Compensating plate** — equalizes optical path in the transmitted arm.

When the moving mirror travels a distance $x$, the **optical path difference (OPD or retardation, $\delta$)** is:

$$\delta = 2(L_2 - L_1) = 2x$$

The two reflected beams recombine at the beam splitter and interfere. For a **monochromatic source** of wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$, the detected intensity at the output is:

$$I(\delta) = I_0 \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta)$$

For a **polychromatic source** with spectral intensity distribution $B(\tilde{\nu})$, the total detected intensity (the **interferogram**) is:

$$I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\tilde{\nu}$$

This equation is the cornerstone of FTIR theory.

### 2.2.2 From Interferogram to Spectrum — The Inverse Operation

Recovering $B(\tilde{\nu})$ from the measured $I(\delta)$ requires the **inverse Fourier cosine transform**:

$$B(\tilde{\nu}) = \int_{0}^{\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$

This is performed numerically by the computer using the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm developed by Cooley & Tukey (1965).

## 2.3 Sample Preparation Techniques

| Technique | Application | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **KBr Pellet Method** | Solid powders | Sample mixed with KBr, pressed at 8–10 ton |
| **Cast Film** | Polymers, thin films | Solvent-cast onto NaCl/KBr window |
| **ATR (Attenuated Total Reflectance)** | Solids, liquids, gels, surfaces | No preparation; uses diamond/ZnSe crystal |
| **Gas Cell** | Gases, vapors | Long path length (10 cm to 10 m) |
| **Diffuse Reflectance (DRIFT)** | Powders, rough surfaces | Mix with KBr, no pellet needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Question:** *"Compare dispersive IR and FTIR."* — Always mention the **Fellgett (multiplex) advantage**, **Jacquinot (throughput) advantage**, and **Connes advantage** (laser-referenced wavelength calibration giving high wavenumber accuracy).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\tilde{\nu}$ = wavenumber (cm⁻¹), $\lambda$ = wavelength (cm) | Unit conversion |
| 2 | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$ | $k$ = force constant, $\mu$ = reduced mass, $c$ = speed of light | Calculate bond vibrational frequency |
| 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Reduced mass of diatomic molecule | Hooke's law for vibrations |
| 4 | $I(\delta) = \int B(\tilde{\nu})\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\,d\tilde{\nu}$ | Interferogram equation | Forward Fourier transform |
| 5 | $B(\tilde{\nu}) = \int I(\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\,d\delta$ | Spectrum recovery | Inverse Fourier transform |
| 6 | $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\delta_{max}}$ | Maximum mirror travel gives resolution | Resolution limits |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{FTIR} \propto \sqrt{N}$ | Fellgett advantage, $N$ = number of scans | SNR improvement |
| 8 | $E_{vib} = \left(v+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)h\nu$ | Vibrational energy (quantum) | Energy levels |
| 9 | Selection rule: $\Delta v = \pm 1$ | Allowed transitions | IR activity condition |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial q} \neq 0$ | Dipole moment change | IR-active vibration condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduced mass — critical KTU formula**: For a C=O bond, $m_C = 12$, $m_O = 16$ (atomic mass units). $\mu = \dfrac{12 \times 16}{12+16} = 6.857$ amu. With $k \approx 1000$ N/m, the calculated $\tilde{\nu}_{C=O} \approx 1730$ cm⁻¹ — matching the experimental value.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer industry**: Identifying polymer type (PE, PP, PET, PMMA), monitoring curing, detecting oxidation.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Polymorph identification, API-excipient compatibility.
- **Semiconductors**: Si-O, Si-H bond quantification in thin films.
- **Forensics**: Identification of unknown liquids, drugs, contaminants.
- **Nanomaterials**: Surface functionalization verification on graphene, CNTs, quantum dots.
- **Environmental science**: Microplastics identification, oil spill analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Hooke's Law for Vibrational Frequency of a Diatomic Bond

Consider a diatomic molecule A–B with masses $m_A$ and $m_B$ connected by a chemical bond that behaves as a Hookean spring with force constant $k$. The classical vibrational frequency is derived below.

**Step 1:** Newton's second law for atom A:

$$m_A \ddot{x}_A = -k(x_A - x_B)$$

**Step 2:** Newton's second law for atom B (opposite direction of force):

$$m_B \ddot{x}_B = -k(x_B - x_A)$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the two equations:

$$m_A \ddot{x}_A - m_B \ddot{x}_B = -2k(x_A - x_B)$$

**Step 4:** Introduce the relative displacement $q = x_A - x_B$:

$$\ddot{q} = \ddot{x}_A - \ddot{x}_B$$

**Step 5:** Divide the relative motion equation by the reduced mass:

$$\mu = \dfrac{m_A m_B}{m_A + m_B}$$

**Step 6:** Obtain the equation of simple harmonic motion:

$$\mu \ddot{q} + 2k q = 0$$

**Step 7:** Solution gives angular frequency:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{\mu}}$$

**Step 8:** Convert angular frequency $\omega$ to ordinary frequency $\nu$:

$$\nu = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{\mu}}$$

**Step 9:** Convert to wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$ (used in IR spectroscopy):

$$\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{\nu}{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{\mu}}$$

The factor of 2 is often absorbed into $k$ (using $k' = 2k$), giving the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — The Interferogram Equation

**Step 1:** Consider an ideal beam splitter that divides an incoming monochromatic plane wave of wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}_0$ and amplitude $E_0$ into two equal-amplitude beams.

**Step 2:** Beam 1 travels to the fixed mirror (distance $L_1$), reflects, returns. Total optical path: $2L_1$.

**Step 3:** Beam 2 travels to the moving mirror (distance $L_2$), reflects, returns. Total optical path: $2L_2$.

**Step 4:** The optical path difference (retardation) between the two recombining beams:

$$\delta = 2(L_2 - L_1) = 2x$$

where $x$ is the displacement of the moving mirror from the zero-path-difference (ZPD) position.

**Step 5:** The phase difference between the two beams at recombination:

$$\phi = 2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta$$

**Step 6:** The two electric fields interfere constructively or destructively. By superposition, the resulting amplitude is proportional to:

$$E_{total} \propto 2E_0 \cos\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = 2E_0 \cos(\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta)$$

**Step 7:** The detected intensity is proportional to the square of the amplitude:

$$I(\delta) = 2 E_0^2 \cos^2(\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta) = E_0^2 [1 + \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta)]$$

**Step 8:** Since the detector cannot follow the optical frequency, only the modulated AC component is recorded. Dropping the constant DC offset $E_0^2$:

$$I(\delta) = B(\tilde{\nu}_0)\cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu}_0 \delta)$$

where $B(\tilde{\nu}_0) = E_0^2$ is the spectral intensity of the source at wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}_0$.

**Step 9:** For a polychromatic source containing all wavenumbers from $0$ to $\infty$, the detected intensity is the sum (integral) over all wavenumbers:

$$\boxed{I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu})\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\,d\tilde{\nu}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Inverse Fourier Transform (Recovering the Spectrum)

The forward transform (interferogram) is known. To recover the spectrum $B(\tilde{\nu})$, we use the orthogonality of cosine functions.

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides of the interferogram equation by $\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)$ and integrate over $\delta$ from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} I(\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)\,d\delta = \int_{0}^{\infty}\int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu})\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)\,d\tilde{\nu}\,d\delta$$

**Step 2:** Swap the order of integration (Fubini's theorem, justified because $I(\delta)$ is square-integrable):

$$= \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu})\left[\int_{0}^{\infty}\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)\,d\delta\right]\,d\tilde{\nu}$$

**Step 3:** Use the orthogonality identity:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty}\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)\,d\delta = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta(\tilde{\nu}-\tilde{\nu}')$$

where $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta function.

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} I(\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)\,d\delta = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu})\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\delta(\tilde{\nu}-\tilde{\nu}')\,d\tilde{\nu}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the sifting property of the Dirac delta:

$$= \tfrac{1}{2}B(\tilde{\nu}')$$

**Step 6:** Relabel $\tilde{\nu}' \rightarrow \tilde{\nu}$ and rearrange:

$$\boxed{B(\tilde{\nu}) = 2\int_{0}^{\infty} I(\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\,d\delta}$$

This is the **inverse Fourier cosine transform** — the mathematical heart of every FTIR spectrometer.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Numerical Worked Example (Resolution Calculation)

**Problem:** A moving mirror in a Michelson interferometer travels a maximum distance of $\delta_{max} = 0.5$ cm. Calculate the spectral resolution of the FTIR spectrometer.

**Step 1:** The resolution $\Delta\tilde{\nu}$ (in cm⁻¹) of an FTIR is governed by the maximum optical path difference:

$$\Delta\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\delta_{max}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the value:

$$\Delta\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{0.5 \text{ cm}} = 2 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation: The instrument can distinguish two absorption bands that are separated by at least **2 cm⁻¹**. To achieve 0.5 cm⁻¹ resolution (high-resolution FTIR), the mirror must travel $\delta_{max} = 2$ cm.

**Step 4:** Engineering note: Modern research-grade FTIRs achieve 0.001 cm⁻¹ resolution, requiring mirror travel of 10 m — requiring extremely precise linear stages.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of FTIR Mode

| Sample Type | Best Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Transparent thin film | Transmission (KBr pellet) | Clean baseline, quantitative |
| Opaque solid | ATR | Minimal preparation, surface-sensitive |
| Powder (catalyst) | DRIFT | No pellet pressing required |
| Liquid (oil, solvent) | ATR or sealed cell | No cell window issues |
| Gas | Long-path gas cell | High sensitivity |
| Polymer film | Transmission or ATR | Depends on thickness |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an FTIR Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    bsA --> mmA[Fixed Mirror\nStationary M1]
    bsA --> mmB[Moving Mirror\nLinear Stage M2]
    mmA --> bsA
    mmB --> bsA
    bsA --> smpA[Sample\nCompartment]
    smpA --> detA[Detector\nDTGS or MCT]
    detA --> adcA[Analog to\nDigital Converter]
    adcA --> cpuA[Computer\nFFT Processor]
    cpuA --> dspA[Spectrum Display\nPercent Transmittance vs Wavenumber]
    laserA[He-Ne Laser\n632.8 nm Reference] --> bsA
    cpuA -.Mirror position feedback.-> mmB
```

```mermaid

    startA[Start] --> prepA[Sample Preparation\nKBr pellet or ATR]
    prepA --> loadA[Place Sample in\nSample Compartment]
    loadA --> scanA[Acquire Interferogram\nMirror scans full range]
    scanA --> coaddA[Co-add Multiple Scans\nTypically 32 to 1024]
    coaddA --> fftA[Apply Fast Fourier Transform\nFFT Algorithm]
    fftA --> ratioA[Compute Absorbance or\nTransmittance Spectrum]
    ratioA --> baseA[Baseline Correction\nand Smoothing]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationship Between Interferogram and Spectrum

```mermaid
    domainB --Forward Fourier Transform--> domainA
    domainA --> charA[Center burst at ZPD\nZero Path Difference]
    domainA --> charB[Decays on both sides\nof center burst]
    domainB --> charC[Peaks correspond to\nabsorbing functional groups]

```

```mermaid
    regionB --> bondB2[C triple bond N\n2200 to 2260]

    regionC --> bondC2[C double bond C\n1620 to 1680]
```
