# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the principle of FTIR spectroscopy. Mention the role of a beam splitter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

FTIR spectroscopy is based on the principle that molecules absorb specific frequencies of infrared radiation corresponding to the natural vibrational frequencies of their chemical bonds. When IR radiation of the same frequency as a molecular vibration strikes the molecule, energy is absorbed and the molecule transitions to an excited vibrational state.

A **beam splitter** in the Michelson interferometer divides the incoming IR beam into two paths — one reflected toward a fixed mirror, the other transmitted toward a moving mirror. On recombination, the two beams interfere, producing an **interferogram** that is later Fourier-transformed into a spectrum.

*(1 mark — principle, 1 mark — vibrational transitions, 1 mark — beam splitter role)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is an interferogram? Why is Fourier transform needed to obtain the IR spectrum?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **interferogram** is the plot of detector signal intensity versus the optical path difference $\delta$ as the moving mirror of the Michelson interferometer translates. At zero path difference (ZPD), all wavelengths interfere constructively, producing a large **center burst**.

The interferogram is a time-domain (or path-domain) signal containing information about all IR frequencies mixed together. To extract the spectrum (intensity versus wavenumber), the **Fourier transform** is applied, which mathematically decomposes the interferogram into its constituent frequency components.

*(1.5 marks — interferogram definition, 1.5 marks — need for Fourier transform)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Michelson interferometer used in FTIR spectroscopy with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the interferogram equation for a polychromatic source and show how the spectrum is recovered using the inverse Fourier transform. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

The Michelson interferometer consists of:

1. **Beam splitter** — A thin film of germanium-coated KBr (or CaF₂) that reflects 50% and transmits 50% of the incident IR beam.

2. **Fixed mirror (M₁)** — Stationary mirror placed at distance $L_1$ from the beam splitter. Returns the reflected beam back to the beam splitter.

3. **Moving mirror (M₂)** — Translates along the optical axis at distance $L_2$ from the beam splitter. The translation is precisely controlled (often using a He-Ne laser reference).

4. **Compensating plate** — A plate identical to the beam splitter substrate placed in the fixed-mirror arm to equalize the optical path traversed through the substrate material in both arms.

**Working Principle:**

- Broadband IR from the source falls on the beam splitter.
- The beam splitter creates two coherent beams — one reflected to M₁, one transmitted to M₂.
- Both beams return and recombine at the beam splitter.
- The recombined beam is directed through the sample to the detector.
- The optical path difference $\delta = 2(L_2 - L_1)$ changes as M₂ moves.
- The detector records intensity $I(\delta)$ versus $\delta$ — this is the interferogram.

**Key feature**: A small He-Ne laser (632.8 nm) is co-aligned with the IR beam to provide an internal calibration for mirror position and sampling rate (Connes advantage — gives wavenumber accuracy better than 0.01 cm⁻¹).

*(2 marks — components, 3 marks — working with labeled diagram, 2 marks — laser reference and Connes advantage)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation:**

For a monochromatic source of wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}_0$:

$$I(\delta) = B(\tilde{\nu}_0)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}_0 \delta)$$

For a polychromatic source with spectral distribution $B(\tilde{\nu})$, integrating over all wavenumbers:

$$\boxed{I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu})\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\,d\tilde{\nu}}$$

To recover $B(\tilde{\nu})$, multiply both sides by $\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)$ and integrate:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} I(\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)\,d\delta = \int_{0}^{\infty}B(\tilde{\nu})\left[\int_{0}^{\infty}\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}'\delta)\,d\delta\right]d\tilde{\nu}$$

Using orthogonality, the inner integral gives $\tfrac{1}{2}\delta(\tilde{\nu}-\tilde{\nu}')$, which sifts to:

$$= \tfrac{1}{2}B(\tilde{\nu}')$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{B(\tilde{\nu}) = 2\int_{0}^{\infty} I(\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\,d\delta}$$

This is the inverse Fourier cosine transform. In practice, FFT algorithm is used.

*(2 marks — monochromatic case, 2 marks — polychromatic integral, 3 marks — inverse transform derivation using orthogonality)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Compare dispersive IR and FTIR spectroscopy. List the advantages of FTIR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diatomic molecule has a bond force constant of 1000 N/m and reduced mass of $1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg. Calculate its vibrational frequency and wavenumber. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | Dispersive IR | FTIR |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength selection | Grating/prism (sequential) | Michelson interferometer (multiplex) |
| Scan time | 10–15 minutes | Few seconds |
| Signal-to-noise ratio | Low | High (Fellgett advantage) |
| Throughput (light reaching detector) | Low (narrow slits) | High (no slits — Jacquinot advantage) |
| Wavenumber accuracy | $\sim$ 0.5 cm⁻¹ | $< 0.01$ cm⁻¹ (Connes advantage) |
| Stray light | Significant problem | Negligible |
| Mechanical complexity | Grating/prism drive | Moving mirror with laser reference |

**Advantages of FTIR:**

1. **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage** — All wavenumbers measured simultaneously; SNR improves as $\sqrt{N}$ where $N$ is the number of resolution elements.

2. **Jacquinot (Throughput) Advantage** — No narrow entrance/exit slits → much higher light throughput → better SNR.

3. **Connes Advantage** — Internal He-Ne laser provides highly accurate wavenumber calibration.

4. **Rapid Scanning** — Enables time-resolved studies and signal averaging.

5. **High Resolution** — Achievable with longer mirror travel.

6. **Computer-Based Operation** — Easy storage, manipulation, library search.

*(3 marks — comparison table, 4 marks — advantages with explanation)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $k = 1000$ N/m, $\mu = 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg

**Step 1:** Calculate vibrational frequency using Hooke's law:

$$\nu = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$\nu = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{1.66 \times 10^{-27}}}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the ratio:

$$\dfrac{1000}{1.66 \times 10^{-27}} = 6.024 \times 10^{29} \text{ s}^{-2}$$

**Step 4:** Take square root:

$$\sqrt{6.024 \times 10^{29}} = 7.762 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $2\pi$:

$$\nu = \dfrac{7.762 \times 10^{14}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{7.762 \times 10^{14}}{6.283} = 1.235 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate wavenumber (using $c = 2.998 \times 10^{10}$ cm/s):

$$\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{\nu}{c} = \dfrac{1.235 \times 10^{14}}{2.998 \times 10^{10}} = 4120 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 7:** Physical interpretation:

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 1.24 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}, \quad \tilde{\nu} \approx 4120 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$

This wavenumber falls in the O-H or N-H stretching region — consistent with light hydrogen bonds. For heavier atoms, $\tilde{\nu}$ shifts to lower values.

*(2 marks — formula, 2 marks — frequency calculation, 2 marks — wavenumber, 1 mark — interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in optical path difference: $\delta = 2x$, not $x$. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Mixing up selection rule**: IR activity requires $\partial \mu / \partial q \neq 0$, **not** $\Delta v = \pm 1$. The latter is a quantum mechanical selection rule; both must be stated.
> 3. **Conflating resolution with accuracy**: Resolution = $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = 1/\delta_{max}$; Accuracy is governed by laser reference.
> 4. **Unit errors**: Wavenumber in cm⁻¹ (not m⁻¹) when used in IR spectroscopy; convert $\nu$ (Hz) to $\tilde{\nu}$ (cm⁻¹) using $c$ in cm/s.
> 5. **Misspelling "interferogram"** as "interferogramme" or "interferograph" — penalised in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Missing the Fellgett advantage factor $\sqrt{N}$** in SNR comparison — easy 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FTIR Spectroscopy**

- **Principle** — Absorption of IR radiation by molecular vibrations (covalent bonds) gives structural information.
- **IR range** — Mid-IR 4000–400 cm⁻¹ is the diagnostic region; far-IR and near-IR have limited use.
- **Selection rule** — $\partial\mu/\partial q \neq 0$ (dipole moment must change during vibration).
- **Hooke's law for vibrations** — $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$. Stronger bonds (larger $k$) → higher $\tilde{\nu}$. Lighter atoms (smaller $\mu$) → higher $\tilde{\nu}$.
- **Group frequencies**:
  - O–H stretch: 3200–3600 cm⁻¹ (broad)
  - N–H stretch: 3300–3500 cm⁻¹
  - C–H stretch: 2850–3000 cm⁻¹
  - C=O stretch: 1650–1750 cm⁻¹ (strong, sharp)
  - C=C stretch: 1620–1680 cm⁻¹
  - C–O stretch: 1000–1300 cm⁻¹
- **Michelson interferometer** — beam splitter + fixed mirror + moving mirror + He-Ne laser reference.
- **Interferogram** — $I(\delta) = \int B(\tilde{\nu})\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\,d\tilde{\nu}$.
- **Spectrum recovery** — $B(\tilde{\nu}) = 2\int I(\delta)\cos(2\pi\tilde{\nu}\delta)\,d\delta$ (inverse Fourier cosine transform).
- **Resolution** — $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = 1/\delta_{max}$.
- **Three FTIR advantages**:
  - Fellgett (multiplex): SNR $\propto \sqrt{N}$.
  - Jacquinot (throughput): No narrow slits.
  - Connes (accuracy): He-Ne laser calibration.
- **Sample preparation** — KBr pellet (transmission), ATR (reflectance, surface), DRIFT (powder).
- **Common detectors** — DTGS (room temperature), MCT (liquid-N₂ cooled, faster, more sensitive).
- **Common sources** — Globar (SiC, mid-IR), Nernst glower, Tungsten-halogen (NIR).
- **Practical tip** — Always record a **background spectrum** (no sample) and ratio it to the sample spectrum to remove atmospheric H₂O and CO₂ contributions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raman Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Raman Spectroscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Raman Spectroscopy** is a non-destructive vibrational spectroscopic technique based on the **inelastic scattering of monochromatic light** (typically from a laser source) by a sample, leading to characteristic frequency shifts that correspond to the molecular vibrational, rotational, and other low-frequency modes of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEPET412 Module 4):** Raman Spectroscopy is studied as a complementary optical characterization technique to IR spectroscopy, with emphasis on its **principle, instrumentation, selection rules, and applications in materials characterization**.

The phenomenon underlying this technique is known as the **Raman Effect** (or **Raman Scattering**), discovered by Nobel laureate **Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman** in **1928** (Nobel Prize in Physics, 1930). When a photon of incident light interacts with a molecule, the majority of the scattered photons are elastically scattered (Rayleigh scattering) with no change in frequency. However, a small fraction (~1 in 10⁶ to 1 in 10⁸) undergoes **inelastic scattering**, wherein energy is either transferred to the molecule (Stokes scattering) or extracted from it (anti-Stokes scattering). The frequency difference between the incident and scattered photons is termed the **Raman shift**, denoted **Δν̃** and expressed in **wavenumbers (cm⁻¹)**.

The fundamental relationship governing the Raman shift is:

$$
\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \tilde{\nu}_0 - \tilde{\nu}_{scattered} = \pm \tilde{\nu}_{vib}
$$

where:
- $\tilde{\nu}_0$ = wavenumber of the incident (laser) radiation
- $\tilde{\nu}_{scattered}$ = wavenumber of the scattered radiation
- $\tilde{\nu}_{vib}$ = vibrational wavenumber of the molecular mode

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Maraca and the Bell"**
> Imagine you shake a maraca containing loose seeds. Most of the time, the seeds simply bounce around without losing energy — this is **Rayleigh (elastic) scattering** (frequency unchanged). Occasionally, a seed strikes the wall and loses some kinetic energy, producing a slightly different (lower-pitched) sound — this is **Stokes scattering** (photon loses energy to molecule). Even more rarely, a seed might already be vibrating vigorously and gives a little kick to the wall, gaining extra energy and producing a higher-pitched sound — this is **anti-Stokes scattering** (photon gains energy from molecule). The "shift" in sound corresponds exactly to the molecular vibration energy. In Raman spectroscopy, we listen carefully for these tiny frequency shifts using laser light as the "rattle."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Raman work?** The oscillating electric field of the incident laser light polarizes the electron cloud of a molecule, creating a short-lived induced dipole. Vibrational motion modulates this polarizability, and only vibrations that *change* the molecular polarizability are Raman-active.

## 1.3 Key Constants & Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
- **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$
- **Typical Raman shift range:** $50 - 4000 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$
- **Typical incident laser wavelengths:** $532 \ \text{nm}$ (Nd:YAG doubled), $633 \ \text{nm}$ (He-Ne), $785 \ \text{nm}$ (diode), $1064 \ \text{nm}$ (Nd:YAG fundamental)
- **Resolution of modern Raman spectrometers:** $0.5 - 2 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$
- **Raman scattering cross-section:** typically $10^{-30}$ to $10^{-25} \ \text{cm}^{2}$/molecule (very small — this is why Raman is a weak effect)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

### Step 1: Polarizability Tensor — The Heart of Raman Activity

When a molecule is placed in an oscillating electric field $\vec{E}$ of the incident light, an induced dipole moment $\vec{\mu}_{ind}$ is created:

$$
\vec{\mu}_{ind} = \overset{\leftrightarrow}{\alpha} \cdot \vec{E}
$$

Here, $\overset{\leftrightarrow}{\alpha}$ is the **polarizability tensor** — a 3×3 matrix that describes how easily the electron cloud can be distorted. The components are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_x &= \alpha_{xx} E_x + \alpha_{xy} E_y + \alpha_{xz} E_z \\
\mu_y &= \alpha_{yx} E_x + \alpha_{yy} E_y + \alpha_{yz} E_z \\
\mu_z &= \alpha_{zx} E_x + \alpha_{zy} E_y + \alpha_{zz} E_z
\end{aligned}
$$

For Raman activity, a molecular vibration must change at least **one component of the polarizability tensor**:

$$
\left( \frac{\partial \alpha_{ij}}{\partial Q} \right)_0 \neq 0
$$

where $Q$ is the normal coordinate of vibration. This is the **Raman Selection Rule**.

### Step 2: Classical Treatment — Origin of the Three Scattering Types

For a diatomic-like mode of frequency $\nu_{vib}$, the polarizability is modulated as:

$$
\alpha(t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_Q \cos(2\pi \nu_{vib} t)
$$

Substituting this into the induced dipole with $E(t) = E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)$ gives three frequency components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{ind}(t) &= \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t) \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{2} \alpha_Q E_0 \cos[2\pi (\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}) t] \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{2} \alpha_Q E_0 \cos[2\pi (\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}) t]
\end{aligned}
$$

| Component | Frequency | Name | Type |
|-----------|-----------|------|------|
| Term 1 | $\nu_0$ | Rayleigh line | **Elastic** scattering |
| Term 2 | $\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$ | Stokes line | **Inelastic** (photon loses energy) |
| Term 3 | $\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$ | Anti-Stokes line | **Inelastic** (photon gains energy) |

### Step 3: Quantum Mechanical Picture — Virtual States

In quantum language, scattering proceeds through a short-lived **virtual state** (not a real eigenstate) reached by absorbing a photon of energy $h\nu_0$. From the virtual state, the molecule relaxes by emitting a photon. Three pathways exist:

- **Rayleigh:** Returns to original vibrational level $\rightarrow$ scattered photon has $h\nu_0$.
- **Stokes:** Relaxes to a *higher* vibrational level (typically $v=1$) $\rightarrow$ scattered photon has $h(\nu_0 - \nu_{vib})$.
- **Anti-Stokes:** Relaxes to a *lower* vibrational level (returns to $v=0$ from a thermally populated $v=1$) $\rightarrow$ scattered photon has $h(\nu_0 + \nu_{vib})$.

### Step 4: Stokes vs Anti-Stokes Intensity Ratio (Boltzmann Factor)

At thermal equilibrium, the population of vibrational level $v$ follows the Boltzmann distribution:

$$
\frac{N_v}{N_0} = \exp\!\left( -\frac{h c \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T} \right)
$$

Because the ground state ($v=0$) is more populated, **Stokes lines are always more intense than anti-Stokes lines**. The intensity ratio is:

$$
\frac{I_{anti\text{-}Stokes}}{I_{Stokes}} = \left( \frac{\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}}{\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}} \right)^4 \exp\!\left( \frac{h c \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fourth-power dependence on frequency comes from the $\nu^4$ scaling of induced dipole radiation, while the Boltzmann exponential accounts for population differences.

### Step 5: Depolarization Ratio — Symmetry Probe

Using plane-polarized laser light, the depolarization ratio is:

$$
\rho = \frac{I_{\perp}}{I_{\parallel}}
$$

where $I_{\parallel}$ and $I_{\perp}$ are the scattered intensities polarized parallel and perpendicular to the incident polarization. Values:

- $\rho < 0.75$ → **polarized band** (totally symmetric vibration, e.g., $A_g$ modes)
- $\rho = 0.75$ → **depolarized band** (non-totally symmetric vibration)
- $\rho > 0.75$ → anomalous — indicates resonance effects

## 2.2 Mutual Exclusion Principle (Raman vs IR)

For molecules possessing a **center of symmetry (inversion center, $i$)**, the rule is:

> **"If a vibrational mode is IR-active, it is Raman-inactive, and vice versa."**

| Property | IR Activity Requirement | Raman Activity Requirement |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Change in | **Dipole moment** $\partial \mu / \partial Q \neq 0$ | **Polarizability** $\partial \alpha / \partial Q \neq 0$ |
| Symmetric stretch of CO₂ | IR **inactive** | Raman **active** (1330 cm⁻¹) |
| Asymmetric stretch of CO₂ | IR **active** (2349 cm⁻¹) | Raman **inactive** |
| Homonuclear diatomics (N₂, O₂, H₂) | IR **inactive** (no dipole change) | Raman **active** |

This is why Raman and IR are considered **complementary techniques** — together they provide a complete vibrational picture of any material.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raman shift | $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \tilde{\nu}_0 - \tilde{\nu}_{scat}$ | $\text{cm}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Induced dipole | $\mu_{ind} = \alpha E$ | C·m |
| 3 | Polarizability modulation | $\alpha(t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_Q \cos(2\pi \nu_{vib} t)$ | — |
| 4 | Stokes condition | $\nu_{scat} = \nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Anti-Stokes condition | $\nu_{scat} = \nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Stokes/Anti-Stokes intensity ratio | $\dfrac{I_{aS}}{I_{S}} = \left(\dfrac{\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}}{\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}}\right)^4 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{hc\tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Boltzmann vibrational population | $\dfrac{N_v}{N_0} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{hc\tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Raman intensity (classical) | $I_{Raman} \propto \nu_0^4 \ \left(\partial \alpha / \partial Q \right)^2 \ I_0$ | — |
| 9 | Depolarization ratio | $\rho = I_{\perp} / I_{\parallel}$ | 0 to 0.75 typical |
| 10 | Laser wavelength vs shift | Smaller $\lambda_0$ $\rightarrow$ stronger Raman (via $\nu^4$) | — |
| 11 | Energy of scattered photon | $E_{scat} = h \nu_0 \mp h \nu_{vib}$ | J |
| 12 | Selection rule — Raman | $\partial \alpha_{ij} / \partial Q \neq 0$ | — |
| 13 | Selection rule — IR | $\partial \mu / \partial Q \neq 0$ | — |
| 14 | Wavenumber conversion | $\tilde{\nu} = 1 / \lambda$ | $\text{cm}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Raman spectroscopy is heavily used in:
> - **Polymer and pharmaceutical industry** — polymorphism detection, crystalline vs amorphous identification
> - **Semiconductor industry** — stress mapping, doping profiling, crystalline quality assessment
> - **Carbon materials** — distinguishing diamond, graphite, graphene, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes via D, G, 2D bands
> - **Geology & mineralogy** — in situ identification of mineral phases
> - **Biomedical diagnostics** — SERS-based detection of cancer biomarkers
> - **Art and archaeology** — non-destructive pigment identification

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Classical Three-Line Spectrum from Modulated Polarizability

**Goal:** Show mathematically how Rayleigh, Stokes, and Anti-Stokes lines arise from the modulated polarizability.

**Step 1 — The driving electric field.** Monochromatic plane-polarized laser light is represented as:

$$
E(t) = E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)
$$

This field has angular frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi \nu_0$.

**Step 2 — The molecular response.** The polarizability of a molecule along a normal mode $Q$ varies linearly with the vibrational displacement. If the molecule is vibrating with frequency $\nu_{vib}$, the polarizability is:

$$
\alpha(t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_Q \cos(2\pi \nu_{vib} t)
$$

Here $\alpha_0$ is the equilibrium polarizability and $\alpha_Q = \left( \partial \alpha / \partial Q \right)_0$ is its derivative with respect to the normal coordinate at equilibrium.

**Step 3 — Induced dipole moment.** Multiplying:

$$
\mu_{ind}(t) = \alpha(t) \cdot E(t) = \left[ \alpha_0 + \alpha_Q \cos(2\pi \nu_{vib} t) \right] \cdot E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)
$$

**Step 4 — Expansion using the product-to-sum identity.** Using $\cos A \cdot \cos B = \frac{1}{2} [\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{ind}(t) &= \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t) \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{2} \alpha_Q E_0 \cos\!\left[ 2\pi (\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}) t \right] \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{2} \alpha_Q E_0 \cos\!\left[ 2\pi (\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}) t \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation of each term.**

- **First term** oscillates at $\nu_0$ — this is the **Rayleigh (elastic) scattering** because the molecule returns to its original state.
- **Second term** oscillates at $\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$ — the photon has lost energy $h\nu_{vib}$ to the molecule — this is **Stokes scattering**.
- **Third term** oscillates at $\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$ — the molecule has given vibrational energy to the photon — this is **anti-Stokes scattering**.

**Step 6 — Power radiated by an oscillating dipole.** A dipole oscillating at frequency $\nu$ radiates power proportional to $\nu^4$. Hence the Raman intensity scales as:

$$
I_{Raman} \propto \nu_{scat}^4 \ \left( \frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q} \right)^2 \ I_0
$$

This explains why **shorter-wavelength (higher-frequency) lasers produce stronger Raman signals** — a key practical consideration.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stokes/Anti-Stokes Intensity Ratio

**Step 1 — Starting point.** The intensity is proportional to the population of the initial state multiplied by the fourth-power frequency factor:

$$
\frac{I_{aS}}{I_S} = \left( \frac{\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}}{\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}} \right)^4 \cdot \frac{N_{v=1}}{N_{v=0}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boltzmann distribution** at temperature $T$:

$$
\frac{N_{v=1}}{N_{v=0}} = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T} \right) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{h c \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$
\frac{I_{aS}}{I_S} = \left( \frac{\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}}{\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}} \right)^4 \exp\!\left( \frac{h c \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T} \right)
$$

(Note: the sign in the exponential flips because we inverted the population ratio.)

**Step 4 — Sample numerical check.** For a C–H stretch with $\tilde{\nu}_{vib} = 2900 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$ at $T = 300 \ \text{K}$ using a 532 nm laser ($\tilde{\nu}_0 \approx 18797 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{h c \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T} &= \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{10})(2900)}{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)} \\
&= \frac{5.760 \times 10^{-20}}{4.143 \times 10^{-21}} \\
&\approx 13.90
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\exp(13.90) \approx 1.09 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\left( \frac{18797 + 2900}{18797 - 2900} \right)^4 = (1.359)^4 \approx 3.41
$$

$$
\frac{I_{aS}}{I_S} \approx 3.41 \times 1.09 \times 10^6 / \text{something too small}
$$

Wait — corrected: because $\exp(-13.90)$ enters when we go from $N_1/N_0$:

$$
\frac{N_{v=1}}{N_{v=0}} = e^{-13.90} \approx 9.16 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\frac{I_{aS}}{I_S} \approx 3.41 \times 9.16 \times 10^{-7} \approx 3.12 \times 10^{-6}
$$

This is why **anti-Stokes lines are nearly invisible** at room temperature for high-frequency modes — they are roughly a million times weaker than Stokes lines.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Raman Activity from Group Theory

**Step 1 — The polarizability tensor is a symmetric rank-2 tensor** — it transforms under the symmetric second-degree representation.

**Step 2 — For a Raman-active mode** with normal coordinate $Q_k$ of symmetry species $\Gamma_k$:

$$
\Gamma(Raman_{activity}) = \Gamma(\alpha)_{sym} = A_{1g} \ (in \ D_{\infty h}); \quad \text{or } A_g, \ B_{1g}, \ B_{2g}, \ B_{3g} \ (in \ D_{2h})
$$

**Step 3 — The selection rule in group-theoretic form:**

$$
\Gamma(Q_k) \otimes \Gamma(\alpha)_{sym} \supseteq A_g
$$

Equivalently, a mode is Raman-active if its symmetry species appears in the decomposition of the polarizability tensor.

**Worked example — CO₂ (linear, $D_{\infty h}$ point group):**

The three vibrational modes are:
- $\nu_1$ (symmetric stretch) — symmetry $\Sigma_g^+$
- $\nu_2$ (bending, doubly degenerate) — symmetry $\Pi_u$
- $\nu_3$ (asymmetric stretch) — symmetry $\Sigma_u^+$

Polarizability components $\alpha_{xx}, \alpha_{yy}, \alpha_{zz}, \alpha_{xy}, \alpha_{xz}, \alpha_{yz}$ transform as $\Sigma_g^+ + \Pi_g$ in $D_{\infty h}$.

- $\Sigma_g^+ \otimes \Sigma_g^+ = \Sigma_g^+$ → **Raman-active** ✓
- $\Pi_u \otimes (\Sigma_g^+ + \Pi_g)$ → contains no $\Sigma_g^+$ → **Raman-inactive** ✗
- $\Sigma_u^+ \otimes (\Sigma_g^+ + \Pi_g)$ → contains no $\Sigma_g^+$ → **Raman-inactive** ✗

Result: **Only the symmetric stretch ($\nu_1 \approx 1330 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$) is Raman-active for CO₂.** Conversely, $\nu_2$ and $\nu_3$ are IR-active. This is a textbook example of the **mutual exclusion principle**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Raman vs IR Spectroscopy

| Parameter | Raman Spectroscopy | IR Spectroscopy |
|---|---|---|
| Physical phenomenon | **Inelastic light scattering** | **Absorption of IR light** |
| Selection rule | Change in **polarizability** $\partial \alpha / \partial Q \neq 0$ | Change in **dipole moment** $\partial \mu / \partial Q \neq 0$ |
| Sample preparation | Minimal — solids, liquids, gases | Mostly solid in KBr pellet, or liquid cells |
| Water interference | **Weak Raman signal from H₂O** — excellent for aqueous/bio samples | **Strong IR absorption from H₂O** — problematic |
| Symmetric stretches (e.g., N₂, O₂) | **Strong** | **Forbidden** |
| Symmetric vs asymmetric CO₂ | Symmetric stretch Raman-active | Asymmetric stretch IR-active |
| Sample volume needed | µL (microsampling possible) | mg (larger amount typical) |
| Glass interference | Tolerable with visible lasers | Glass absorbs strongly — use special cells |
| Fluorescence problem | Major issue (visible lasers) | None |
| Quantitative capability | Moderate (with care) | Good (Beer's law applies) |
| Spatial resolution | ~1 µm (with confocal microscope) | ~10–20 µm (FT-IR microspectroscopy) |
| In situ / in vivo use | Yes — fiber optic probes common | Limited |
| Frequency scaling | $I \propto \nu_0^4$ — strong enhancement at short wavelength | — |

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Raman Scattering Cross-Section Scaling

The differential Raman cross-section for Stokes scattering is:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega} \propto \omega_L^4 \left| e_s \cdot \overset{\leftrightarrow}{\alpha}' \cdot e_L \right|^2
$$

where $e_L$ and $e_s$ are the unit polarization vectors of the incident laser and scattered light, and $\overset{\leftrightarrow}{\alpha}'$ is the derivative of the polarizability tensor. This explains:

- The fourth-power frequency dependence (use blue/green lasers to boost signal)
- The polarization selection rules (parallel vs perpendicular band intensities)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Raman Effect. Distinguish between Stokes and anti-Stokes lines.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question (May 2024 Pattern)]** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Raman Effect** is the inelastic scattering of monochromatic light by a molecule, in which the frequency of the scattered light differs from that of the incident light by an amount equal to a vibrational frequency of the molecule.

The difference arises in two ways:

- **Stokes lines:** The scattered photon has **lower** frequency ($\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$) than the incident photon. The molecule gains vibrational energy. Stokes lines are **more intense** because they originate from the more populated ground state ($v=0$).

- **Anti-Stokes lines:** The scattered photon has **higher** frequency ($\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$) than the incident photon. The molecule loses vibrational energy. Anti-Stokes lines are **weaker** because they require the molecule to be initially in an excited vibrational state ($v=1$), whose population follows the Boltzmann factor.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Rayleigh line** lies at the incident frequency $\nu_0$ and corresponds to elastic scattering.

---

### Question 2: State the selection rule for Raman activity. Why is a homonuclear diatomic molecule like N₂ Raman-active but IR-inactive?

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question (Dec 2023 Pattern)]** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A vibrational mode is **Raman-active** if it produces a change in the **polarizability** of the molecule, expressed mathematically as:

$$
\left( \frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q} \right)_0 \neq 0
$$

For **homonuclear diatomic molecules** such as N₂, H₂, or O₂:

- The molecule has **no permanent dipole moment** because both atoms have identical electronegativity. As a result, the dipole moment remains zero at all internuclear distances, and $\partial \mu / \partial Q = 0$ — hence **IR-inactive**.

- However, the symmetric stretch causes the **electron cloud to expand and contract**, so the polarizability changes with bond length: $\partial \alpha / \partial Q \neq 0$ — hence **Raman-active**.

This is a direct consequence of the **mutual exclusion principle** for centrosymmetric molecules.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A: 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question (Module 4, PEPET412)]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of Raman spectroscopy** with a neat energy-level diagram. Discuss the origin of Stokes, anti-Stokes, and Rayleigh scattering using the classical theory. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **instrumentation of a typical Raman spectrometer** with a block diagram, covering the laser source, sample illumination, Rayleigh rejection, dispersion, and detection. Compare dispersive Raman with FT-Raman. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a)

**Principle:**
Raman spectroscopy is based on the **inelastic scattering of monochromatic light** by molecules. When a sample is irradiated with a laser of frequency $\nu_0$, most photons are scattered elastically (Rayleigh). A small fraction is scattered inelastically, with frequency shifts corresponding to the vibrational modes of the molecule. The frequency difference $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \tilde{\nu}_0 - \tilde{\nu}_{scat}$ is the **Raman shift**, plotted as the spectrum.

**Energy-level diagram (described in text — see Mermaid in Section 4.1):**

- **Rayleigh scattering:** Molecule absorbs photon, is excited to a virtual state, and returns to the *same* vibrational level — photon re-emitted at $\nu_0$.
- **Stokes scattering:** Molecule returns to a *higher* vibrational level — photon re-emitted at $\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$.
- **Anti-Stokes scattering:** Molecule was initially in $v=1$, returns to $v=0$ — photon re-emitted at $\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$.

**Classical derivation:**

The polarizability oscillates as the molecule vibrates:
$$
\alpha(t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_Q \cos(2\pi \nu_{vib} t)
$$
Multiplying with the incident field $E(t) = E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)$ and using the product-to-sum identity yields three oscillating terms at $\nu_0$, $\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$, and $\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$, corresponding exactly to Rayleigh, Stokes, and anti-Stokes scattering, respectively. (Full expansion shown in Section 3.1.)

The **Raman selection rule** is $\partial \alpha / \partial Q \neq 0$ — i.e., the vibration must change the polarizability.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Instrumentation — Block diagram (see Section 4.2):**

1. **Laser source:** Provides intense monochromatic light. Common choices: He-Ne (633 nm), Nd:YAG (532 nm or 1064 nm), diode laser (785 nm).
2. **Sample stage:** Bulk macro-stage or microscope objective for micro-Raman (1 µm spatial resolution).
3. **Rayleigh rejection filter:** Holographic notch filter or edge filter blocks the strong Rayleigh line (which would otherwise saturate the detector).
4. **Dispersion element:** Diffraction grating in a monochromator or polychromator separates frequencies.
5. **Detector:** Charge-coupled device (CCD) array for visible Raman; InGaAs or Ge for FT-Raman (1064 nm).
6. **Computer/Software:** Generates the intensity vs. Raman shift plot.

**Comparison — Dispersive Raman vs. FT-Raman:**

| Feature | Dispersive Raman | FT-Raman (NIR) |
|---|---|---|
| Laser wavelength | 532 / 633 / 785 nm | **1064 nm** (Nd:YAG) |
| Filter type | Notch / edge | Interferometer + filters |
| Detector | **CCD** (silicon) | **InGaAs or Ge** |
| Fluorescence | Major problem with visible | **Almost eliminated** (NIR) |
| Spectral range | $50 - 4000 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$ | $100 - 3500 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$ |
| Resolution | $0.5 - 2 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$ | $1 - 4 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$ |
| Speed | Slower (sequential) | **Faster** (Fellgett advantage) |
| Best for | Strong Raman-active samples | **Fluorescent samples** (polymers, biomolecules) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes in This Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting Rayleigh line** in the energy diagram — students often draw only Stokes and anti-Stokes, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong selection rule** — confusing IR ($\partial \mu / \partial Q \neq 0$) with Raman ($\partial \alpha / \partial Q \neq 0$) costs heavily.
> 3. **Confusing the Raman shift with frequency** — Raman shift is the *difference*, plotted on the x-axis as $\Delta \tilde{\nu}$ in $\text{cm}^{-1}$, *not* absolute frequency.
> 4. **No mention of virtual state** — examiners expect the term "virtual state" (not a real eigenstate) for full credit.
> 5. **In FT-Raman comparison**, students often write "FT-Raman uses a prism" — the correct element is a **Michelson interferometer**.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **mutual exclusion principle** with a suitable example. Compare Raman and IR spectroscopy in terms of selection rules, sample handling, and information content. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What are the **applications of Raman spectroscopy** in materials characterization? Explain its use in identifying carbon polymorphs (diamond, graphite, graphene) and in stress/strain analysis of semiconductors. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a)

**Mutual Exclusion Principle:**

For a molecule possessing a **center of symmetry** (inversion center, $i$), the principle states:

> A vibrational mode cannot be simultaneously IR-active and Raman-active.

- IR-active modes: $\partial \mu / \partial Q \neq 0$ — these have **ungerade (u)** symmetry.
- Raman-active modes: $\partial \alpha / \partial Q \neq 0$ — these have **gerade (g)** symmetry.

**Worked example — CO₂ ($D_{\infty h}$):**
- Symmetric stretch ($\Sigma_g^+$, 1330 cm⁻¹) → **Raman-active only**
- Bending ($\Pi_u$, 667 cm⁻¹) → **IR-active only**
- Asymmetric stretch ($\Sigma_u^+$, 2349 cm⁻¹) → **IR-active only**

The mode is either $g$ or $u$, never both — hence "mutual exclusion."

**Comparison Table — Raman vs IR:**

| Aspect | Raman | IR |
|---|---|---|
| Selection rule | $\partial \alpha / \partial Q \neq 0$ | $\partial \mu / \partial Q \neq 0$ |
| Aqueous samples | Excellent (water is weak) | Difficult (water absorbs) |
| Symmetric stretches | Strong | Weak/absent |
| Sample prep | Minimal | KBr pellet, ATR crystal |
| Glass containers | Tolerable | Not suitable |
| Quantitative | Moderate | Excellent (Beer-Lambert) |

**Conclusion:** Raman and IR are **complementary**; full vibrational characterization requires both.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Applications in Materials Characterization:**

Raman spectroscopy finds extensive use across multiple engineering domains:

1. **Pharmaceuticals:** Polymorph identification, amorphous vs crystalline discrimination, tablet uniformity.
2. **Polymers:** Degree of crystallinity, chain orientation, monitoring cure reactions.
3. **Biomaterials:** Disease diagnosis, protein secondary-structure analysis.
4. **Forensics and Art:** Non-destructive identification of pigments, drugs, explosives.
5. **2D Materials:** Layer count, defects, doping in graphene, MoS₂, hBN.
6. **Mineralogy:** Identification of mineral phases in rocks and soils.
7. **In situ reaction monitoring:** Real-time process control in reactors.

**Identification of Carbon Polymorphs:**

Each carbon allotrope has a distinctive Raman fingerprint:

| Material | Key Raman Bands (cm⁻¹) | Assignment |
|---|---|---|
| Diamond | **1332** | First-order $sp^3$ C–C stretch (single, sharp) |
| Graphite (single crystal) | **1582** (G band) | $E_{2g}$ in-plane $sp^2$ vibration |
| Disordered graphite / activated carbon | **1350** (D band) | Disorder-activated $A_{1g}$ |
| Graphene (monolayer) | **1580** (G), **2700** (2D) | G band + sharp 2D band (single Lorentzian) |
| Few-layer graphene | 1580 (G), 2700 (2D, multi-peak) | 2D shape reveals layer number |
| Carbon nanotube (SWCNT) | **100 - 300** (RBM), 1580 (G), 1350 (D) | Radial breathing mode + G + D |
| Fullerene (C₆₀) | **1469** | Pentagonal pinch mode $A_g(2)$ |

The **D band / G band intensity ratio** $I_D / I_G$ is widely used to estimate the **in-plane crystallite size** and **defect density** in graphitic carbons.

**Stress/Strain Analysis in Semiconductors:**

The Raman peak position in crystalline silicon (and other semiconductors) is sensitive to local stress. For Si, the unstrained Raman peak is at **520.5 cm⁻¹**.

- **Compressive stress** shifts the peak to **higher wavenumbers** (blue shift).
- **Tensile stress** shifts the peak to **lower wavenumbers** (red shift).

The relationship between peak shift $\Delta \tilde{\nu}$ and stress $\sigma$ (in GPa, for uniaxial stress) is approximately:

$$
\Delta \tilde{\nu} \ (\text{cm}^{-1}) \approx - 2.0 \ \sigma \quad \text{(for Si under tensile stress)}
$$

This enables **confocal Raman mapping** of stress distributions in MEMS devices, microelectronic chips, photovoltaic silicon, and heterostructures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong example for mutual exclusion** — students sometimes cite H₂O, but water is *not* centrosymmetric. Use **CO₂, N₂, C₂H₂, or benzene** instead.
> 2. **Confusing D and G bands in carbon** — D = "Disorder" (~1350 cm⁻¹), G = "Graphitic" (~1580 cm⁻¹). Mixing these up costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong direction of stress shift** — students often say "compressive = red shift." Correct: **compressive → blue shift** (peak moves to higher cm⁻¹).
> 4. **No mention of 2D band** in graphene — examiners expect mention of the 2D (or G') band around 2700 cm⁻¹ to identify monolayer graphene.
> 5. **Generic applications** — listing "chemical analysis" without specifying *which* materials and *what* information is obtained loses marks. Be **specific**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- **Raman Effect (1928, C.V. Raman, Nobel 1930):** inelastic scattering of light with frequency shift equal to a vibrational mode.
- **Three lines:** Rayleigh (at $\nu_0$), Stokes (at $\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$, strong), Anti-Stokes (at $\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$, weak).
- **Raman selection rule:** $\dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q} \neq 0$ — change in **polarizability**.
- **IR selection rule:** $\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial Q} \neq 0$ — change in **dipole moment**.
- **Mutual exclusion:** applies to centrosymmetric molecules — IR- and Raman-active modes are mutually exclusive.
- **Classical theory:** modulated polarizability $\alpha(t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_Q \cos(2\pi \nu_{vib} t)$ produces three frequencies via product-to-sum identity.
- **Quantum picture:** scattering through short-lived **virtual state** (not real eigenstate).
- **Intensity ratio Stokes/Anti-Stokes:** dominated by Boltzmann factor $\exp(-hc\tilde{\nu}_{vib}/k_BT)$ → Stokes ~10⁶× stronger at 300 K.
- **Raman intensity scaling:** $I \propto \nu_0^4 \left( \dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q} \right)^2 I_0$ — shorter wavelength laser = stronger signal.
- **Instrumentation block:** Laser → Sample → Notch filter → Monochromator/grating → CCD detector → Computer.
- **FT-Raman:** 1064 nm laser + Michelson interferometer + InGaAs detector — eliminates fluorescence.
- **Dispersive Raman:** visible laser + notch/edge filter + grating + CCD.
- **Depolarization ratio $\rho$:** $\rho < 0.75$ → polarized (totally symmetric mode); $\rho = 0.75$ → depolarized.
- **Carbon polymorphs (D and G bands):** Diamond → 1332 cm⁻¹; Graphite G → 1582 cm⁻¹; D band → 1350 cm⁻¹ (disorder); 2D band → 2700 cm⁻¹ (graphene fingerprint).
- **Stress in Si:** unstrained 520.5 cm⁻¹; compressive → **blue shift**; tensile → **red shift**.
- **Homonuclear diatomics (N₂, O₂, H₂):** Raman-active (polarizability change), IR-inactive (no dipole change).
- **Water:** very weak Raman scatterer → ideal for **aqueous and biological** samples.
- **Micro-Raman spatial resolution:** ~1 µm (confocal).
- **Resonance Raman:** when laser wavelength matches electronic transition → enhancement of 10³–10⁶× for specific chromophores.
- **SERS (Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy):** enhancement factors up to 10⁸–10¹¹ using rough metal (Au/Ag) surfaces — used in biosensing.
- **Units:** Raman shift is reported in **wavenumbers (cm⁻¹)**, not Hz or nm.
- **Range:** typically $50 - 4000 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$ for fingerprint + functional-group region.
- **Key equation to remember:** $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \tilde{\nu}_0 - \tilde{\nu}_{scat}$ — *this single line will earn partial credit even on unfamiliar questions.*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Study Tip for KTU PEPET412:** Raman spectroscopy questions almost always test (i) the **selection rule**, (ii) the **three types of scattering** with an energy diagram, and (iii) **instrumentation block diagram**. Master these three, and you can answer 80% of exam questions confidently. Always include the term **"virtual state"** in your diagrams for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET412 - MATERIAL CHARACTERIZATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS)**, also historically known as **Electron Spectroscopy for Chemical Analysis (ESCA)**, is a quantitative, surface-sensitive analytical technique based on the **photoelectric effect**. It irradiates a material with a beam of monochromatic soft X-rays (typically Al Kα at **1486.6 eV** or Mg Kα at **1253.6 eV**) and measures the kinetic energy and number of electrons escaping from the top **1–10 nm** of the sample surface. By measuring the kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons, the binding energy of the core-level electrons in the parent atoms can be calculated, yielding information about the **elemental composition, chemical state, electronic state, and empirical formula** of the analyzed region. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** XPS is a core topic in Module 4 (Spectroscopic & Advanced Techniques). Students must know the principle, instrumentation, the binding energy equation, chemical shift concept, and applications in materials engineering (polymers, metals, ceramics, catalysts, semiconductors). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a forensic scientist trying to identify a person by throwing a small ball at them and measuring how fast it bounces back. A heavier person will cause the ball to bounce back faster (more energy retained), while a lighter person will absorb more energy. Similarly, in XPS: - The **X-ray photon** = the "thrown ball" of known energy ($h\nu$) - The **core electron** = the "person" with a specific binding energy ($E_b$) - The **photoelectron's kinetic energy** = the "bounce-back speed" of the ball - The **analyzer** = the forensic "speed camera" By measuring the kinetic energy of the ejected electron, we can "weigh" how strongly the electron was bound to its parent atom. If an atom forms a chemical bond (e.g., oxidation), it pulls the electrons tighter, increasing the binding energy — this is the famous **chemical shift**, the heart of XPS analysis. > [!NOTE] > **Surface Sensitivity Rule:** XPS is *only* sensitive to the outermost atomic layers. Electrons escaping from deeper than ~10 nm lose their energy through inelastic scattering and contribute only to the background noise. This is why XPS is the gold standard for studying **surface oxidation, thin-film coatings, catalyst surfaces, and contamination layers**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Al Kα X-ray energy | **1486.6 eV** | electron-volt | | Mg Kα X-ray energy | **1253.6 eV** | electron-volt | | Planck's constant ($h$) | **4.135667696 × 10⁻¹⁵** | eV·s | | Speed of light ($c$) | **2.998 × 10⁸** | m/s | | Work function of spectrometer ($\phi_{sp}$) | **3–5** (typical) | eV | | Probe depth | **1–10** | nm | | Energy resolution | **0.1–1.0** | eV | | Sample area analyzed | **~10 μm – 1 mm** | diameter | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Photoelectric Effect — Foundational Principle The photoelectric effect, explained by **Albert Einstein in 1905** (Nobel Prize 1921), forms the bedrock of XPS. When a photon of energy $h\nu$ strikes a bound electron, the electron absorbs the entire quantum of energy. If this energy exceeds the electron's binding energy ($E_b$) plus the work needed to escape the solid (work function, $\phi$), the electron is ejected as a free **photoelectron** with measurable kinetic energy. Einstein's photoelectric equation is written as: $$h\nu = E_b + \phi + E_k$$ where: - $h\nu$ = energy of the incident X-ray photon - $E_b$ = binding energy of the core-level electron (referenced to the Fermi level) - $\phi$ = work function of the solid (energy required for the electron to escape the surface) - $E_k$ = measured kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectron Since the sample and the spectrometer are in **electrical contact**, their Fermi levels align. The binding energy is therefore referenced to the spectrometer's Fermi level, and the work function that matters is that of the **spectrometer** ($\phi_{sp}$), not the sample. Hence the practical KTU formula becomes: $$E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Universal XPS Equation:** This single equation, $E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$, is the most important formula in the entire XPS topic. It is the basis for **all** binding energy calculations in KTU exam questions. ## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Irradiation:** Monochromatic X-rays of known energy $h\nu$ strike the sample surface. Common X-ray sources: - **Al Kα** (1486.6 eV) — most widely used, narrow line width (~0.85 eV) - **Mg Kα** (1253.6 eV) — older source, useful for resolving overlapping peaks **Step 2 — Photoemission:** Core-level electrons absorb the X-ray photon and are ejected from the atom. The photoelectron's kinetic energy is measured by a hemispherical electron energy analyzer. **Step 3 — Charge referencing:** Since samples can be insulators, they tend to charge up during measurement. The energy scale is calibrated by referencing a known peak (e.g., adventitious carbon C 1s at **284.8 eV**). **Step 4 — Spectrum generation:** The number of electrons (intensity, in counts per second, cps) is plotted against binding energy. Each element has characteristic peak positions (e.g., C 1s ≈ 285 eV, O 1s ≈ 531 eV, Fe 2p₃/₂ ≈ 711 eV, Si 2p ≈ 99 eV). **Step 5 — Chemical state identification:** Small shifts in the peak position (0.1–5 eV) reveal the chemical environment — oxidation state, bonding partners, electronegativity differences. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$ | Universal XPS binding energy equation | eV | | 2 | $E_k = h\nu - E_b - \phi_{sp}$ | Kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron | eV | | 3 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | X-ray photon energy | eV | | 4 | $\lambda_{AlK\alpha} = 0.834$ nm | Al Kα X-ray wavelength | nm | | 5 | $\lambda_{MgK\alpha} = 0.989$ nm | Mg Kα X-ray wavelength | nm | | 6 | $\Delta E_b = E_b^{compound} - E_b^{element}$ | Chemical shift (positive → oxidation) | eV | | 7 | $\text{IMFP} \approx 0.41 \cdot E_k^{0.5}$ (in nm, for $E_k$ in eV) | Inelastic mean free path (Tanuma-Powell approx.) | nm | | 8 | $\text{Probe depth} \approx 3\lambda_{IMFP}$ | 95% of signal originates within this depth | nm | | 9 | $\text{Spin-orbit splitting: } \Delta E_{s-o} = E_{j=l+1/2} - E_{j=l-1/2}$ | Doublet separation for p, d, f orbitals | eV | | 10 | $\text{Branching ratio: } I_{3/2}/I_{1/2} = (2j+1)_{3/2}/(2j+1)_{1/2}$ | Intensity ratio of spin-orbit doublet | — | | 11 | $C_x = \frac{I_x / S_x}{\sum_i (I_i / S_i)}$ | Atomic concentration from peak intensities | atomic % | | 12 | $E_k^{Auger} = E_b^{core} - E_b^{Auger\;final} - E_b^{core\;ejected} - \phi_{sp}$ | Auger electron kinetic energy (independent of X-ray source) | eV | | 13 | $\phi_{sp} = h\nu - E_k^{Au\;4f_{7/2}} - E_b^{Au\;4f_{7/2}}$ | Spectrometer work function calibration (Au 4f₇/₂ = 84.0 eV) | eV | | 14 | $\lambda = h/mv$ (de Broglie) | Inelastic scattering in materials requires this lambda | nm | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** Branching ratio for p-orbitals (2p₃/₂ : 2p₁/₂) = **2 : 1**; for d-orbitals (3d₅/₂ : 3d₃/₂) = **3 : 2**; for f-orbitals = **4 : 3**. These are extremely common in KTU problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility XPS is the workhorse technique in modern materials engineering for: - **Semiconductor industry:** Verifying oxidation states in SiO₂/Si stacks, gate dielectrics, and thin-film transistors (TFTs). - **Catalysis research:** Quantifying surface coverage of active metal sites and identifying poisoning species. - **Corrosion science:** Measuring oxide layer thickness and composition on aluminum, titanium, stainless steel. - **Polymer & biomedical materials:** Detecting surface functional groups, plasma-treatment effects, biocompatibility coatings. - **Battery technology:** Studying SEI (solid-electrolyte interphase) layer chemistry in Li-ion batteries. - **Failure analysis:** Identifying contamination, delamination, and adhesion failures in multilayer packaging. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Universal XPS Equation The following step-by-step derivation connects Einstein's photoelectric equation to the practical XPS binding energy formula, which is the most frequently tested concept in KTU exams. **Starting point — Energy conservation:** When an X-ray photon strikes a core electron, the total energy must be conserved. The energy of the photon is partitioned into three sinks: 1. The energy required to free the electron from the atomic potential well → **Binding Energy $E_b$** 2. The energy required to transport the electron from the Fermi level to the vacuum level outside the solid → **Work Function $\phi$** 3. The remaining energy becomes the kinetic energy of the free electron → **$E_k$** Therefore: $$h\nu = E_b + \phi + E_k$$ **Step 1 — Rearrange to solve for binding energy:** Subtract $\phi$ and $E_k$ from both sides: $$E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi$$ **Step 2 — Clarify which work function is used:** In a real XPS experiment, the sample is mounted in electrical contact with the metallic spectrometer. Their Fermi levels equilibrate. When the photoelectron travels from the sample into the analyzer, it sees the work function of the **spectrometer** ($\phi_{sp}$), not the sample's. The binding energy is therefore referenced to the **Fermi level of the spectrometer**, not the vacuum level of the sample. $$E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$$ **Step 3 — Final form (KTU standard form):** $$\boxed{E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}}$$ This is the equation that all KTU problems will expect students to apply. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard Binding Energy Calculation **Problem (KTU-typical):** A specimen is irradiated with Al Kα X-rays ($h\nu = 1486.6$ eV). The spectrometer work function is 4.0 eV. A photoelectron is detected with a kinetic energy of 1045.6 eV. Calculate the binding energy and identify the orbital. **Step 1 — Identify the given values:** $$h\nu = 1486.6 \text{ eV}, \quad \phi_{sp} = 4.0 \text{ eV}, \quad E_k = 1045.6 \text{ eV}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the universal XPS equation:** $$E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$E_b = 1486.6 - 1045.6 - 4.0$$ **Step 4 — Compute stepwise:** $$1486.6 - 1045.6 = 441.0 \text{ eV}$$ $$441.0 - 4.0 = 437.0 \text{ eV}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the orbital:** An $E_b$ of **437.0 eV** corresponds to the **In 3d₅/₂** orbital of indium metal. (This is the well-known reference peak for XPS calibration, alongside Au 4f₇/₂ at 84.0 eV and Cu 2p₃/₂ at 932.6 eV.) $$\boxed{E_b = 437.0 \text{ eV} \;(\text{In } 3d_{5/2})}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Chemical Shift Calculation **Problem:** A sample of pure iron metal (Fe⁰) shows the Fe 2p₃/₂ peak at a binding energy of 706.7 eV. A sample of Fe₂O₃ (Fe³⁺) shows the same Fe 2p₃/₂ peak at 710.8 eV. Calculate the **chemical shift** and comment on the physical meaning. **Step 1 — Define the chemical shift:** $$\Delta E_b = E_b^{compound} - E_b^{element}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$\Delta E_b = 710.8 \text{ eV} - 706.7 \text{ eV}$$ **Step 3 — Compute:** $$\Delta E_b = +4.1 \text{ eV}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret:** The **positive shift of +4.1 eV** indicates that the iron in Fe₂O₃ is in a higher oxidation state (+3) compared to metallic Fe (0). Oxygen is more electronegative than iron, so it draws electron density away from the Fe nucleus, reducing the screening of the nuclear charge. The remaining core electrons therefore experience a higher effective nuclear charge and are more tightly bound — hence the larger $E_b$. $$\boxed{\Delta E_b = +4.1 \text{ eV (positive shift confirms oxidation)}}$$ ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Concentration Calculation Using Sensitivity Factors **Problem:** A polymer surface is analyzed by XPS using Al Kα radiation. The integrated peak intensities (after background subtraction) are: - C 1s: $I_C = 25{,}000$ cps·eV - O 1s: $I_O = 12{,}500$ cps·eV The sensitivity (Scofield) factors are: - $S_C = 1.00$ - $S_O = 2.93$ Calculate the atomic concentration ratio of O to C and the empirical formula (assuming the polymer contains only C and O). **Step 1 — Apply the concentration formula:** $$C_x = \frac{I_x / S_x}{\sum_i (I_i / S_i)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute normalized intensities:** For C: $$\frac{I_C}{S_C} = \frac{25000}{1.00} = 25000$$ For O: $$\frac{I_O}{S_O} = \frac{12500}{2.93} \approx 4266$$ **Step 3 — Compute atom fractions:** $$C_C = \frac{25000}{25000 + 4266} = \frac{25000}{29266} \approx 0.854 \;(85.4\%)$$ $$C_O = \frac{4266}{29266} \approx 0.146 \;(14.6\%)$$ **Step 4 — Empirical formula ratio:** $$\frac{O}{C} = \frac{0.146}{0.854} \approx 0.171 \approx \frac{1}{5.85}$$ This ratio (~1:6) suggests a polymer similar to oxidized polyethylene or a polyester surface with a moderate O:C ratio. $$\boxed{C_C \approx 85.4\%, \quad C_O \approx 14.6\%, \quad O/C \approx 0.17}$$ ## 3.5 Auger Parameter and Wagner Plot (Advanced — KTU Supplementary) For Auger peaks, the kinetic energy is **independent of the X-ray source**. The **Auger parameter** $\alpha$ is defined as: $$\alpha = E_k^{Auger} + E_b^{photoelectron}$$ This quantity is independent of the spectrometer work function and sample charging, making it the most reliable chemical-state fingerprint. The **Wagner plot** plots $\alpha$ on the x-axis against $E_b$ (or a related parameter) on the y-axis, allowing unambiguous identification of chemical states even in poorly conducting samples. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — XPS vs. Competing Surface Techniques | Property | XPS | AES (Auger) | SIMS | UPS | |---|---|---|---|---| | Probe | X-ray photon | Electron beam | Ion beam | UV photon | | Detected particle | Photoelectron | Auger electron | Secondary ion | Photoelectron | | Probe depth | 1–10 nm | 1–5 nm | 1–3 monolayers | <5 nm | | Detection limit | 0.1–1 at% | 0.1–1 at% | ppm–ppb | — | | Spatial resolution | ~10 μm | ~10 nm | ~50 nm | ~mm | | Chemical state info | Excellent | Moderate | Limited | Valence only | | Quantification | Good | Difficult | Matrix effects | Good | | Destructive? | No (mild) | Mildly | Yes | No | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The comparison between XPS, AES, and other surface techniques is a frequently asked 7-mark question. Memorize the probe, detected particle, and probe depth for each. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an XPS Spectrometer The following Mermaid diagram maps the modular architecture of a modern XPS instrument. Node IDs use the safe alphanumeric prefix rule (no reserved keywords). ```mermaid graph TD A[X-ray Source
Al K alpha 1486.6 eV
Mg K alpha 1253.6 eV] --> B[Monochromator
Quartz Crystal
Line width less than 0.85 eV] B --> C[Sample Chamber
Ultra High Vacuum 10 to the power of minus 9 mbar] C --> D[Hemispherical Electron Energy Analyzer
Two Concentric Hemispheres
Pass Energy 10 to 100 eV] D --> E[Electron Multiplier Detector
Channeltron or MCP] E --> F[Data Acquisition System
Pulsed Counting Mode] F --> G[Spectrum Plot
Intensity versus Binding Energy] H[Vacuum System
Ion Pump plus Ti Sublimation] --> C I[Charge Neutralizer
Flood Gun for Insulators] --> C J[Sample Stage
Five Axis Manipulator] --> C style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#ffe1e1 style D fill:#e1ffe1 style E fill:#f0e1ff style F fill:#ffe1f0 style G fill:#e1f0ff ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — XPS Analysis Workflow ```mermaid graph LR A[Sample Preparation
Mounting on Stub
Sputter Cleaning Optional] --> B[Load into UHV Chamber
Pump Down to 10 to the power of minus 9 mbar] B --> C[Irradiate with X rays
h nu equals 1486.6 eV] C --> D[Photoelectron Emission
from Core Levels] D --> E[Energy Analysis
in Hemispherical Analyzer] E --> F[Detection
Counting Photoelectrons] F --> G[Spectrum Generation
Intensity versus Kinetic Energy] G --> H[Convert to Binding Energy
E b equals h nu minus E k minus phi sp] H --> I[Peak Identification
Elemental and Chemical State] I --> J[Quantification
Atomic Percent via Sensitivity Factors] J --> K[Report Results
Survey plus High Resolution Spectra] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Energy Level Diagram for XPS ```mermaid graph TD A[Vacuum Level
E equals 0 reference] --> B[Fermi Level E F
Aligned between sample and spectrometer] C[Core Level E b
Bound Electron State] --> B B --> D[Photoelectron Kinetic Energy E k
Measured by Analyzer] E[Incident X ray Photon h nu equals 1486.6 eV] --> D C --> E F[Work Function phi sp
4 eV typical] --> A B --> F style A fill:#ffe1e1 style C fill:#e1f5ff style D fill:#e1ffe1 style B fill:#fff4e1 style E fill:#f0e1ff style F fill:#ffe1f0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Energy Diagram:** The X-ray photon (hν) provides all the energy. Some of it goes to overcome the binding energy (E_b) holding the electron in the core level. Some goes to overcome the spectrometer work function (ϕ_sp). The remainder is the measured kinetic energy (E_k). This is the physical basis of $E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$. ## 4.4 Architecture — Spin-Orbit Doublet Structure ```mermaid graph TD A[Core Orbital with Orbital Quantum Number l] --> B[l equals 0 s Orbital
Singlet Peak Only] A --> C[l equals 1 p Orbital
Doublet p3/2 and p1/2
Area Ratio 2 to 1] A --> D[l equals 2 d Orbital
Doublet d5/2 and d3/2
Area Ratio 3 to 2] A --> E[l equals 3 f Orbital
Doublet f7/2 and f5/2
Area Ratio 4 to 3] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#e1ffe1 style C fill:#fff4e1 style D fill:#ffe1e1 style E fill:#f0e1ff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define the term "chemical shift" in XPS and explain its physical origin.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **chemical shift** is the small change (typically 0.1 to 5 eV) in the measured binding energy of a core-level photoelectron caused by the chemical environment of the emitting atom. When an atom forms a bond with a more electronegative partner, electron density is drawn away from the atom, reducing the screening of the nucleus. The core electrons experience a higher effective nuclear charge and are bound more tightly, producing a higher measured $E_b$ — a **positive chemical shift** that signals oxidation, increased formal charge, or bonding to electronegative species. Conversely, bonding to electropositive species or electron-donating groups produces a **negative shift**. The chemical shift is the foundation for using XPS as a chemical-state analytical tool. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Why is XPS considered a "surface-sensitive" technique, and what is the typical probe depth?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** XPS is surface-sensitive because it detects **photoelectrons** escaping from the sample. These electrons have a very short **inelastic mean free path (IMFP, $\lambda$)** in solids — typically 1 to 3 nm for the kinetic energies of interest (Al Kα source). Electrons originating from deeper than ~3λ lose their energy through inelastic scattering with other electrons and phonons, and contribute only to the spectral background rather than to sharp peaks. The effective probe depth is therefore approximately **3λ ≈ 3 to 10 nm**, corresponding to roughly **3 to 10 atomic monolayers**. This makes XPS ideal for studying surface oxidation, contamination, and thin-film chemistry, but unsuitable for bulk analysis. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024]` #### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the main components of an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Explain the function of each component. **(7 marks)** **(b)** State and derive the fundamental equation used in XPS for calculating the binding energy of a core-level electron. A sample is irradiated with Mg Kα X-rays (hν = 1253.6 eV). The spectrometer work function is 3.5 eV, and a photoelectron is detected with a kinetic energy of 320.0 eV. Calculate the binding energy and identify the most likely orbital. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram and Components of XPS (7 marks):** A modern XPS instrument consists of the following modules: 1. **X-ray Source (1 mark):** Provides monochromatic soft X-rays. The two standard laboratory sources are **Al Kα (1486.6 eV)** and **Mg Kα (1253.6 eV)**. A heated tungsten filament emits electrons that are accelerated onto an Al or Mg anode, producing characteristic X-rays. The line width is ~0.85 eV for Al Kα and ~0.7 eV for Mg Kα. 2. **Monochromator (0.5 mark):** A bent quartz crystal focuses and narrows the X-ray line to ~0.3 eV, removing satellite lines and Bremsstrahlung background. This improves energy resolution and signal-to-noise ratio. 3. **Ultra-High Vacuum (UHV) Chamber (1 mark):** Maintains pressure at **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹⁰ mbar**. UHV is essential because (i) photoelectrons have a short mean free path and would scatter in air, and (ii) a clean surface must be maintained for the duration of measurement. The chamber typically includes ion pumps, turbomolecular pumps, and titanium sublimation pumps. 4. **Sample Stage and Manipulator (0.5 mark):** A 5-axis manipulator allows precise positioning, heating, cooling, and sometimes in-situ treatment (sputtering, gas dosing) of the sample. A charge neutralizer (flood gun) may be used for insulating samples. 5. **Electron Energy Analyzer (1.5 marks):** A **hemispherical sector analyzer (HSA)** — two concentric hemispheres with a voltage difference — acts as an energy filter. Only electrons with a specific **pass energy** (10 to 100 eV) reach the detector, providing energy resolution down to ~0.1 eV. A multichannel detection scheme allows simultaneous acquisition over a range of energies. 6. **Detector (0.5 mark):** A **channel electron multiplier (CEM)** or **microchannel plate (MCP)** amplifies the signal. Output is in the form of pulses counted per second (cps). 7. **Data Acquisition System (0.5 mark):** A computer with XPS software plots **counts per second (intensity) versus binding energy**. Two main scan modes are used: **survey scan** (wide energy range, low resolution) and **high-resolution scan** (narrow range around a specific peak). 8. **Sample Preparation / Loading Chamber (0.5 mark):** A separate vacuum lock allows sample introduction without breaking UHV in the main chamber. Sputter ion guns (Ar⁺) can clean the surface in situ. **Block Diagram:** ``` X-ray Source → Monochromator → Sample (in UHV) ↓ Detector ← Analyzer ← Photoelectrons ↓ Data Acquisition System ``` **(b) Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):** **Statement of the equation (1 mark):** The fundamental XPS equation relates the binding energy $E_b$ of a core-level electron to the incident X-ray photon energy $h\nu$, the measured kinetic energy $E_k$ of the ejected photoelectron, and the spectrometer work function $\phi_{sp}$: $$E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$$ **Derivation (3 marks):** The derivation follows from the conservation of energy during the photoemission process. An X-ray photon of energy $h\nu$ strikes a bound electron. The electron absorbs the entire photon energy. To leave the atom and the solid, the electron must overcome two energy barriers: the binding energy $E_b$ (which holds it in the core level, referenced to the Fermi level) and the work function $\phi$ (which represents the energy needed to transport the electron from the Fermi level to the vacuum level outside the solid). The remaining energy appears as the kinetic energy $E_k$ of the free photoelectron. By conservation of energy: $$h\nu = E_b + \phi + E_k$$ Because the sample and spectrometer are in electrical contact, their Fermi levels align. The work function that enters the equation is therefore that of the spectrometer, $\phi_{sp}$. Rearranging for $E_b$: $$E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$$ **Calculation (3 marks):** Given: $$h\nu = 1253.6 \text{ eV}, \quad \phi_{sp} = 3.5 \text{ eV}, \quad E_k = 320.0 \text{ eV}$$ Apply the equation: $$E_b = 1253.6 - 320.0 - 3.5$$ Step 1: $1253.6 - 320.0 = 933.6$ eV Step 2: $933.6 - 3.5 = 930.1$ eV $$\boxed{E_b = 930.1 \text{ eV}}$$ **Identification of the orbital:** A binding energy of **930.1 eV** corresponds most closely to the **Cu 2p₃/₂** core level of copper (standard reference value ≈ 932.6 eV for Cu⁰; the small discrepancy is attributed to charging or chemical shift). The Cu 2p₃/₂ peak is one of the most frequently used XPS reference lines for calibration. --- #### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** What is the "inelastic mean free path" (IMFP) of a photoelectron? Explain its role in determining the surface sensitivity of XPS. Using the empirical relation $\lambda \approx 0.41 \cdot E_k^{0.5}$ (in nm, with $E_k$ in eV), estimate the IMFP of a C 1s photoelectron excited by Al Kα X-rays ($h\nu = 1486.6$ eV), given that $E_b \approx 285$ eV and $\phi_{sp} \approx 4$ eV. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **spin-orbit splitting** in XPS spectra. Why does a 2p orbital appear as a doublet, while a 1s orbital appears as a single peak? Compute the expected intensity ratio of the two components of the 3d doublet. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) IMFP and Surface Sensitivity (7 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Inelastic Mean Free Path (IMFP, $\lambda$)** is defined as the average distance a photoelectron can travel through a solid before losing energy through an inelastic collision (e.g., plasmon excitation, interband transitions, phonon scattering). IMFP is a strong function of the photoelectron's kinetic energy, typically 1 to 5 nm in the energy range of interest for XPS (50 to 1500 eV). **Role in surface sensitivity (1.5 marks):** Because photoelectrons escaping from deeper than a few multiples of $\lambda$ lose their characteristic energy, only electrons originating from within approximately **3λ** of the surface (which contains ~95% of the signal) contribute to the XPS peaks. Hence, XPS is sensitive to the outermost **1 to 10 nm** of a sample. This is why XPS is called a **surface analysis** technique rather than a bulk technique. **Calculation (4 marks):** Step 1: Compute the kinetic energy: $$E_k = h\nu - E_b - \phi_{sp} = 1486.6 - 285 - 4 = 1197.6 \text{ eV}$$ Step 2: Apply the empirical relation: $$\lambda = 0.41 \cdot (E_k)^{0.5}$$ Step 3: Compute the square root: $$\sqrt{1197.6} \approx 34.6$$ Step 4: Multiply by 0.41: $$\lambda = 0.41 \cdot 34.6 \approx 14.2 \text{ nm}$$ Step 5: Probe depth: $$d \approx 3\lambda \approx 42.6 \text{ nm}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda \approx 14.2 \text{ nm}, \quad d \approx 43 \text{ nm}}$$ **Interpretation:** Although the C 1s photoelectron has a relatively large IMFP due to its high kinetic energy, the signal still originates essentially from the top 40–50 nm, confirming the surface-selective nature of XPS. (Note: lower $E_b$ orbitals such as valence levels would have lower $E_k$ and hence a shorter IMFP, providing even more surface sensitivity.) **(b) Spin-Orbit Splitting (7 marks):** **Concept (3 marks):** A core electron has both **orbital angular momentum** (quantum number $l$) and **spin angular momentum** (quantum number $s = 1/2$). The two momenta **couple** through the **spin-orbit interaction**, producing a total angular momentum $j = l + s$ or $j = l - s$. - For **s orbitals** ($l = 0$): only $j = 1/2$ is possible → **singlet peak**. - For **p orbitals** ($l = 1$): $j = 3/2$ or $j = 1/2$ → **doublet** (e.g., 2p₃/₂ and 2p₁/₂). - For **d orbitals** ($l = 2$): $j = 5/2$ or $j = 3/2$ → **doublet** (e.g., 3d₅/₂ and 3d₃/₂). - For **f orbitals** ($l = 3$): $j = 7/2$ or $j = 5/2$ → **doublet** (e.g., 4f₇/₂ and 4f₅/₂). The two components of a doublet are separated by a small, element-specific energy — the **spin-orbit splitting** $\Delta E_{s-o}$. For example, for Au 4f, $\Delta E_{s-o} = 3.7$ eV; for Si 2p, $\Delta E_{s-o} = 0.6$ eV; for Mg 2p, $\Delta E_{s-o} = 0.28$ eV. **Why s orbitals show no splitting (1 mark):** For an s electron ($l = 0$), there is no orbital angular momentum to couple with spin; hence the spin-orbit interaction has no effect and only a single peak appears. **Intensity ratio of 3d doublet (3 marks):** The intensity of each spin-orbit component is proportional to its **degeneracy** $(2j+1)$. For 3d: $j = 5/2$ has $(2j+1) = 6$ states; $j = 3/2$ has $(2j+1) = 4$ states. Therefore: $$\frac{I_{3d_{5/2}}}{I_{3d_{3/2}}} = \frac{6}{4} = \frac{3}{2}$$ $$\boxed{I_{3d_{5/2}} : I_{3d_{3/2}} = 3 : 2}$$ Similarly, for p orbitals: $I_{2p_{3/2}} : I_{2p_{1/2}} = 4:2 = 2:1$; for f orbitals: $I_{4f_{7/2}} : I_{4f_{5/2}} = 8:6 = 4:3$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the work function:** Many students write $E_b = h\nu - E_k$ and lose 1 mark. Always include $\phi_{sp}$. > 2. **Wrong spin-orbit direction:** The **higher-j** component has the **lower binding energy** (e.g., 2p₃/₂ is at lower $E_b$ than 2p₁/₂). > 3. **Confusing XPS with AES:** XPS uses X-rays to detect photoelectrons; AES uses electrons to detect Auger electrons. The kinetic energy of Auger electrons is **independent** of the X-ray source, while photoelectron kinetic energy **depends** on the source — a key distinguishing feature. > 4. **Mixing up IMFP and probe depth:** IMFP is a single number; probe depth ≈ 3λ. Do not interchange them. > 5. **Incorrect branching ratio:** A common mistake is to assume equal intensities for the two spin-orbit components. They are unequal in the ratio $(2j_1+1):(2j_2+1)$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **XPS = X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy**, a surface-sensitive quantitative technique based on the **photoelectric effect**. - **Universal XPS equation:** $E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi_{sp}$ — memorize this in all three forms. - **Standard X-ray sources:** **Al Kα (1486.6 eV)** — most common; **Mg Kα (1253.6 eV)** — secondary. - **Binding energy reference:** All $E_b$ values are referenced to the **Fermi level**, with **Au 4f₇/₂ = 84.0 eV**, **Cu 2p₃/₂ = 932.6 eV**, and **C 1s (adventitious) = 284.8 eV** as calibration standards. - **Probe depth:** ~3λ ≈ **1 to 10 nm** — XPS probes the outermost atomic layers only. - **Chemical shift (ΔE_b):** A positive shift (0.1 to 5 eV) indicates oxidation or bonding to a more electronegative species; a negative shift indicates reduction or bonding to an electropositive species. - **Spin-orbit splitting:** p, d, f orbitals each split into doublets; s orbitals do not split. Intensity ratios: **2:1 (p), 3:2 (d), 4:3 (f)**. - **Auger vs photoelectrons:** Auger kinetic energy is **independent** of $h\nu$; photoelectron kinetic energy **depends** on $h\nu$. This is the basis for the **Auger parameter** and **Wagner plots**. - **Quantification:** Atomic concentration $C_x = (I_x/S_x)/\sum_i(I_i/S_i)$, where $S_x$ is the Scofield sensitivity factor. - **UHV is mandatory:** Pressure must be ≤ 10⁻⁹ mbar; otherwise surface contamination and electron scattering destroy the signal. - **Imaging and depth profiling:** Modern XPS can do **small-area analysis** (~10 μm), **depth profiling** via Ar⁺ sputtering, and **angle-resolved XPS (ARXPS)** for non-destructive depth analysis. - **Valuable tips for the exam:** - Always show all three terms ($h\nu$, $E_k$, $\phi_{sp}$) in the equation. - State units in every step. - For chemical shift problems, **always interpret the sign** (positive = oxidation). - For doublet questions, **state the j values and degeneracy** explicitly. - Use **ASTM E2735-15** or **ISO 19318:2004** nomenclature for peak labeling (e.g., "C 1s", not "C 1s₁/₂").
